questions.question: If A and B be any two sets, then (A $\cup$ B)' is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A and B be any two sets, then (A {tex}\cup{/tex} B)' is equal to
questions.explanation:
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B)'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ (A $\cup$ B)
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ A or x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' and x $\in$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' $\cap$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cup$ B)' $\subseteq$ A' $\cap$ B'
Similarly, we can show (A' $\cap$ B') $\subseteq$ (A $\cup$ B)'
So (A $\cup$ B)' = (A' $\cap$ B')

x $\in$ A' $\cup$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' or x $\in$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A or x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A $\cap$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ (A $\cap$ B)
Hence (A $\cap$ B)' = A' $\cup$ B'

questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is a null set?

  1. {x : x2 + 1 = 0, x {tex}\in{/tex} R}
  2. {x : x > 0 or x < 0}
  3. {x : x2 = 4 or x = 3}
  4. {0}
questions.explanation:
x^2 + 1 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 = -1
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 = $\sqrt{-1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = i $\in$ C
[i = imaginary root of unity which is a complex no.]
Since the solution of x does not belong to real no hence the set is null set.

questions.question: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A – (B $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ C) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A – (B {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} C) is equal to
questions.explanation:
A - (B $\cap$ C) = (A - B) $\cup$ (A - C)
Proof:
Use following properties:
X - Y = X $\cap$ Y'
(X $\cap$ Y)' = X' $\cup$ Y' 
A - (B $\cap$ C) = A $\cap$ (B $\cap$ C)'
= A $\cap$ (B' $\cup$ C') = (A $\cap$ B') $\cup$ (A $\cap$ C') = (A - B) $\cup$ (A - C)

questions.question: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ (B $\mathop \cup \nolimits $ C) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} (B {tex}\mathop \cup \nolimits {/tex} C) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let x $\in$ A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C)
x $\in$ A or x $\in$ (B and C)
x $\in$ A or {x $\in$ B and x $\in$ C}
{x $\in$ (A or B) and x $\in$ (A or C)
x $\in$ (A or B) $\cap$ x $\in$ (A or C)
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ x $\in$ (A $\cap$ C)
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C)
Therefore, A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) $\subset$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C) .....(i)
Similarly,
(A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C) $\subset$ A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) .....(ii)
So
A $\cup$ (B $\cup$ C) = (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C)

questions.question: P(A) = P(B) $ \Rightarrow $
questions.question_raw: P(A) = P(B) {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}
questions.explanation:
To prove A = B it is enough to prove B $\subseteq$ A and A $\subseteq$ B
So let P(A) = P(B)
Also let x $\in$ A
Now we have A $\in$ P(A)
$\Rightarrow$ A $\in$ P(B), since P(A) = P(B)
$\therefore$ x $\in$ E for some E $\in$ P(B)
Now E $\subset$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ B
Hence we proved A $\subseteq$ B
Similarly by taking x $\in$ B and showing x $\in$ A we get B $\subseteq$ A
Hence A = B

Let x $\in$ A - B $\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B

Now let some y $\in$ B^c - A^c

$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ B and x $\in$ A
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A - B
$\Rightarrow$ B^c - A^c $\subset$ A - B ......(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get A - B = B^c - A^c

questions.question: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The total number of subsets of the first set is 56 more than the total number of subsets of the second set. The values of m and n are
questions.question_raw: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The total number of subsets of the first set is 56 more than the total number of subsets of the second set. The values of m and n are
questions.explanation:
Let A and B be two sets having m and n elements respectively. Then,
Number of subsets of A = 2^m, Number of subsets of B = 2^n
It is given that 2^m - 2^n = 56
So, 2^n(2^m-n - 1) = 2^3(2^3 - 1)
n = 3 and m - n = 3 $ \Rightarrow $ n = 3 and m = 6.

questions.question: In a class of 100 students, 55 students have passed in Mathematics and 67 students have passed in Physics. Then the number of students who have passed in Physics only is
questions.question_raw: In a class of 100 students, 55 students have passed in Mathematics and 67 students have passed in Physics. Then the number of students who have passed in Physics only is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of students of the class and let M and P denote the sets of students who passed in mathematics and physics respectively. Then
n(U) = 100, n(M) = 55 and n(P) = 67
Since all the students have passed in any of these subjects, we have 
n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cup$ P) = 100
Now we have, n(M $\cup$ P) = n(M) + n(P) - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ 100 = 55 + 67 - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cap$ P) = 122 - 100 = 22
Which means the number of students who passed in both the subjects = 22
Hence the number of students who passed only in physics = n(P) - n(M $\cap$ P) = 67 - 22 = 45

questions.question: Out of 800 boys in a school, 224 played cricket, 240 played hockey and 336 played basketball. Of the total, 64 played both basketball and hockey, 80 played cricket and basketball and 40 played cricket and hockey, 24 played all the three games. The number of boys who did not play any game is:
questions.question_raw: Out of 800 boys in a school, 224 played cricket, 240 played hockey and 336 played basketball. Of the total, 64 played both basketball and hockey, 80 played cricket and basketball and 40 played cricket and hockey, 24 played all the three games. The number of boys who did not play any game is:
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of boys in a school and let C, H and B denote the sets of boys who played Cricked, Hockey and Basketball respectively.
Then we have n(U) = 800, n(C) = 224, n(H) = 240 and n(B) = 336
Also $n(C \cap H)=40 \quad, n(B \cap H)=64,$ $n(C \cap B)=80$ and $n(C \cap B \cap H)=24$
Now we have $n(C \cup H \cup B)=n(C)+n(H)+n(B)$$-n(C \cap H)-n(B \cap H)-n(C \cap B)+n(C \cap B \cap H)$
$\Rightarrow n(C \cup H \cup B)=$ 224 + 240 + 336 - 40 - 64 - 80 + 24
$\Rightarrow n(C \cup H \cup B)=640$
Which means the number of boys who play any one game = 640
Hence the number of boys who Did not play any game $=n(U)-n(C \cup H \cup B)=800-640=160$

questions.question: A survey shows that 75% of the Indians like apples, whereas 68% like oranges. What percentage of Indians like both apples and oranges.
questions.question_raw: A survey shows that 75% of the Indians like apples, whereas 68% like oranges. What percentage of Indians like both apples and oranges.
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of Indians and let A and B denote the sets of Indians who like apples and oranges respectively. Then
n(U) = 100, n(A) = 75 and n(B) = 68
We have n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow$ n(A $\cup$ B) = 100
So n(A $\cup$ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A $\cap$ B)
$\Rightarrow$ 100 = 75 + 68 - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cap$ P) = 143 - 100 = 43
Which means 43 percentage of Indians like apples and oranges.

questions.question: Which of the following is (A – B) $\mathop \cup \nolimits $ (A – C)?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is (A – B) {tex}\mathop \cup \nolimits {/tex} (A – C)?
questions.explanation:
Let $x\in A-(B\cap C)\Rightarrow x\in A\quad $and x $\notin$ B $\cap$ C
$\Rightarrow x{ \in A }$ and ($x\notin B$ or $x\notin { A }$)
$\Rightarrow$ (x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B) or (x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ C)
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A − B or x $\in$ A − C
$\Rightarrow$ x$\in$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A−C)
$\Rightarrow$ A − (B $\cap$ C) $\subset$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A-C) ......(i)
Now let y ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A−C)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A−B or y $\in$ A − C
$\Rightarrow$ (y $\in$ A and y ∉ B) or (y $\in$ A and y$\notin$ C)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A and (y$\notin$ B or y$\notin$ A)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A and y $\notin$ B $\cap$ C
$\Rightarrow$ y ∈ A − (B $\cap$ C) 
$\Rightarrow$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A − C) $\subset$ A − (B $\cap$ C)......(ii)
From (i) and (ii) we get A − (B $\cap$ C) = (A−B) $\cup$ (A − C)

questions.question: A survey shows that 63% of Americans like cheese whereas 76% like apples. If x% of the Americans like both cheese and apples, then the value of x is
questions.question_raw: A survey shows that 63% of Americans like cheese whereas 76% like apples. If x% of the Americans like both cheese and apples, then the value of x is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of Americans and Lete A and B denote the sets of Americans who like cheese and apples respectively. Then
$n(U)=100$, n(A) = 62 and n(B) = 76
We have n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow n(A \cup B)=100$
So $n(A \cup B)=n(A)+n(B)-n(A \cap B)$
$\Rightarrow 100=63+76-n(A \cap B)$
$\Rightarrow n(A \cap B)=139-100=39$
Which means 39 percent of Americans like Cheese and apples.

questions.question: In a town of 10,000 families it was found that 40% families buys newspaper A, 20% buy newspaper B, and 10% families buy newspaper C, 5% families buy A and B, 3% buy B and C and 4% buy A and C. If 2% buy all the three newspapers, then number of families which buy A only is
questions.question_raw: In a town of 10,000 families it was found that 40% families buys newspaper A, 20% buy newspaper B, and 10% families buy newspaper C, 5% families buy A and B, 3% buy B and C and 4% buy A and C. If 2% buy all the three newspapers, then number of families which buy A only is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of families who buy news paper and let X, Y and Z denote the sets of families who buy newspaper A, B and C respectively
Then we have n(U) = 10000, n(X) = 40% of 10000 = 4000, n(Y) = 20% of 10000 = 2000 and n(Z) = 10% of 10000 = 1000
Also n(X $\cap$ Y) = 5% of 10000 = 500, n(Y $\cap$ Z) = 3% of 10000 = 300
n(X $\cap$ Z) = 4% of 10000 = 400 and (X $\cap$ Y $\cap$ Z) = 2% of 10000 = 200
Hence the number of families who buy only newspaper A = n[X - (Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - n[X $\cap$(Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - n[X $\cap$(Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - [n(X $\cap$ Y) + n(X $\cap$ Z) - n((X $\cap$ Y) $\cap$(X $\cap$ Z))]
= n(X) - [n(X $\cap$ Y) + n(X $\cap$ Z) - n(X $\cap$ Y $\cap$ Z)]
= 4000 - [500 + 400 - 200]
= 4000 - 700 = 3300

questions.question: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85% a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.question_raw: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85%  a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.explanation:
Given that percentage of combatants who lost one eye = 70%, an ear = 80% and an arm = 75% and a leg = 85%
Hence we get the percentage of combatants who lost one eye and one ear = (70% + 80%) - 100% = 50%
Percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear and one arm = (50% + 75%) - 100% = 25%
Similarly, the percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear, one arm, and one leg = (25% + 85%) - 100% = 10%
Hence we get the percentage of the combatants who lost all 4 limbs = 10%
Hence if x% represents the percentage of combatants who lost all 4 limbs we get the minimum value of x = 10

questions.question: If A = {(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 = 25} and B = {(x, y) : x^2 + 9y^2 = 144} then A $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ B contains
questions.question_raw: If A = {(x, y) : x2 + y2 = 25} and B = {(x, y) : x2 + 9y2 = 144} then A {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} B contains
questions.explanation:
From A, x^2 + y^2 = 25 and from B, x^2 + 9y^2 = 144
$\therefore$ From B, (x^2 + y^2) + 8y^2 = 144
$ \Rightarrow$ 25 + 8y^2 = 144
$ \Rightarrow$ 8y^2 = 119 
$\Rightarrow$y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{119}{8}}$ 
$\therefore$ x^2 + y^2 = 25 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 = 25 - y^2 = 25 - $\frac{119}{8}$ = $\frac{81}{8}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{81}{8}}$ 
Since we solved equations simultaneously, therefore ${{\text{A}} \cap \;{\text{B}}}$ has four points A has 2 elements & B has 2 elements.

$\Rightarrow$ y(x^2 + x - 6) = x^2 - 3x + 2
$\Rightarrow$ (y - 1)x^2 + (y + 3)x - 6y - 2 = 0
x = $\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}$

$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{-(y+3) \pm \sqrt{25 y^{2}-10 y+1}}{2(y-1)}$
Now for x to be defined, y - 1 $\ne$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ y $\ne$ 1
Also, 
25y^2 - 10y + 1 $\ge$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ (5y - 1)^2 $\ge$ 0
But domain of f(x): x $\ne$ 2, -3
But, 
When, y = $\frac{1}{5}$, x = 2

We know that if A and B are two sets having m and n elements respectively such that ${m \leqslant n,}$ then total number of injective from A to B is ${^n{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}} \times {\text{m}}!}$

questions.question: The range of the function f(x) = $\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1}$ is
questions.question_raw: The range of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let y = $ \;\frac{{{{\text{x}}^2} - {\text{x}} + 1}}{{{{\text{x}}^2} + {\text{x}} + 1}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ y (x^2 + x + 1) = x^2 - x + 1 
$\Rightarrow $ (y - 1)x^2 + (y + 1)x + y - 1 = 0
For x to be real, we must have
(y + 1)^2 - 4(y - 1)^2 $\ge$ 0 (Discriminant = b^2 - 4ac) 
$\Rightarrow$ -3y^2 + 10y - 3 $\ge$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ 3y^2 - 10y + 3 $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ (3y - 1)(y - 3) $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{3} \leq y \leq 3$ $\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ $\left[\frac{1}{3}, 3\right]$ 
$\therefore$ Range ${ = \left[ {\frac{1}{3},\;3} \right]}$

questions.question: On the set Z of all integers define f : Z $ \to $ Z as follows : f(x) = $\frac{x}{2}$ if x is even, and f(x) = 0 if x is odd, then f is

we have, f : z $\rightarrow$ z defined by,
${\left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\frac{x}{2},\;if\;x\;is\;even} \\ {0,\;if\;x\;is\;odd} \end{array}} \right.}$ 
One - One: - Here f(1) = 0, f(3) = 0
$\therefore$ 1 $\ne$ 3 $\Rightarrow$ f(1) = f(3)
f is one - one.
Onto: Let ${y \in z}$ be arbitrary
If y is even then there exists an even number ${2y \in z}$ such that
f(2y) = $ \frac{{2y}}{2} $ = y
If y is odd then there exists an even number ${2y \in z}$ such that
f(2y) = $\frac{{2y}}{2}$ = y 
f is onto.

questions.question_raw: If {tex}A = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\} {/tex} and {tex}B = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = 144\} ,{/tex} then {tex}A \cap B{/tex} contains
questions.explanation:
We will solve equations in A and B simultaneously and find values of x and y. The no. of possible ordered pairs from these values will be elements in ${A \cap B.}$
Now, From B,${{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = \;144}$ and 
From ${A,\;{x^2} + {y^2}\; = \;25}$
${\therefore 9{y^2} + 25\; = \;144\; \Rightarrow 9{y^2}\; = 119}$
${ \Rightarrow y\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{119}}{9}} }$
${\therefore {x^2} + {y^2} = 25\; \Rightarrow {x^2}\; = 25 = \frac{{119}}{9}\; = \;\frac{{106}}{9}}$
${ \Rightarrow x\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{106}}{9}} }$
$\therefore$ x has two value, y has two values
$\therefore$ possible ordered pairs = 4
$\therefore$ ${A \cap B}$ has 4 elements

$\Rightarrow$ f(y) = $\frac{y}{y-1}=\frac{\frac{x-1}{z}}{\frac{x-1}{x}-1}=\frac{x-1}{x-1-x}$ = $\frac{x-1}{-1}$ = 1 - x

questions.question: If f (x + y + z) = f (x) f (y ) f (z) for all x , y z and if f (2) = 4, f’ (0) = 5 and f (0) $ \ne $0, then f’ (2) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f (x + y + z) = f (x) f (y ) f (z) for all x , y z and if f (2) = 4, f’ (0) = 5 and f (0) {tex} \ne {/tex}0, then f’ (2) is equal to
questions.explanation:
We know that if ${{\text{f}}({\text{x}} + {\text{y}} + {\text{z}})\; = \;{\text{f}}({\text{x}}){\text{f}}({\text{y}}){\text{f}}({\text{z}}),}$ then
${{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{\text{a}}^{{\text{kx}}}}}$, a and k being constants
Now, ${{\text{f}}(2)\; = \;{{\text{a}}^{2{\text{k}}}}\; \Rightarrow \;{{({{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}})}^2}\; = {2^2}\; \Rightarrow \;{{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}}\; = \pm 2}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{({{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}})}^{\text{x}}}\; = \;{{( \pm \;2)}^{\text{x}}}}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f'}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{(2)}^{\text{x}}}{{\log }_{\text{e}}}2}$ (as negative log is not defined)
${ \Rightarrow \;{\text{f'}}(0)\; = \;({{\log }_{\text{e}}}2)\; \Rightarrow \;5\; = \;{{\log }_{\text{e}}}2}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f'}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{(2)}^{\text{x}}}.5\; \Rightarrow {\text{f'}}(2)\; = {{(2)}^2}\;.5\; = \;20}$

questions.question: The domain of the function $\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = $ \frac{1}{{\left[ x \right]}} + \sqrt {2x - {x^2}}$ 
For f(x) to be real, x $\in$ [0, 1] and 2x - x^2 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x^2 - 2x = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x(x - 2) = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x = 0, 2
$\therefore$ Domain = [1, 2]

questions.question: If A = {(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 = 5} and B = {(x, y) : 2x = 5y}, then A $\cap$ B contains

From A, x^2 + y^2 = 5 and from B, 2x = 5y
Now, 2x = 5y $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{5}{2 y}$ 
$\therefore$ x^2 + y^2 = 5 $\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{5}{2 y}\right)^{2}$ + y^2 = 5
$\Rightarrow$ 29y^2 = 20 $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 29y^2 = 20 $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}$ 
$\therefore$ x = $\frac{5}{2}(\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}})$ 
$\therefore$ Possible ordered pairs = four
But two ordered pair in which c is positive and y is negative will be rejected as it will not be satisifed by the equation in B. Therefore,
A $ \cap$ B contains 2 elements.

questions.question: If f : N $ \times $ N $ \to $N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: If f : N {tex} \times {/tex} N {tex} \to {/tex}N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?

options.label: A
options.text: f is one-one but not onto

options.label: C
options.text: f is neither one-one nor onto

options.label: D
options.text: f is onto but not one-one

correct_option_texts: f is neither one-one nor onto
questions.explanation:
One - One: f(1, 2) = 1 + 2 = 3; f(2, 1) = 2 + 1
$\therefore$ f is not one - one
onto: f(1, 1) = 2, f(1, 2) = 3, f(2, 1) = 3
f(1, 3) = 4 = f(3, 1) and so on
$\therefore$ range of f = {2, 3, 4, 5, ....} $\ne$ N
$\therefore$ f is not onto.

questions.question: The function f(x) = ${10^x}$ from R to [0, $\infty $) is
questions.question_raw: The function f(x) = {tex}{10^x}{/tex} from R to [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
We have, ${f:R \to \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}$ defined by,
${f(x) = \;{{10}^x}}$
one - one: ${f({x_1})\; = \;f({x_2})}$
${ \Rightarrow \;{{10}^{{x_1}}}\; = {{10}^{{x_2}}}\; \Rightarrow \;{x_1}\; = \;{x_2}}$
$\therefore$ f is one - one
onto: Here, ${0 \in \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}$ (Co - domain)
Now, ${f(x)\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;{{10}^x}\; = \;0}$
But this is not possible for ${x \in R}$
$\therefore$ 0 does not has pre - image
$\therefore$ f is not onto

questions.question: For all x $ \in $ (0, 1)
questions.question_raw: For all x {tex} \in {/tex} (0, 1)
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = x - log(1 + x) in [0, x]; x $ \in$ (0, 1) clearly, f is continuous on [0, x] and differentiable on (0, x).
Therefore by Lagrange's mean value theorem, there exists c $ \in$ (0, x) such that,
f'(c) = $ \;\frac{{{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; - \;{\text{f}}(0)}}{{{\text{x}} - 0}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 - $\frac{1}{c}$ = $\frac{x-\log (1+x)-0}{x}$ {$\because$ f'(x) = 1 - $\frac{1}{1+x}$}
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}$ = 1 - $\frac{1}{1+c}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}$ > 0 [$\because$ c $\in$ (0, 1) $\Rightarrow$ 1 - $\frac{1}{1+c}$ > 0]
$\Rightarrow$ x - log (1 + x) > 0 [$\because$ x $\in$ (0, 1)]
$\Rightarrow$ log (1 + x) < x

questions.question: If f : [1, $\infty \infty $) $ \to $ [2, $\infty \infty $) is given by f(x) = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ then f^-1(x) equals]
questions.question_raw: If f : [1, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) {tex} \to {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) is given by f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} then f-1(x) equals]
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 1 = xy $\Rightarrow$ x^2 - xy + 1 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ (negative sign is rejected)
f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}$

questions.question: The minimum value of (x - $\alpha $) (x – $\beta $) is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of (x - {tex}\alpha {/tex}) (x – {tex}\beta {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = (x - $\alpha$)(x - $\beta$) then, 
f'(x) = $(x - \alpha ).1\; + 1.\;(x\; - \beta )$ 
$ \Rightarrow$ f'(x) = 2x - ($\alpha$ + $\beta$) 
$\Rightarrow$ f''(x) = 2
Now, f''(x) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 2x - $(\alpha+\beta)$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}$ 
At x = $\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}$, f''(x) = 2 > 0
$\therefore$ x = $ \frac{{\alpha \; + \beta }}{2}$ is point of minimum value and minimum value is,
$f\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)$ = $\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\alpha\right)\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\beta\right)$ 
= $\left(\frac{\beta-\alpha}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$ = $-\frac{1}{4}(\alpha-\beta)^{2}$

= $\left\{25-\left(25-x^{4}\right)\right\}^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = $\left(x^{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = x 
$\therefore$ $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ = $\frac{1}{2}$
= 2^-1

questions.question: Let the function f be defined by f(x) = $\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}$, then f^-1(x) is
questions.question_raw: Let the function f be defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}{/tex}, then f-1(x) is
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = $\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ y(1- 3x) = 2x + 1 
$\Rightarrow$ y - 3xy = 2x + 1
$\Rightarrow$ 3xy + 2x = y - 1
$\Rightarrow$ x(3y + 2) = y - 1
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y-1}{3 y+2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y-1}{3 y+2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(x) = $\frac{x-1}{3 x+2}$

questions.explanation:
Let A denote the set of persons traveling by car, B denotes the set of persons traveling by bus, then
n(A) = 20, n(B) = 50, n(A $\cap$ B) = 10
$\therefore$ n(A $\cup$ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A $\cap$ B)
= 20 + 50 - 10 = 60

questions.question: If g(x) = x^2 + x - 2 and $\frac 1 2$(gof)(x) = 2x^2 - 5x + 2, then f(x) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If g(x) = x2 + x - 2 and {tex}\frac 1 2{/tex}(gof)(x) = 2x2 - 5x + 2, then f(x) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Consider, f(x) = 2x - 3; g(x) = x^2 + x - 2
$\therefore$ gof(x) = g[f(x)] = g[2x - 3]
= (2x - 3)^2 + 2x - 3 - 2
= 4x^2 + 9 - 12x + 12x - 3 - 2
= 4x^2 - 10x + 4
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2}$gof(x) = 2x^2 - 5x + 2
$\therefore$ f(x) = 2x - 3 is correct.

questions.question: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.explanation:
Let ${f(x)\; = \;\sin \;x + \;cos\;x}$
${\therefore \;f'(x)\; = {\text{co}}s\;x\; - \;\sin \;x}$
${ \Rightarrow \;f''\;(x)\; = \; - \sin \;x\; - \;\cos \;x}$
Now, ${f'(x)\; = 0}$
${ \Rightarrow \;\cos \;x\; - \;\sin \;x\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;\sin \;x\; = \;\cos \;x\; \Rightarrow \;\tan \;x\; = \;1}$
${ \Rightarrow \;x\; = n\pi \; + \;\frac{\pi }{4},\;{\text{n}} \in \;{\text{z}}}$
At ${{\text{x}}\; = \pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4},}$
${{\text{f''}}\;({\text{x}})\; = \; - \sin \;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \;\;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \;\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; + \cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \sqrt {2\;} \;\; > \;0}$
${\therefore \;{\text{x}}\; = \;\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}}$ is point of minimum
Minimum value ${ = \;\sin \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\;\; + \cos \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \; - \sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \sqrt 2 }$

questions.question: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Given Relation R = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)}
Reflexive: If a relation has {(a, b)} as its element, then it should also have {(a, a), (b, b)} as its elements too.
Symmetric: If a relation has (a, b) as its element, then it should also have {(b, a)} as its element too.
Transitive: If a relation has {(a, b), (b, c)} as its elements, then it should also have {(a,c)} as its element too.
Now, the given relation satisfies all these three properties.
Therefore, its an equivalence relation.

questions.question: In Z, the set of all integers, the inverse of –7 w.r.t. $*$ defined by a * b = a + b + 7 for all a, b $ \in $ Z, is
questions.question_raw: In Z, the set of all integers, the inverse of –7 w.r.t. {tex}*{/tex} defined by a * b = a + b + 7 for all a, b {tex} \in {/tex} Z, is
questions.explanation:
We have, a $\times$ b = a + b + 7
Let e be an identity element then, 
a $\times$ e = a = e $\times$ a
$\Rightarrow$ a $\times$ e = a and e $\times$ a = a
$\Rightarrow$ e = -7 $\in$ z
$\therefore$ -7 is identity elements
Since the inverse of identity elements, e is itself.
$\therefore $ inverse of - 7 = - 7

questions.question: A class has 175 students. The following data shows the number of students opting one or more subject. Maths - 100, Physics - 70, Chemistry - 40, Maths and Physics - 30, Maths and Chemistry - 38, Physics and Chemistry - 23, Maths, Physics and Chemistry - 18. How many have opted for Mathematics alone?
questions.question_raw: A class has 175 students. The following data shows the number of students opting one or more subject. Maths - 100, Physics - 70, Chemistry - 40, Maths and Physics - 30, Maths and Chemistry - 38, Physics and Chemistry - 23, Maths, Physics and Chemistry - 18. How many have opted for Mathematics alone?
questions.explanation:
Let M denote the set of students who had taken mathematics, P the set of students who had taken physics and C the set of students who had taken chemistry. Then we have
n(U) = 175, n(M) = 100, n(P) = 70, n(C) = 40, n(M $\cap$ P) = 30, n(M $\cap$ C) = 38, n(P $\cap$ C) = 23, n(M $\cap$ P $\cap$ C) = 18
$\therefore$ no. of students who opted for mathematics alone = n(M $\cap$ P' $\cap$ C')
= n(M $\cap$ P' $\cap$ C')
= n[M $\cap$ (P $\cap$ C)'] = n(M) - n(M $\cap$ (P $\cup$ C))
= n(M) - n[(M $\cap$ P) $\cup$ (M $\cap$ C)]
= n(M) - n[(M $\cap$ P) $\cup$ (M $\cap$ C)]
= n(M) - [n(M $\cap$ P) + n(M $\cap$ C) - n(M $\cap$ P $\cap$ C)]
= 100 - [30 + 38 - 18]
= 100 - [50] = 50

questions.question:
Consider the following relations: 1. A - B = A - $(A \cap B)$ 2. A = $(A \cap B)$ $\mathop \cup 
olimits $(A - B). 3. A - (B $\mathop \cup 
olimits $ C) = (A - B) $\mathop \cup 
olimits $ (A - C). Which of these is/are correct?
questions.question_raw: Consider the following relations: 1. A - B = A - {tex}(A \cap B){/tex} 2. A = {tex}(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex}(A - B). 3. A - (B {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex} C) = (A - B) {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex} (A - C). Which of these is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
$A - (A \cap B) = A \cap (A \cap B')\,\,[\because A - B = A \cap B']$
 $ = A \cap (A' \cap B')\,$[By De - morgan's law]
 $ = (A \cap A') \cup (A \cap B')\,$
 $ = \phi \cup (A \cap B') = A \cap B' = A - B\,$
 $(A \cap B) \cap (A - B) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap B')$
 $ = X \cup (A \cap B')$ Where X = $A \cap B$
 $ = (X \cup A) \cap (X \cup B')$
 $ = {\text{A}} \cap ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}})\left[ {{\text{X}} \cup {\text{A}} = ({\text{A}} \cap {\text{B}}) \cup {\text{A}} = {\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}} \right]$ $= {\text{A}}(\because {\text{A}}{\text{B}} \subset {\text{A}}){\text{X}} \cup {\text{B'}} $ $= ({\text{A}} \cap {\text{B}}) \cup {\text{B'}} = ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}) \cap ({\text{B}} \cup {\text{B'}}) = ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}) \cap {\text{U}}$
 ${\text{ = A}}\left[ {\because {\text{A}} \subset {\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}} \right]$
 This is correct because,
 $A - (B \cup C) = (A - B) \cap (A - C)$

questions.question: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The number of elements in the power set of the first is 48 more than the total number of elements in the power set of the second. Then the values of m and n are
questions.question_raw: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The number of elements in the power set of the first is 48 more than the total number of elements in the power set of the second. Then the values of m and n are
questions.explanation:
Let A has m elements and B gas n elements. Then, no. of elements in
P(A) = 2^m and no. of elements in P(B) = 2^n.]
By the question,
2^m = 2^n + 48
$\Rightarrow $ 2^m - 2^n = 48
This is possible, if 2^m = 64, 2^n = 16. (As 64 - 16 = 48) 
$\therefore$ 2m = 64 $\Rightarrow$ 2^m = 2^6 
$\Rightarrow$ m = 6
Also, 2^4 = 16 $\Rightarrow$ 2^4 = 24

questions.question: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Then the relation R = {(a, b) : 1 + ab > 0} on S is
questions.question_raw: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Then the relation R = {(a, b) : 1 + ab > 0} on S is

options.label: B
options.text: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

options.label: C
options.text: symmetric and transitive but not reflexive.

options.label: D
options.text: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.

correct_option_texts: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.
questions.explanation:
Reflexive: since 1 + a^2 > 0 $\forall \;{\text{a}} \in {\text{S}}$ 
$ \Rightarrow \;1 + \;{\text{a}},\;{\text{a}} > \;0\forall \;{\text{a}}\; \in \;{\text{S}}$ $\Rightarrow(a, a) \in R \forall a$ 
$ \Rightarrow $ R is reflexive.
Symmetric: Let a, b $ \in $ S Such that (a, b) $ \in $ R, then,
1 + ab > 0 $\Rightarrow$ 1 + ba > 0 $\Rightarrow$ (b, a ) $\in$ R
$ \Rightarrow $ R is symmetric.
Transitivity: Here 1 + 1.2 = 1 + 2 = 3 > 0 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 + (-2)(-3) = 1 + 6 = 7 > 0
$\Rightarrow$ (1, 2), (-2, -3) $\in$ R
Now, 1 + 1(-3) = 1 - 3 = -2 < 0
$\therefore $ (1, -3) $\notin$ R
$\therefore$ R is not a transition.

questions.question: A function f : [0, $\infty $] $ \to $ [0, $\infty $) defined as $f(x) =\frac {x} {1 + x}$ is
questions.question_raw: A function f : [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}] {tex} \to {/tex} [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}) defined as {tex}f(x) =\frac {x} {1 + x}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
${{\text{f}}:\left[ {0,\omega } \right]\; \to \left( {0,\omega } \right)}$ is defined by,
${{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;\frac{{\text{x}}}{{1 + {\text{x}}}}}$
one - one : ${{\text{f}}({{\text{x}}_1})\; = \;{\text{f}}({{\text{x}}_2})}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{1+\mathrm{x}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_2}{1+\mathrm{x}_2}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1\left(1+\mathrm{x}_2\right)=\mathrm{x}_2\left(1+\mathrm{x}_1\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1=\mathrm{x}_2$
$ \Rightarrow $ f is one - one
onto: Let ${{\text{y}} \in \left( {0,\omega } \right)}$ (co - domain) be arbitrary, such that f(x) = y
$\Rightarrow \frac{x}{1+x=y}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{y}} \notin(0, \omega) \forall \mathrm{y} \in(0, \omega)$
e.g. y = 2 $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{1-2}=\frac{2}{-1}=-2 \notin(0, \omega)$
$\therefore$ f is not onto

questions.question: If f : [1, $\infty \infty $) $ \to $ [2, $\infty \infty $) is given by f(x) = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ then f^-1(x) equals]
questions.question_raw: If f : [1, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) {tex} \to {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) is given by f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} then f-1(x) equals]
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 1 = xy $\Rightarrow$ x^2 - xy + 1 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ (negative sign is rejected)
f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}$

questions.question: A function f from the natural numbers to the set of integers defined by f(n) = $ \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{n - 1}}{2},\;\;when\;n\;is\;odd \\ - \frac{n}{2},\;\;\;\;when\;n\;is\;even \\ \end{gathered} \right.$ is
questions.question_raw: A function f from the natural numbers to the set of integers defined by f(n) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{n - 1}}{2},\;\;when\;n\;is\;odd \\ - \frac{n}{2},\;\;\;\;when\;n\;is\;even \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
F(n) = {$\frac{n-1}{2}$, when n is odd; $\frac{-n}{2}$, when n is even 
one - one : Let n_1, n_2 $\in$ N
Case I: n_1 is even, n_2 is even 
$\therefore$ f(n_1) - f(n_2) $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-n_{1}}{2}=\frac{-n_{2}}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ n_1 = n_2
Case II: n_1 is odd, n_2 is odd 
$\therefore$ f(n_1) - f(n_2) $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-n-1}{2}=\frac{-n-2}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ n_1 = n_2 
Case III: n_1 is even, n_2 is odd 
$\therefore f({n_1}) - \frac{{ - {n_1}}}{2} = $ even as n_1 is even
$\therefore $ f(n_2) is odd, $\frac{{{n_2} - 1}}{2}$ = even as n_2 is odd
But f(n_1) takes values, -1, -2, -3,..... f(n^2) take values, 0, 1, 2, 3,.....
$\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2})$ 
Similarly n, is odd, n_2, is even, then $\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2})$ 
$\Rightarrow $ f is one - one
onto: f(n) = {$\frac{n-1}{2}$, when n is odd; $\frac{-n}{2}$, when n is even} 
$\therefore$ f(1) = 0, f(2) = 1, f(5) = 2, f(7) = 3, f(9) - 4, ... f(2)= -1, f(4) = -2, f(6) = -3, f(8) = -4, ......
$\therefore$ Range of f = {......, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ....) = z
$\therefore$ F is onto

For f(x) to be real, we must have
4 - x^2 $ \ne$ 0 and x^3 - x > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 $ \ne$ 4 and x(x^2 - 1) > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \ne$ 2, -2 and x(x - 1)(x + 1) > 0

$\therefore$ Domain = (-1, 0) $\cup$ (1, 2) $\cup$ (2, $\infty$)

questions.question: If f : $R \to R$ satisfies f (x + y) = f (x ) + f (y) for all x, y $ \in $ R and f (1) = 7, then $\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {f(r)} $ is
questions.question_raw: If f : {tex}R \to R{/tex} satisfies f (x + y) = f (x ) + f (y) for all x, y {tex} \in {/tex} R and f (1) = 7, then {tex}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {f(r)} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y).....(i) and f(1) = 7
Put x=1, y=1 in equation (i), we obtain quad
f(1+1) = f(1) + f(1) = 14 $\Rightarrow$ f(2) = 14
Similarly f(1 + 1 + 1) = f(2) + f(1) = 14 + 7 $\Rightarrow$ f(3) = 21
Since we have f(1) = 1 $\times$ 7 = 7, f(2) = 2 $\times$ 7 = 14, f(3) = 3 $\times$ 7 = 21,........
we can get f(n) = n $\times$ 7 = 7n
Now $\sum \limits_{r=1}^{n}$ f(r) = f(1) + f(2) + f(3) + ..... + f(n)
7^r=1 + 14 + 21 + ..... + 7n
= 7[1 + 2 + 3 + ..... + n] = 7 $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, tan $\frac{A}{2}=\frac{5}{6}, \tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{20}{37}$ then tan $\frac C 2$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, tan {tex}\frac{A}{2}=\frac{5}{6}, \tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{20}{37}{/tex} then tan {tex}\frac C 2{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given tan $\frac{A}{2}$ = $\frac{5}{6}$ $\Rightarrow$ cot $\frac{A}{2}$ = $\frac{6}{5}$, tan $\frac{B}{2}$ = $\frac{20}{37}$ $\Rightarrow$ cot $\frac{B}{2}$ = $\frac{37}{20}$ 
Given A, B and C are angles of a triangle 
Then, A + B + C = 180
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{A+B+C}{2}$ = $\frac{180}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{A+B}{2}+\frac{C}{2}$ = 90
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{C}{2}$ = $90-\frac{A+B}{2}$ 
Taking tan on both sides, we get
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \frac{C}{2}$ = $\tan \left(90-\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \frac{C}{2}$ = $\cot \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$ = $\cot \left(\frac{A}{2}+\frac{B}{2}\right)$ = $\frac{\cot \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cot \frac{B}{2}-1}{\cot \frac{A}{2}+\cot \frac{B}{2}}$ = $\frac{\frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{37}{20}-1}{\frac{6}{5}+\frac{37}{20}}$ = $\frac{\frac{222}{100}-1}{\frac{305}{100}}$ = $\frac{\frac{122}{100}}{\frac{305}{100}}$ = $\frac{122}{305}$ = $\frac{2}{5}$

Given $A=75^{\circ} \text { and } B=45^{\circ}$
then $A+B+C=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow 75+45+C=180$
$\Rightarrow$C=180-120=60
From sin e formula
$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=k(\operatorname{lc} t)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{\sin 75}=\frac{b}{\sin 45}=\frac{c}{\sin 60}=k$
Here
$\frac{a}{\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}}=k, \frac{b}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=k, \frac{c}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=k$ Now,
$\Rightarrow a=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) k, b=\frac{k}{\sqrt{2}}, c=\frac{\sqrt{3} k}{2}$
$b+2 c=\frac{k}{\sqrt{2}}+2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3} k}{2}=\frac{2 k+2 \sqrt{3} k}{2 \sqrt{2}}$$=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) k=2 a$

Then, x + 2x + 7x = 180^o 
$\Rightarrow$ 10x = 180
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{180}{10}$ = 18^o 
From sin formula
$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$ = k(let)
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a}{\sin 18}=\frac{b}{\sin 36}=\frac{c}{\sin 126}$ = k
Here a = k sin 18, b = k sin 36, c = k sin 126

a : b = $\sqrt{5}+1: \sqrt{5}-1$

questions.question: If the sides of a triangle are 13, 7, 8 the greatest angle of the triangle is
questions.question_raw: If the sides of a triangle are 13, 7, 8 the greatest angle of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Let the sides of the triangle be a = 13, b = 7 and c = 8.
Since a is the longest side the greatest angle is A.
Using Cosine rule, we have
cos A = $\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}$ = $\frac{49+64-169}{112}$ = $\frac{-1}{2}$
But we have $\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)$ = $\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $-\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $\frac{-1}{2}$
$\therefore$ A = $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$

Now using sine rule a = k sin A, b = k sin B, c = k sin C

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, $\tan \frac{A+B}{2} \cot \frac{A-B}{2}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, {tex}\tan \frac{A+B}{2} \cot \frac{A-B}{2}{/tex} is equal to

We have ${ tan\left( \frac { A-B }{ 2 } \right) }=\frac { a-b }{ a+b } .cot\frac { C }{ 2 } $
But in a triangle ABC, we have A + B + C = ${ 180 }^{ \circ }\Rightarrow \frac { C }{ 2 } ={ 90 }^{ \circ }-\left( \frac { A+B }{ 2 } \right) $
$\therefore \tan \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=\frac{a-b}{a+b} \cdot \cot \left[90^{\circ}-\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{\dot{A}-B}{2}\right)=\frac{a-b}{a+b} \cdot \tan \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=\frac{a+b}{a-b}$

questions.question_raw: If b2 + c2 = 3a2, then cot B + cot C - cot A =
questions.explanation:
We have cos A = $\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}$, cos B = $\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{2 c a}$, cos C = $\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{2 a b}$ 
Also sin A = $\frac{2 \Delta}{b c}$, sin B = $\frac{2 \Delta}{c a}$, sin C = $\frac{2 \Delta}{a b}$, where $\Delta$ = $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$ 
Hence we get cos B + cos C - cot A = $\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{4 \Delta}+\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{4 \Delta}-\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{4 \Delta}$ = $\frac{3 a^{2}-\left(b^{2}+c^{2}\right)}{4 \Delta}=\frac{0}{4 \Delta}$ = 0 [$\because$ 3a^2 = b^2+ c^2]

questions.question: $bc{\cos ^2}{\frac A 2} + ca{\cos ^2}{\frac B 2} + ab{\cos ^2}{\frac C 2}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}bc{\cos ^2}{\frac A 2} + ca{\cos ^2}{\frac B 2} + ab{\cos ^2}{\frac C 2}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$b \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)+{cacos}^{2}\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)+a b \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)$
$=b c\left(\frac{1+\cos A}{2}\right)+c a\left(\frac{1+\cos B}{2}\right)+a b\left(\frac{1+\cos C}{2}\right)$
$=b c\left(\frac{1+\left(\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}\right)}{2}\right)+c a\left(\frac{1+\left(\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{2 c a}\right)}{2}\right)+a b\left(\frac{1+\left(\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{2 a b}\right)}{2}\right)$
$=b c\left(\frac{2 b c+b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{4 b c}\right)+c a\left(\frac{2 c a+c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{4 c a}\right)+a b\left(\frac{2 a b+a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{4 a b}\right)$
$=\frac{2 b c+b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}+2 c a+c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a b+a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{4}$
$=\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+2 b c+2 c a+2 a b}{4}$
$=\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}\right)^{2} \quad[\because \quad 2 s=a+b+c]$
$=s^{2}$

questions.question: If the sides of a triangle be 2x + 1, x^2 - 1 and x^2 + x + 1, then the greatest angle is
questions.question_raw: If the sides of a triangle be 2x + 1, x2 - 1 and x2 + x + 1, then the greatest angle is
questions.explanation:
Let the sides of the triangle be a = 2x + 1, b = x^2 - 1 and c = x^2 + x + 1
We have cos C = $\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{2 a b}$ = $\frac{(2 x+1)^{2}+\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}-\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)^{2}}{2(2 x+1)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}$ 
= $\frac{4 x^{2}+4 x+1+x^{4}-2 x^{2}+1-\left(x^{4}+x^{2}+1+2 x^{3}+2 x^{2}+2 x\right)}{2\left(2 x^{3}-2 x+x^{2}-1\right)}$ 
= $\frac{-2 x^{3}+2 x-x^{2}+1}{2\left(2 x^{3}-2 x+x^{2}-1\right)}$ = $\frac{-1}{2}$ 
Now cos C = $\frac{-1}{2}$ 
But we have $\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)$ = $\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $-\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $\frac{-1}{2}$ 
$\therefore$ C = $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ = 120^o

questions.question: If in a triangle ABC, $a{\cos ^2}\left( {{\frac C2}} \right) + c{\cos ^2}\left( {{\frac A2}} \right) = {\frac {3b} {2}}$ then its sides a, b, c are in
questions.question_raw: If in a triangle ABC, {tex}a{\cos ^2}\left( {{\frac C2}} \right) + c{\cos ^2}\left( {{\frac A2}} \right) = {\frac {3b} {2}}{/tex} then its sides a, b, c are in
questions.explanation:
$a \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)+c \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)$ = $\frac{3 b}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $a\left(\frac{1+\cos C}{2}\right)+c\left(\frac{1+\cos A}{2}\right)$ = $\frac{3b}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2}$ (b + a + c) = $\frac{3b}{2}$ [$\because$ a cos C + c cos A = b using projection rule]
$\Rightarrow$ a + c = 2b
Which means they are in A.P.

questions.question: In a $\Delta ABC$, if a sin A = b sin B then the triangle is
questions.question_raw: In a {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, if a sin A = b sin B then the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Given a sin A = b sin B .....(i)
But using sine rule we have $\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$ = k
$\therefore$ sin A = $\frac{a}{k}$ and sin B = $\frac{b}{k}$ 
Now from (i) we get $\frac{1}{k} $(a^2 - b^2) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ (a + b) (a - b) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ a = b
Hence the triangle is isosceles.

questions.question: If in a $\Delta ABC$ , a cos A = b cos B, then the triangle is
questions.question_raw: If in a {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} , a cos A = b cos B, then the triangle is
questions.explanation:
a.cos A=b.cos B
$\Rightarrow a \cdot\left(\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}\right)=b \cdot\left(\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{2 c a}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}\left(b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}\right)-b^{2}\left(c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}\right)}{2 a b c}=0$
$\Rightarrow a^{2} c^{2}-b^{2} c^{2}=a^{4}-b^{4}$
$\Rightarrow c^{2}\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)=\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a^{2}-b^{2}=0 \quad \text { or } \quad a^{2}+b^{2}=c^{2}$$\Rightarrow a=b \quad \text { or } \quad a^{2}+b^{2}=c^{2}$
Hence the triangle is either isosceles or right angle

questions.question: In a $\Delta ABC$, $\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{a}+\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}{b}+\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)}{c}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: In a {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, {tex}\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{a}+\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}{b}+\frac{\cos ^2\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)}{c}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\frac{\cos ^{2}\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{a}+\frac{\cos ^{2}\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}{b}+\frac{\cos ^{2}\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)}{c}$ = $\frac{\frac{1+\cos A}{2}}{a}+\frac{\frac{1+\cos B}{2}}{b}+\frac{\frac{1+\cos C}{2}}{c}$ 
= $\frac { \frac { 1+\left( \frac { { b }^{ 2 }+{ c }^{ 2 }-a^{ 2 } }{ 2bc } \right) }{ 2 } }{ a } +\frac { \frac { 1+\left( \frac { { c }^{ 2 }+a^{ 2 }-{ b }^{ 2 } }{ 2ca } \right) }{ 2 } }{ b } +\frac { \frac { 1+\left( \frac { { a^{ 2 }+b }^{ 2 }-{ c }^{ 2 } }{ 2ab } \right) }{ 2 } }{ c }$ 
= $\frac{2 b c+b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}+2 c a+c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}+2 a b+a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{4 a b c}$ 
= $\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+2 b c+2 c a+2 a b}{4 a b c}$ 
= $\frac{\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}\right)^{2}}{a b c}$ [$\because$ 2s = a + b + c]
= $\frac{s^{2}}{a b c}$

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, a = 2b and $\angle A = 3\angle B,$ then angle A is
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, a = 2b and {tex}\angle A = 3\angle B,{/tex} then angle A is
questions.explanation:
Using Sine rule we have $\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$ = k
But given a = 2b and $\angle A=3 \angle B$ 
$\therefore$ $\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 b}{\sin 3 B}=\frac{b}{\sin B}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ sin 3B = 2 sin B
$\Rightarrow$ 3 sin B - 4 sin^3 B = 2 sin B
$\Rightarrow$ sin B (1 - 4 sin^2 B) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ sin^2 B = $\frac{1}{4}$ [$\because$ sin B $\ne$ 0]
$\Rightarrow$ sin B = $\frac{\pm 1}{2}$ 
Now sin B = $\frac{1}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ B = 30^o and sin B = $\frac{-1}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ B = 210^o, which is not possible.
$\Rightarrow$ B = 30^o
Hence $\angle A=3 \angle B$ = 90^o

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, AD is the median A to BC, then its length is equal to
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, AD is the median A to BC, then its length is equal to
questions.explanation:
Using the given information let the length of the median be d units, and let BD = DB = m units. Using Apollonius theorem we have the following results.
From triangle ADC, if the angle ADC is $\theta$, then b^2 = m^2 + d^2 - 2mdcos $\theta$. The angle ADB is the supplement of angle ADC and it will be - cos $\theta$. Hence from triangle ADB we have b^2 = m^2 + d^2+ 2mdcos $\theta$ . When we add both the results we have b^2 + c^2 = 2 d^2 + 2 m^2 = 2 d^2 + 2 $(\frac{a}{2})^2$ = 2 d^2 + ( $\frac{a^2}{2} $).
On simplifying we get the result.

questions.question: If cos A + cos B = $4{\sin ^2}{\frac C 2}$, then the sides of the triangle ABC are in
questions.question_raw: If cos A + cos B = {tex}4{\sin ^2}{\frac C 2}{/tex}, then the sides of the triangle ABC are in
questions.explanation:
cos A + cos B = $4 \sin ^{2} \frac{C}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ = $4 \sin ^{2} \frac{C}{2}$ [$\because$ $\cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$ = $\sin \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)$] 
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ = $2 \sin \frac{C}{2}$ = $\frac{2 \sin \frac{C}{2} \cos \frac{C}{2}}{\cos \frac{C}{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos \frac{C}{2} \cdot \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ = sin C [$\because$$\sin \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$ = $\cos \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\sin A+\sin B}{2}$ = sin C [$\because$ 2sin A cos B = sin (A + b) + sin (A - B)]
$\Rightarrow$ sin A, sin B, sin C are in A.P.
$\Rightarrow$ a, b, c are in A.P. using Sine rule

questions.question: ABC is an equilateral triangle of each side a ($>$ 0). The inradius of the triangle is
questions.question_raw: ABC is an equilateral triangle of each side a ({tex}>{/tex} 0). The inradius of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
If r is the inradius of the circle then we have, r = $\frac{\Delta}{s}$ 
Given ABC is an equilateral triangle, so $\triangle$ = $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\triangle$ = $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-a)(s-a)}$ 
$\therefore$ r = $\frac{\Delta}{s}=\frac{s-a}{s} \sqrt{s(s-a)}$ 
Also, s = $\frac{3 a}{2}$ [$\because$ 2s = a + b + c]
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\frac{\frac{3 a}{2}-a}{\frac{3 a}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{3 a}{2}\left(\frac{3 a}{2}-a\right)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\frac{a}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3 a} \sqrt{\frac{3 a^{2}}{4}}=\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{3}}$

questions.question: In any $\Delta ABC,$ the expression (a + b + c) (a + b – c) (b + c – a) (c + a – b) is equal to
questions.question_raw: In any {tex}\Delta ABC,{/tex} the expression (a + b + c) (a + b – c) (b + c – a) (c + a – b) is equal to
questions.explanation:
In a triangle A B C, we have 2s=a+b+c
$\therefore a+b-c=2(s-c), b+c-a=2(s-a), c+a-b=2(s-b)$
Hence $(a+b+c)(a+b-c)(b+c-a)(c+a-b)$ 
= $2 s .2(s-c) \cdot 2(s-a) \cdot 2(s-b)$
= $16 s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)$
But we have $\Delta=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$
$\therefore(a+b+c)(a+b-c)(b+c-a)(c+a-b)=4 \Delta^{2}$

questions.question: In any $\Delta ABC$, if C = 90^o, then tan $\frac B2$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: In any {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, if C = 90o, then tan {tex}\frac B2{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
In any triangle ABC, A + C = 90^o, since C = 90^o $\Rightarrow$ B = 90^o - A 
We know that $tan(\frac{(C-A)}{2})= \frac{(c-a)}{(c +a)} cot⁡(\frac{B}{2}) $ $\Rightarrow$ $tan (\frac{(90 - A)}{2})$ = $\frac{(c-a)}{(c +a)} (\frac{1}{tan⁡(\frac{B}{2})}) $ 
$\Rightarrow$ $tan (\frac{B}{2}) tan (\frac{B}{2})$ = $\frac{(c-a)}{(c+a)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $tan^2 (\frac{B}{2}) $ = $\frac{(c-a)}{(c+a)}$ 
Hence the result follows.

questions.question: Angles of triangle are in A.P. If the number of degrees in the smallest to the number of radians in the largest is as $60:\pi $, then the smallest angle is
questions.question_raw: Angles of triangle are in A.P. If the number of degrees in the smallest to the number of radians in the largest is as {tex}60:\pi {/tex}, then the smallest angle is
questions.explanation:
Let the measures of the angles of the triangle be a-d, a, a+d degrees respectively
Then we have $a-d+a+a+d=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow 3 a=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow a=\frac{180^{\circ}}{3}=60^{\circ}$
But it is clear that $a-d=60^{\circ}-d$ is the least and $a+d= 60^{\circ}+d$ is the greatest angle.
Given greatest angle = $\pi$ $\text {radian}=180^{\circ}$
$\frac{\text {least angle}}{\text {greatest angle}}=\frac{60^{\circ}}{\pi \text {radian}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{60^{\circ}-d}{60^{\circ}+d}=\frac{60^{\circ}}{180^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\Rightarrow 180^{\circ}-3 d=60^{\circ}+d$
$\Rightarrow 4 d=120^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow d=30^{\circ}$

questions.question: If x, y, z are perpendiculars drawn from the vertices of a triangle having sides a, b and c, then $\frac{b x}{c}+\frac{c y}{a}+\frac{a z}{b}$ =
questions.question_raw: If x, y, z are perpendiculars drawn from the vertices of a triangle having sides a, b and c, then {tex}\frac{b x}{c}+\frac{c y}{a}+\frac{a z}{b}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
In triangle ABC given that the length of the altitude from A to BC is x units. let AD be the altitude. from triangle ADC sin C = x/b and from triangle ABD sin B = x/c Hence we get two values of x as x = c sin B and x = b sin C . similarly we have y = a sin C and z = bsin A. so when we substitute in the given equation we get b sin B $
eq$ c sinC $
eq$ a sin A.
The sine formula gives that $ rac{a}{sin⁡A} =  rac{b}{sin⁡B} =  rac{c}{sin⁡C} =2R$ where R is the circumcenter of the triangle.By replacing Sin A = $ rac{a}{2R}$, sin B = $ rac{b}{2R}$, sin C =$ rac{c}{2R}$.
If we use these in b sin B $
eq$ c sinC $
eq$ a sin A. we get the required result.

questions.question: The greatest value of sin x cos x is
questions.question_raw: The greatest value of sin x cos x is
questions.explanation:
sin x cos x = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot$2sin x cos x = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot$ sin 2x
But the maximum value of sin 2x is 1.
So the maximum value of sin x cos x = $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: If $tan\theta + \sec \theta = \sqrt 3 ,0 < \theta< \;\pi ,$ then $\theta $ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}tan\theta + \sec \theta = \sqrt 3 ,0 < \theta< \;\pi ,{/tex} then {tex}\theta {/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$tan\theta +\sec \theta =\sqrt { 3 } $
$\Rightarrow { sec }^{ 2 }x={ \left[ \sqrt { 3 } -tan\theta \right] }^{ 2 }$
$\Rightarrow \left( 1+{ tan }^{ 2 }x \right) =3+{ tan }^{ 2 }x-2\sqrt { 3 } tanx$
$\Rightarrow 2\sqrt { 3 } tanx=2$
$tanx=\frac { 1 }{ \sqrt { 3 } } $
$\Rightarrow tan x=tan\left( \frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right)$
$\Rightarrow x=n\pi +\left( \frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right) ,n \in Z$
$\Rightarrow x=\frac { \pi }{ 6 } ,0<\theta < \pi $

questions.question: If sin $({120^o} - \alpha ) = \sin ({120^o} - \beta )$ and 0 $ < \alpha ,\beta {\rm{ }} < {\rm{ }}\pi $ , then all values of $ \alpha$ and $\beta$ are given by
questions.question_raw: If sin {tex}({120^o} - \alpha ) = \sin ({120^o} - \beta ){/tex} and 0 {tex} < \alpha ,\beta {\rm{ }} < {\rm{ }}\pi {/tex} , then all values of {tex} \alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are given by
questions.explanation:
Sin A = sin B will be sin A - sin B = 0 using the formula we get,
2 cos $\frac{A+B}{2}$ sin $\frac{A-B}{2}$ = 0
Replacing A by 120 - $α$ and B by 120 - $β$ we get 
$cos(\frac{(120- \alpha+120- \beta)}{2})$ = 0
$cos⁡[\frac{(240- \alpha - \beta)}{2}] $ = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ 120 - $\alpha$ - $\beta$ = $\pi$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\alpha + \beta =60= \frac{\pi}{3}$ the other result.
Replacing A by 120 - $α$ and B by 120 - $β$ we get sin A- B = 0 which gives $α=β$

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, a = 13, b = 14, c = 15; then the radius of the incircle r of triangle ABC =
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, a = 13, b = 14, c = 15; then the radius of the incircle r of triangle ABC =
questions.explanation:
If r is the inradius of the circle then we have, r = $\frac{\Delta}{s}$ 
Given a = 13, b = 14 and c = 15 and so s = $\frac{a+b+c}{2}$ = $\frac{ 42}{2}$ = 21
$\therefore$ $\Delta$ = $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$ = $\sqrt{21(21-13)(21-14)(21-15)}$ = 84
r = $\frac{\Delta}{s}$ = $\frac{84}{21}$ = 4

questions.question: If sin $\theta + {\rm{ cosec }}\theta $ = 2, then ${\sin ^2}\theta + \cos e{c^2}\theta $ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If sin {tex}\theta + {\rm{ cosec }}\theta {/tex} = 2, then {tex}{\sin ^2}\theta + \cos e{c^2}\theta {/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given $\sin \theta+cosec \theta=2$ 
Squaring on both sides, we get 
$\sin ^{2} \theta+\csc ^{2} \theta+2 \sin \theta \csc \theta=4$ [$\because$ $\sin \theta cosec \theta=1$]
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin ^{2} \theta+cosec ^{2} \theta$ = 4 - 2 = 2

questions.question: The value of tan ${75^ \circ }$ - cot ${75^ \circ }$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: The value of tan {tex}{75^ \circ }{/tex} - cot {tex}{75^ \circ }{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
tan 75° = tan(45° + 30°
$= \frac{{1 + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}{{1 - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}\therefore cot{75^\circ } = \frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}$
Now $tan{75^\circ } - cot{75^\circ } = \frac{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}{{\sqrt 3 - 1}} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}$
$ = \frac{{{{\left( {\sqrt 3 + 1} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {\sqrt 3 - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{\left( {\sqrt 3 + 1} \right)\left( {\sqrt 3 - 1} \right)}}$
$\Rightarrow tan{75^\circ } - cot{75^\circ } = \frac{{4\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 2\sqrt 3 $

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, cosec A (sin B cos C + cos B sin C) equals

questions.explanation:

= $\frac{\sin (B+C)}{\sin A}=\frac{\sin (\pi-A)}{\sin A}=\frac{\sin A}{\sin A}$ = 1

questions.explanation: Using the given triangle we have tan A = a/b and tan B = b/a Also we have tan A . tan B = 1 and tan A + tan B = a/b + b /a = ( a^2 + b^2 )/ab = c^2 / ab . Hence x = tan A and x = tan B are two roots of the given quadratic equation.

questions.question: Let the angles A, B, C of $\Delta ABC$ be in A.P. and let b: c:: $\sqrt 3 :\sqrt 2 ,$ then the angle A is
questions.question_raw: Let the angles A, B, C of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} be in A.P. and let b: c:: {tex}\sqrt 3 :\sqrt 2 ,{/tex} then the angle A is
questions.explanation:
Since A, B, C are in A.P. we get $\frac{A+C}{2}$ = B $\Rightarrow$ A + C = 2B .....(i)
But in triangle ABc, we have A + B + C = $\pi$ $\Rightarrow$ 3B = $\pi$ $\Rightarrow$ B = $\frac{\pi}{3}$ 
From (i) A + C = $2 \frac{\pi}{3}$ $\Rightarrow$ C = $\frac{2 \pi}{3}-A$ 
Using sine rule we have $\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$ = k (say)
Now $\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{b}{\sin \frac{\pi}{3}}=\frac{c}{\sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}-A\right)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}-A\right)$ = $\frac{c}{b} \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ [$\because$ b : c = $\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2}$ ]
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}-A\right)$ = $\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 \pi}{3}-A$ = $\frac{\pi}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ A = $\frac{5 \pi}{12}$ = 75^o

questions.question: cot $\theta $ = sin 2 $\theta (\theta $ $ \ne $n $\pi $, n integer) if $\theta $ equals
questions.question_raw: cot {tex}\theta {/tex} = sin 2 {tex}\theta (\theta {/tex} {tex} \ne {/tex}n {tex}\pi {/tex}, n integer) if {tex}\theta {/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
sin 2$\theta$ = cot $\theta$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 2 sin $\theta$ cos $\theta$ = $\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos \theta\left(2 \sin \theta-\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right)$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ -cos $\theta$ (1 - 2 sin^2 $\theta$) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ -cos $\theta$ $\cdot$ cos 2$\theta$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ cos $\theta$ = cos $\frac{\pi}{2}$ or cos 2$\theta$ = cos $\frac{\pi}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\theta$ = $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}$ [$\because$ $\theta \neq n \pi$, n $\in$ Z]

questions.question: If A = ${\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta $ then for all real values of $\theta $
questions.question_raw: If A = {tex}{\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta {/tex} then for all real values of {tex}\theta {/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given, A = $\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{4} \theta$ 
$\Rightarrow$ A = $\sin ^{2} \theta+\left(1-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ A = $\sin ^{4} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta+1$ 
Let $\sin ^{2} \theta$ = x, then
We get A = $x^{2}-x+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ A = $\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{3}{4}$ 
But we have $\sin ^{2} \theta$ = x ranges in the interval [0, 1]
Hence we get $\frac{3}{4} \leq A \leq 1$

questions.question: The general solution of tan 3x = 1 is (n $ \in $ I)
questions.question_raw: The general solution of tan 3x = 1 is (n {tex} \in {/tex} I)
questions.explanation:
tan 3x = 1
$\Rightarrow$ tan (3x) = tan ($\frac{\pi}{4}$)
$\Rightarrow$ 3x = $n \pi+\frac{\pi}{4}, n \in Z$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{n \pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{12}, n \in Z$

questions.question: The value of $\sin \frac{\pi}{14} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{5 \pi}{14} \sin$$ \frac{7 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{9 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{11 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{12}{1}$ is
questions.question_raw: The value of  {tex}\sin \frac{\pi}{14} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{5 \pi}{14} \sin{/tex}{tex} \frac{7 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{9 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{11 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{12}{1}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
First, convert the odd multiples of $\frac{\pi}{14}$ to even multiples using the formulas $\sin \theta=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ and $\cos \theta=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\theta\right)$ 
So, we get $\sin \frac{\pi}{14}=\cos \frac{6 \pi}{14}$, $\sin \frac{3 \pi}{14}=\cos \frac{4 \pi}{14}$, $\sin \frac{5 \pi}{14}=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{14}$, $\sin \frac{7 \pi}{14}$ = 1
$\sin \frac{9 \pi}{14}=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{2 \pi}{14}\right)$ = $\cos \frac{2 \pi}{14}$, $\sin \frac{11 \pi}{14}=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{4 \pi}{14}\right)$ = $\cos \frac{4 \pi}{14}, \sin \frac{13 \pi}{14}$ = $\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{6 \pi}{14}\right)=\cos \frac{6 \pi}{14}$ 
Now $\sin \frac{\pi}{14} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{5 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{7 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{9 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{11 \pi}{14} \sin \frac{13 \pi}{14}$ 
= $\cos ^{2} \frac{6 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{4 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}$ 
= $\frac{\left(\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}\right) \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{4 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{6 \pi}{14}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ [$\because$ sin 2x = 2 sinx cos x]
= $\frac{\frac{1}{4} \sin ^{2} \frac{4 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{4 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{6 \pi}{14}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ 
= $\frac{\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \sin ^{2} \frac{8 x}{14} \cdot \cos ^{2} \frac{6 \pi}{14}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ [$\because$ sin 2x = 2 sin x cos x]
= $\frac{\frac{1}{16} \cdot\left(\sin \frac{8 \pi}{14} \cdot \cos \frac{6 \pi}{14}\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ 
= $\frac{\frac{1}{16} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot\left(\sin \frac{14 \pi}{14}+\sin \frac{2 \pi}{14}\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ [$\because$ 2 sin A cos B = sin (A + B) + sin (A - B)]
= $\frac{\frac{1}{64} \cdot\left(0+\sin \frac{2 \pi}{14}\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \frac{2 \pi}{14}}$ [$\because$ sin $\pi$ = 0]
= $\frac{1}{64}$

questions.question: If the radius of the circumcircle of an isosceles triangle PQR is equal to PQ ( = PR), then the angle P is
questions.question_raw: If the radius of the circumcircle of an isosceles triangle PQR is equal to PQ ( = PR), then the angle P is
questions.explanation:
We have if R is the radius of the circumcircle of triangle PQR then we have
$R=\frac{P Q}{2 \sin R}=\frac{Q R}{2 \sin P}=\frac{P R}{2 \sin Q}$ ...(i)
But given radius of the circumcircle R = PQ = PR ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get $\frac{1}{2 \sin R}=1 $ and $\frac{1}{2 \sin Q}=1$
$\Rightarrow \sin R=\sin Q=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \angle R=\angle Q=30^{\circ}$
But in a triangle $\angle P+\angle Q+\angle R=180^{\circ}$
So, $\angle P=180^{\circ}-30^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=120^{\circ}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$

questions.question: The maximum value of sin $\left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ in the interval $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is attained at
questions.question_raw: The maximum value of sin {tex}\left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} in the interval {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} is attained at
questions.explanation:
$\sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\sin x \cdot \cos \frac{\pi}{6}+\cos x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{6}$$+\cos x \cdot \cos \frac{\pi}{6}-\sin x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{6}$
$=\sin x \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\cos x \cdot \frac{1}{2}+\cos x \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{2}$
$=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2}\right) \sin x+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2}\right) \cos x$
$=\sqrt{2}\left[\sin 15^{\circ} \sin x+\cos 15^{\circ} \cos x\right]$
$=\sqrt{2}\left[\cos \left(x-15^{\circ}\right)\right]$
But we have the maximum value of cos x is 1, when x = 0
Hence $\cos \left(x-15^{\circ}\right)$ takes its maximum value when $x-15^{\circ}=0 \Longrightarrow$ $x=15^{\circ} \Longrightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{12}$

questions.question: The solution of tan 2 $\theta $ tan $\theta $ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The solution of tan 2 {tex}\theta {/tex} tan {tex}\theta {/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
$\tan 2 \theta \cdot \tan \theta$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan 2 \theta=\cot \theta$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan 2 \theta$ = $\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ 2 \theta$ = $n \pi+\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ 3 \theta$ = $n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ \theta$ = $\frac{n \pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ \theta$ = $(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{6}, n \in Z$

questions.question: sin $\left( {180 + \phi } \right)$ sin$\left( {180 - \phi } \right)$ $cose{c^2}\phi $

questions.explanation: $\sin (180+\phi)(180-\phi) \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \phi$ = $-\sin \phi \cdot \sin \phi \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \phi$ = $-\sin ^{2} \phi \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \phi$ = -1

questions.question: The general solution of the equation sin x + cos x = 2 is
questions.question_raw: The general solution of the equation sin x + cos x = 2 is
questions.explanation:
Given $sinx+cosx=2$ ...(i)
This is of the form acos x + bsin x = c
Now divide the equation (i) throughout by $\sqrt { { a }^{ 2 }+{ b }^{ 2 } } =\sqrt { 1+1 } =\sqrt { 2 } $, we get
$\frac { 1 }{ \sqrt { 2 } } cosx+\frac { 1 }{ \sqrt { 2 } } sinx=\frac { 2 }{ \sqrt { 2 } }$
$\Rightarrow cos\left( \frac { \pi }{ 4 } \right) cosx+sinxsin\left( \frac { \pi }{ 4 } \right) =\sqrt { 2 }$
$\Rightarrow cos\left[ \frac { \pi }{ 4 } -x \right] =\sqrt { 2 } > 1$
So no solution possible

questions.question:
Which of the following is not possible?
 $\cos \theta=\frac{1+t^2}{1-t^2}, t \neq 0$
 $\sin \theta = \frac 57$
 $\tan \theta = 100$
 $\sec \theta = \frac 52$
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is not possible?

  1. {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{1+t^2}{1-t^2}, t \neq 0{/tex}
  2. {tex}\sin \theta = \frac 57{/tex}
  3. {tex}\tan \theta = 100{/tex}
  4. {tex}\sec \theta = \frac 52{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The value of $\cos \theta$ lies between - 1 and 1.
But when t = 2 the value of $\cos \theta=\frac{1+t^2}{1-t^2}, t \neq 0$ is $\frac { 5 }{ -3 } $ which is more than 1, so it is not possible.

questions.question: If (1 + tan $\theta $) (1 + tan $\phi $) = 2, then $\theta $ + $\phi $ =
questions.question_raw: If (1 + tan {tex}\theta {/tex}) (1 + tan {tex}\phi {/tex}) = 2, then {tex}\theta {/tex} + {tex}\phi {/tex} =
questions.explanation:
$(1+\tan \theta)(1+\tan \phi)$ = 2 $\Rightarrow$ $1+\tan \theta+\tan \phi+\tan \theta \tan \phi$ = 2
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \theta+\tan \phi+\tan \theta \tan \phi$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \theta+\tan \phi$ = $1-\tan \theta \tan \phi$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\tan \theta+\tan \phi}{1-\tan \theta \tan \phi}$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan (\theta+\phi)$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\theta+\phi$ = 45^o [tan 45^o = 1]

questions.question: The set of values of x for which $\frac{\tan 3 x-\tan 2 x}{1+\tan 3 x \tan 2 x}$ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The set of values of x for which {tex}\frac{\tan 3 x-\tan 2 x}{1+\tan 3 x \tan 2 x}{/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
$\frac{\tan 3 x-\tan 2 x}{1+\tan 3 x \tan 2 x}$ = 1 $\Rightarrow$ tan x = 1
$\Rightarrow$ tan (x) = tan ($\frac{\pi}{4}$) [$\because$ tan (A - B) = $\frac{\tan A-\tan B}{1+\tan A \tan B}$]
$\Rightarrow$ x = $n \pi+\frac{\pi}{4}, n \in Z$

questions.question: The general value of $\theta $ satisfying the equation $2{\sin ^2}\theta - 3\sin \theta - 2 = 0$ is
questions.question_raw: The general value of {tex}\theta {/tex} satisfying the equation {tex}2{\sin ^2}\theta - 3\sin \theta - 2 = 0{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
$2 \sin ^2 \theta-3 \sin \theta-2=0$
$ \Rightarrow \left( 2\sin \theta +1 \right) \left( \sin \theta -2 \right) =0$
$ \Rightarrow \sin \theta =\frac { -1 }{ 2 } $ or $\sin \theta =2$
$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ [$\because$ sin $\theta$ = 2 > 1 so no solution]
$\Rightarrow \theta =n\pi +{ \left( -1 \right) }^{ n }\left( -\frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right) ,n\epsilon Z$
$\Rightarrow \theta =n\pi +{ \left( -1 \right) }^{ n+1 }\left( \frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right) ,n\epsilon Z$

questions.question: Number of solutions of the equation tan x + sec x = 2 cos x. lying in the interval $\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]$ is
questions.question_raw: Number of solutions of the equation tan x + sec x = 2 cos x. lying in the interval {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
$ \tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x $
$ \frac{\sin x+1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x $
$ 1+\sin x-2 \cos ^2 x=0 $
$ 1+\sin x-2+2 \sin ^2 x=0 $
$ 2 \sin ^2 x+\sin x-1=0 $
Since the equation is a quadratic equation in $\sin x$. So, there will be two solutions.

questions.question: In a triangle ABC, if a, b, c are in A. P., then tan $\frac A2,\tan \frac B2, \tan \frac C2$ are in
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC, if a, b, c are in A. P., then tan {tex}\frac A2,\tan \frac B2, \tan \frac C2{/tex} are in
questions.explanation:
If a, b, c are in A.P then we have $s-a, s-b, s-c$ are also in A . P
We have $\tan \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}}, \tan \frac{B}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-c)}{s(s-b)}}, \tan \frac{C}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{s(s-c)}}$
Multipling and dividing$\tan \frac{A}{2} \quad b y(s-a), \tan \frac{B}{2} \quad b y \quad(s-b) \text { and } \quad \tan \frac{C}{2}$ by $(s-b) a n d \quad \tan \frac{C}{2} \quad b y(s-c)$, we get
$\tan \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{s}}}{s-a}=\frac{K}{s-a}$
Similarly we can get $\tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{K}{s-b}$ and $\tan \frac{C}{2}=\frac{K}{s-c}$, where K=$\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{s}}$
We have ifa, b, c are in A.P then $\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}$ are in H.P
$\therefore \quad s-a, s-b, s-c$ are in $A \cdot P \cdot \Rightarrow \frac{K}{s-a}, \frac{K}{s-b}, \frac{K}{s-c}$ are in H.P
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \frac{A}{2}, \tan \frac{B}{2}, \tan \frac{C}{2}$ are in H.P

questions.question: In a $\Delta ABC,$ $\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}$ and the side a = 2, then area of the triangle is
questions.question_raw: In a {tex}\Delta ABC,{/tex} {tex}\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}{/tex} and the side a = 2, then area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}$.....(i)
Using Sine rule we have $\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=k$....(ii)
From(i) and (ii) we get $\frac{\cos A}{\sin A}=\frac{\cos B}{\sin B}=\frac{\cos C}{\sin C}$
$\Rightarrow $ cot A = cot B = cot C
In a triangle this is possible only when $A=B=C=60^{\circ}$ 
Hence the triangle is equilateral
So a = b = c = 2
$\therefore \text { Area }=\frac{1}{2} a b . \sin C=\frac{1}{2} 2.2 . \sin 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$

questions.question: In a triangle ABC , a = 4, b = 3 and A = ${60^ \circ }$ ; then c is a root of the equation
questions.question_raw: In a triangle ABC , a = 4, b = 3 and A = {tex}{60^ \circ }{/tex} ; then c is a root of the equation
questions.explanation: $Using\quad Cosine\quad Rule\quad we\quad have\quad cosA=\frac { { b }^{ 2 }+{ c }^{ 2 }-a^{ 2 } }{ 2bc } \\ \Rightarrow cos{ 60 }^{ \circ }=\frac { 1 }{ 2 } =\frac { 9+{ c }^{ 2 }-16 }{ 6c } \\ \Rightarrow { c }^{ 2 }-7=3c\\ \Rightarrow { c }^{ 2 }-3c-7=0\\ \Rightarrow c\quad is\quad the\quad root\quad of\quad the\quad equation\quad { x }^{ 2 }-3x-7=0$

questions.question: If x = $y \cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}=z \cos \frac{4 \pi}{3}$, then xy + yz + zx =
questions.question_raw: If x = {tex}y \cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}=z \cos \frac{4 \pi}{3}{/tex}, then xy + yz + zx =
questions.explanation:
x = $y \cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}$ = $z \cos \frac{4 \pi}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $y \cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $z \cos \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $-y \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ = $-z \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $-y \times \frac{1}{2}$ = $-z \times \frac{1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{-y}{2}=\frac{-z}{2}$ = k (say)
$\Rightarrow$ x = k, y = -2k, z = -2k
$\therefore$ xy + yz + zx = -2k^2 + 4k^2 - 2k^2 = 0

questions.question: The values of $\theta $ (0 < $\theta $ < 360^o) satisfying cosec $\theta $ + 2 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The values of {tex}\theta {/tex} (0 < {tex}\theta {/tex} < 360o) satisfying cosec {tex}\theta {/tex} + 2 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
cosec $\theta$ + 2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ cosec $\theta$ = -2
$\Rightarrow$ sin $\theta$ = $\frac{-1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ sin $\theta$ = $\sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\theta$ = $n \pi+(-1)^{n}\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right), n \epsilon Z$ 
Hence the values of $\theta$ betwwen 0 and 360 are 210^o, 330^o [when n = 1, n = 2]

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers such that z_1 and z_2 and z_1z_2 are real numbers, then
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers such that z1 and z2 and z1z2 are real numbers, then
questions.explanation:
Let z_1 = x_1 + iy_1 and z_2 = x_2 + iy_2 
Given z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers, so we have ${ y }_{ 1 }\neq 0,{ y }_{ 2 }\neq 0$ 
z_1 + z_2 = (x + iy_1) + (x_2 + iy_2) = (x_1 + x_2) + i(y_1 + y_2) 
z_1 + z_2 is a real number 
$\Rightarrow$ y_1 + y_2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ y_1 = -y_2 .....(i)
Now z_1z_2 = (x_1 + iy_1) $\cdot$ (x_2 + iy_2) = (x_1x_2 - y_1y_2) + i(x_1y_1 + x_1y_2)
z_1z_2 is a real number $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 + x_1y_2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 = -x_1y_2 ....(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii) we get $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 = x_1y_1 $\Rightarrow$ x_2 = x_1 
Hence we get z_1 = x_2 - iy_2 and z_2 = x_2 + iy_2 
So ${z_1} = \overline {{z_2}} $

questions.question: If $sin\alpha = {\rm{ sin}}\beta $ and ${\rm{cos}}\alpha = {\rm{ cos}}\beta $ , then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}sin\alpha = {\rm{ sin}}\beta {/tex} and {tex}{\rm{cos}}\alpha = {\rm{ cos}}\beta {/tex} , then
questions.explanation:
Given $\sin \alpha=\sin \beta$ and $\cos \alpha=\cos \beta$ 
Now $\sin \alpha \cdot \cos \beta$ = $\cos \alpha \cdot \sin \beta$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin \alpha \cdot \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \cdot \sin \beta$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin (\alpha-\beta)$ = 0

questions.question: If $sin\alpha + {\rm{ sin}}\beta $ = 0 = ${\rm{cos}}\alpha + {\rm{ cos}}\beta $, then ${\rm{cos 2}}\alpha + {\rm{ cos 2}}\beta $ =
questions.question_raw: If  {tex}sin\alpha + {\rm{ sin}}\beta {/tex} = 0 = {tex}{\rm{cos}}\alpha + {\rm{ cos}}\beta {/tex}, then {tex}{\rm{cos 2}}\alpha + {\rm{ cos 2}}\beta {/tex} =
questions.explanation:
$\cos 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \beta$ 
= $2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \alpha+2 \beta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{2 \alpha-2 \beta}{2}\right)$ 
= $2 \cos (\alpha+\beta) \cos (\alpha-\beta)$ ....(i)
But $\sin \alpha+\sin \beta$ = 0 and $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\sin \alpha= -\sin \beta$ and $\cos \alpha=-\cos \beta$ .....(ii)
$\therefore$ $\cos (\alpha-\beta)$ = $\cos \alpha \cdot \cos \beta+\sin \alpha \cdot \sin \beta$ = $-\cos ^{2} \beta-\sin ^{2} \beta$ = $-\left(\cos ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \beta\right)$ = -1 [using (ii)]
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos (\alpha-\beta)$ = -1 ....(iii)
Now from (i) and (iii) we have $\cos 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \beta$ = $-2 \cos (\alpha+\beta)$

questions.question: If $\cot \frac{A}{2}=\frac{b+c}{a}$ then $\Delta ABC$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\cot \frac{A}{2}=\frac{b+c}{a}{/tex} then {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given $\cot \frac{A}{2}=\frac{b+c}{a}$ 
Now using sine rule a = k sin A, b = k sin B, c = k sin C
$\Rightarrow$ $\cot \frac{A}{2}$ = $\frac{k \sin B+k \sin C}{k \sin A}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\cot \frac{A}{2}$ = $\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{B+C}{2}\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{A}{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\cot \frac{A}{2}$ = $\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{A}{2}}$ [$\because$ A + B + C = $\pi$ in a triangle]
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}$ = $\frac{\cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos \frac{A}{2}$ - $\cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ A + B - C = 0 or A - B + C = 180^o 
Solving we get C = 90^o or B = 90^o 
Hence we get $\Delta ABC$ is a right angled triangle.

questions.question: When the length of the shadow of a pole is equal to the height of the pole, then the elevation of source of light is
questions.question_raw: When the length of the shadow of a pole is equal to the height of the pole, then the elevation of source of light is
questions.explanation:
Consider a triangle ABC right angled at B. 
Let AB represent the pole then we have BC is it's shadow where angle C = $\theta$ is the elevation of the sun.
Then $tan \theta = \frac {AB} {BC} = 1,$ since the length of the shadow of pole = height of the pole
$ \Rightarrow tan\theta =tan{ 45 }^{ \circ }\\ \Rightarrow \theta ={ 45 }^{ \circ }$

questions.question: In a $\Delta ABC,B = \frac \pi 3$ and C $ = \frac \pi 4$. Let D divide BC internally in the ratio 1 : 3, then $\frac{\sin (\angle B A D)}{\sin (\angle C A D)}=$
questions.question_raw: In a {tex}\Delta ABC,B = \frac \pi 3{/tex} and C {tex} = \frac \pi 4{/tex}. Let D divide BC internally in the ratio 1 : 3, then {tex}\frac{\sin (\angle B A D)}{\sin (\angle C A D)}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
In $\triangle$ABC given $\angle$ B = $\frac { \pi }{ 3 } $, $\angle $C = $\frac { \pi }{ 4 }$
Since D divide BC in the ratio 1 : 3 we can take BD = x and DC = 3x
Let AD = y
In $\triangle $CAD, using Sine Rule we get,
$\frac { AD }{ sin\angle C } = \frac { DC }{ sin\angle CAD } $
$\Rightarrow \frac { y }{ sin\frac { \pi }{ 4 } } = \frac { 3x }{ sin\angle CAD }$
$\Rightarrow \frac { y }{ \frac { 1 }{ \sqrt { 2 } } } = \frac { 3x }{ sin\angle CAD }$
$\Rightarrow sin\angle CAD=\frac { 3x }{ \sqrt { 2 } y } $ ...(i)
In $\triangle$ADB, using Sine Rule we get,
$\frac { AD }{ sin\angle B } = \frac { BD }{ sin\angle BAD } $
$\Rightarrow \frac { y }{ sin\frac { \pi }{ 3 } } = \frac { x }{ sin\angle BAD }$
$\Rightarrow \frac { y }{ \frac { \sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } } = \frac { x }{ sin\angle BAD }$
$ \Rightarrow sin\angle BAD=\frac { \sqrt { 3 } x }{ 2y } $ ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac { sin\angle BAD }{ sin\angle CAD } =\frac { 3x }{ \sqrt { 2 } y } .\frac { 2y }{ \sqrt { 3 } x } =\frac { 1 }{ \sqrt { 6 } } $

questions.question: If the sides a, b, c of a triangle ABC are in A.P., then
questions.question_raw: If the sides a, b, c of a triangle ABC are in A.P., then
questions.explanation:
Since a, b, c are in AP, b = $\frac{(a+c)}{2}$ which gives a + c = 2b. adding b on both sides we get a + b + c - 2b = b. Hence 2s - 2b = b (since a + b + c = 2s) 
On simplification we have $\frac{2(s-b)}{b}$ = 1. Rewriting this equation. Using the semi sum formulae we have
$2 √(((s-b ))/b ) √(((s-b ))/b ) √(((s-c ) (s-a))/ac )$$= √(((s-c ) (s-a))/ac )$ 
$2√(((s-b) (s-c))/bc ) √(((s-a) (s-b))/ab ) $ $= √(((s-c) (s-a))/ca )$ which is the given answer.

questions.question: If the sides of a triangle ABC are given as 4, 5, 7 respectively then cot $\frac B2 \cot \frac C2$ equals
questions.question_raw: If the sides of a triangle ABC are given as 4, 5, 7 respectively then cot {tex}\frac B2 \cot \frac C2{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
Since the sides of a triangle ABC are 4, 5, 7 respectively we can take a = 4x, b = 5x, c = 7x.
Then, 2s = a + b + c = 16x $\Rightarrow$ s = 8x
Now $\tan \frac{B}{2} \cdot \tan \frac{C}{2}$ = $\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-c)}{s(s-b)}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{s(s-c)}}$ = $\frac{(s-a)}{s}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\tan \frac{B}{2} \cdot \tan \frac{C}{2}$ = $\frac{(s-a)}{s}=\frac{4 x}{8 x}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\cot \frac{B}{2} \cdot \cot \frac{C}{2}$ = 2

questions.question: If sin x - cos x =$\sqrt 2 $, then x = ( n is any integer)
questions.question_raw: If sin x - cos x ={tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex}, then x = ( n is any integer)
questions.explanation:
$\sin x-\cos x=\sqrt{2}$
Dividing throughout by $\sqrt{2}$, we get
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x=1$
$\Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi}{4} \sin x-\cos \frac{\pi}{4} \cos x=1$
$\Rightarrow \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-1$
$\Rightarrow \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\cos \pi$
$\Rightarrow x+\frac{\pi}{4}=2 n \pi \pm \pi$
$\Rightarrow x=2 n \pi \pm \pi-\frac{\pi}{4}, n \in Z$

questions.question: The general value of $\theta $ satisfying sin$\theta$ = -$\frac 12$ and tan $\theta $ = $\frac {1} {\sqrt 3 }$ is
questions.question_raw: The general value of {tex}\theta {/tex} satisfying sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = -{tex}\frac 12{/tex} and tan {tex}\theta {/tex} = {tex}\frac {1} {\sqrt 3 }{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
$\sin \theta=\frac{-1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \theta=n \pi+(-1)^{n}\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right), n \in Z$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{7 \pi}{6}, \frac{11 \pi}{6}$
$\tan \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \theta=n \pi+\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right), n \in Z$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}$
So principal value for both $\sin \theta$ and $\tan \theta$ is $\frac{7 \pi}{6}$
Hence the general solution is $2 n \pi+\frac{7 \pi}{6}, n \in I$

questions.question: The number of values of x satisfying the condition sin x + sin 5 x = sin 3x in the interval [0, $\pi $] is
questions.question_raw: The number of values of x satisfying the condition sin x + sin 5 x = sin 3x in the interval [0, {tex}\pi {/tex}] is
questions.explanation:
sin x = sin 5x = sin 3x [$\because$ sin C + sin D = $2 \sin \left(\frac{C+D}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{C-D}{2}\right)$]
$\Rightarrow$ 2 sin 3x cos (-2x) - sin 3x = 0
$\Rightarrow$ sin 3x [2 cos 2x - 1] = 0
$\Rightarrow$ sin 3x = 0 or, cos 2x = $\frac{1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ sin 3x = sin 0 or cos 2x = cos $\frac{\pi}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 3x = $n \pi+(-1)^{n} .0$ $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{n \pi}{3}$ or 2x = $2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3}$ $\Rightarrow$ x = $n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{6}, n \in Z$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $0, \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \pi$ or x = $\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 6 solutions in the interval [0, $\pi$].
