questions.question: n^2 + 3n is always divisible by which number, provided n is an integer?
questions.question_raw: n2 + 3n is always divisible by which number, provided n is an integer?
questions.explanation:
P(n) = n^2 + 3n
P(1) = 1 + 3
P(1) = 4
Let’s assume that P(k) is true and divisible by 4. Therefore, P(k) = k^2 + 3k can be written as 4c.
We need to check if P(k + 1) is divisible by 4
P(k+1) = (k + 1)^2 + 3(k + 1)
P(k+1) = k^2 + 1 + 2k + 3k + 3
P(k+1) = k^2 + 5k + 4
P(k+1) = (k^2 + 3k) + 2k + 4
P(k+1) = 4c + 2k + 4
P(k+1) = 4c + 2(k + 2)
Clearly the second part of the equation is not divisible by 4. However P(k) = 4c is divisible by 2 and
P(k + 1) is also divisible by 2. Therefore, 2 divides P(n).

questions.question: Let P(k) = 1 + 3 + 5 + …………….+ (2k - 1) = (3 + k^2). Then which of the following is true?

P(k) = 1 + 3 + ... + (2k - 1) = 3 + k^2 .....(i)
When k = 1, LHS of p(k) $\ne$ R.H.S of P(k)
So P(1) is not ture.
Now P(k + 1); 1 + 3 + 5 + .... + (2k - 1) + (2k + 1)
= 3 + (k + 1)^2 .....(ii)
Let P(k) is true 
$\therefore$ 1 + 3 + 5 + ..... + (2k - 1) = k^2 + 3
$\Rightarrow$ 1 + 3 + 5 + .... + (2k - 1)
= 3 + k^2 + 2k + 1 = (k + 1)^2 + 3
$\Rightarrow$ P(k + 1) true 
$\therefore$ P(k) $\Rightarrow$ P(k + 1)

For n = 2, we have
Sum of the terms in the second bracket = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 and, (n − 1)^3 + n^3 = (2 − 1)^3 + 2^3 = 1 + 8 = 9
For n = 3, we have
Sum of the terms in the third bracket = (5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9) = 35 and, (3 − 1)^3 + 3^3 = 8 + 27 = 35

questions.question: $\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+...$ to n terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+...{/tex} to n terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+\ldots .$ to n terms
= $\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(1-\frac{1}{16}\right)+\left(1-\frac{1}{64}\right)$ + .... to n terms
= n - $\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\frac{1}{4}\right.\left.+\frac{1}{4^{2}}+\ldots . . \text { to } \mathrm{n} \text { terms }\right) $ 
= $n-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1-\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{n}}{1-\frac{1}{4}}$ 
= $n-\frac{1}{3}\left(1-4^{-n}\right)$

questions.question: The complex numbers z = x + iy which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1$ lie on
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers z = x + iy which satisfy the equation {tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1{/tex} lie on

options.label: D
options.text: a circle passing through the origin

questions.explanation:
Given$\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1$
Putting z = x + iy , we get
$\left|\frac{x+i y-3 i}{x+i y+3 i}\right|=1$
$\Rightarrow\left| {{{x+iy - 3i} }} \right| =\left| {{{x+iy + 3i} }} \right|$
$\Rightarrow\left| {{{x+i(y - 3)} }} \right| =\left| {{{x+i(y + 3)} }} \right|$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y-3 } \right) }^{ 2 } } =\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y+3 } \right) }^{ 2 } } $ $\left[ \because \left| x+iy \right| =\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } \right]$
Squaring on both sides, we get
${ x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y-3 } \right) }^{ 2 }={ x }^{ 2 }+{ \left( { y+3 } \right) }^{ 2 }$
$ \Rightarrow { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 }-6y+9={ x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 }+6y+9$
$ \Rightarrow 12y=0$
$ \Rightarrow y=0$
Which means the X-axis.

questions.question: The inequality |z - 4| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.question_raw: The inequality |z - 4| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.explanation:
Given |z - 4| < |z - 2|
Putting z = x + iy, we get
|x + iy - 4| < |x + iy - 2|
$\Rightarrow$ |(x - 4) + iy| < |(x - 2) + iy| 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(x-4)^{2}+y^{2}}$ < $\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}$ [$\because$ |x + iy| = $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$]
Squaring on both sides we get
(x - 4)^2 + y^2 < (x - 2)^2 + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 16 - 8x + y^2 < x^2 + 4 - 4x + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 16 - 4 < 8x - 4x
$\Rightarrow$ 12 < 4x
$\Rightarrow$ x > 3

questions.question: If Z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}$, Z^3 then equals
questions.question_raw: If Z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}{/tex}, Z3 then equals
questions.explanation:
Given Z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}$ 
Z^3 = $\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^{3}$ [$\because$ (a + b)^3 = a^3 + b^3 + 3ab(a + b)]
$\Rightarrow$ Z^3 = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}^{3}+\frac{i^{3}}{2}^{3}+3 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{i}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ Z^3 = $\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{8}+\frac{i^{3}}{8}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3} i}{8}(\sqrt{3}+i)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ Z^3 = $\frac{3 \sqrt{3}-i+9 i-3 \sqrt{3}}{8}$ [$\because$ i^2 = -1]
$\Rightarrow$ Z^3 = $\frac{8 i}{8}$ = i

questions.question: If ${\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib;a,b \in R,$ then a and b are respectively :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib;a,b \in R,{/tex} then a and b are respectively :
questions.explanation:
First we will find the polar representation of the complex number $\sqrt { 3 } +i$
Let $\sqrt{3}+i=r(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta) \Rightarrow r \cos \theta=\sqrt{3} a n d \quad r \sin \theta=1$
$\therefore r^{2}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)=3+1=4 \Rightarrow r^{2}=4 \Rightarrow r=2$
Now $cos\theta =\frac { \sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } ,\quad sin\theta =\frac { 1 }{ 2 } $ , both are positive .
So Amplitude $=\theta =\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } $
Hence $\sqrt { 3 } +i=2\left( cos\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { \Pi }{ 6 } \right) =2{ e }^{ \frac { i\Pi }{ 6 } }$
Now,
$(\sqrt{3}+i)^{10}=\left(2 e^{\frac{i \pi}{6}}\right)^{10}=2^{10} e^{\frac{i 5 \pi}{3}}=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{5 \Pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{5 \Pi}{3}\right)\right)$
$=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(2 \Pi-\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(2 \Pi-\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)\right)=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right)\right)$
$=2^{10}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)-i \sin \left(\frac{\Pi}{3}\right)\right)=2^{10}\left(\frac{1}{2}-i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=2^{10}\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{3} i}{2}\right)=2^{9}(1-\sqrt{3} i)$
Hence ${\left( {\sqrt 3 + i} \right)^{10}} = a + ib$ $\Rightarrow { 2 }^{ 9 }\left( 1-\sqrt { 3 } i \right) =a+ib$
$\Rightarrow a={ 2 }^{ 9 }=512\quad and\quad b={ -2 }^{ 9 }\sqrt { 3 } =-512\sqrt { 3 } \\ $

questions.question: Let x, y $ \in$ R, then x + iy is a purely imaginary number if
questions.question_raw: Let x, y {tex} \in{/tex} R, then x + iy is a purely imaginary number if
questions.explanation:
A purely imaginary number is a complex number which has only an imaginary part (iy) 
But if y = 0 the complex number iy will become 0 which is real.
Hence the condition for a number to be purely imaginary is x = 0 and $ y\neq 0$

questions.question: Multiplicative inverse of the non zero complex number x + iy (x, y $\in$ R)
questions.question_raw: Multiplicative inverse of the non zero complex number x + iy (x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R)
questions.explanation: Multiplicative inverse of the complex number x + iy = $\frac{1}{x+i y}=\frac{1}{x+i y} \cdot \frac{x-i y}{x-i y}=\frac{x-i y}{x^2+y^2}$ [$\because$ i^2 = -1]

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers such that z_1 and z_2 and z_1z_2 are real numbers, then
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers such that z1 and z2 and z1z2 are real numbers, then
questions.explanation:
Let z_1 = x_1 + iy_1 and z_2 = x_2 + iy_2 
Given z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers, so we have ${ y }_{ 1 }\neq 0,{ y }_{ 2 }\neq 0$ 
z_1 + z_2 = (x + iy_1) + (x_2 + iy_2) = (x_1 + x_2) + i(y_1 + y_2) 
z_1 + z_2 is a real number 
$\Rightarrow$ y_1 + y_2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ y_1 = -y_2 .....(i)
Now z_1z_2 = (x_1 + iy_1) $\cdot$ (x_2 + iy_2) = (x_1x_2 - y_1y_2) + i(x_1y_1 + x_1y_2)
z_1z_2 is a real number $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 + x_1y_2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 = -x_1y_2 ....(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii) we get $\Rightarrow$ x_2y_1 = x_1y_1 $\Rightarrow$ x_2 = x_1 
Hence we get z_1 = x_2 - iy_2 and z_2 = x_2 + iy_2 
So ${z_1} = \overline {{z_2}} $

questions.question: The inequality |z - 6| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.question_raw: The inequality |z - 6| < |z - 2| represents the region given by
questions.explanation:
Given |z - 6| < |z - 2|
Putting z = x + iy, we get
|x + iy - 6| <|x + iy - 2|
$\Rightarrow$ |(x - 6) + iy| < |(x - 2) + iy|
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(x-6)^{2}+y^{2}}$ < $\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}$ [$\because$ |x + iy| = $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$]
Squaring on both sides we get
(x - 6)^2 + y^2 < (x - 2)^2 + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 36 - 12x + y^2 < x^2 + 4 - 4x + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 36 - 4 < 12x - 4x
$\Rightarrow$ 32 < 8x
$\Rightarrow$ x > 4

questions.question: z + $\overline {z} \ne 0$ if and only if
questions.question_raw: z + {tex}\overline {z} \ne 0{/tex} if and only if
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy then we have $\bar { Z } $ = x - iy
So Z + $\bar { Z }$ = 2x
Now z + $\overline {z} \ne 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 2x $\ne$ 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ne$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ Re(z) $\ne$ 0

questions.question: Distance of the representative of the number 1 + i from the origin (in Argand’s diagram) is
questions.question_raw: Distance of the representative of the number 1 + i from the origin (in Argand’s diagram) is
questions.explanation:
Let P(x, y) represent the complex number Z = x + iy in the complex plane(Argand plane) and let O(0, 0) be the origin
Then we have OP =$\sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } $ 
Here Z = 1 + i so that P(x, y) = P(1, 1)
Hence OP =$\sqrt { 1+1 } =\sqrt { 2}$

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a cube root of unity, then $\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right)...$ upto 2n factors is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega {/tex} is a cube root of unity, then {tex}\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right)...{/tex} upto 2n factors is
questions.explanation:
$\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^4}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^8}} \right)$ .... upto 2n factors
= $\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^3.\omega}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^3.\omega ^3.\omega^2}} \right)...$ upto 2n factors
= $\left( {1 + \omega } \right)\left( {1 + {\omega ^2}} \right)\left( {1 +{\omega}} \right)\left( {1 + {\omega^2}} \right)....$ upto 2n factors [ since ${ \omega }^{ 3 }=1$ ]
=$\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)...$ upto 2n factors $\left[ \because 1+\omega +{ \omega }^{ 2 }=0 \right] $ 
= $\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)\left(- {\omega ^2 } \right)\left( {- {\omega }} \right)...$upto 2n factors 
= $\left[\left(-\omega^{2}\right)(-\omega)\right]\left[\left(-\omega^{2}\right)(-\omega)\right]$ ... n factors
= $\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{3} \dots$ n factors [Since ${ \omega }^{ 3 }$ = 1]
= 1.1.1 .... n factos
= 1^n = 1

questions.question: If |z - 2| = |z - 6| then locus of z is given by :
questions.question_raw: If |z - 2| = |z - 6| then locus of z is given by :

options.label: A
options.text: a straight line parallel to x axis

options.label: C
options.text: a straight line parallel to y axis

correct_option_texts: a straight line parallel to y axis
questions.explanation:
|z - 2| = |z - 6|
$\Rightarrow$ |x - 2 + iy| = |x - 6 + iy|
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+y^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{(x-6)^{2}+y^{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 4 - 4x + y^2 = x^2 + 36 - 12x + y^2
$\Rightarrow$ 8x = 32
$\Rightarrow$ x = 4
We have any line of the form x = a constant is a line parallel to Y-axis.

questions.question: If k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers, then i^k + i^l + i^m + i^n is equal to :
questions.question_raw: If k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers, then ik + il + im + in is equal to :
questions.explanation:
Given k, l, m, n are four consecutive integers,
So we have l = k + 1, m = k + 2, n = k + 3
i^k + i^l + i^m + i^n
= i^k + i^k+1 + i^k+2 + i^k+3
= i^k (1 + i + i^2 + i^3) 
= i^k(1 + i - 1 - i)
= i^k.0 = 0 [$\because$ i^2 = -1 and i^3 = -i]

questions.question: $1 + {i^2} + {i^4} + {i^6} + {i^8} + .......$ up to 1001 terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}1 + {i^2} + {i^4} + {i^6} + {i^8} + .......{/tex} up to 1001 terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
$1+i^{2}+i^{4}+i^{6}+i^{8}+\ldots \ldots \ldots . \text { upto } 1001 \text { terms }$
$=\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}+\ldots \ldots . \text { upto } 1001 \quad \text { terms }$
$=\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1000}$
$=\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{0}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{1}\right]+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{3}\right]+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{998}+\left(i^{2}\right)^{999}\right]+\left[\left(i^{2}\right)^{1000}\right]$
$=[1-1]+[1-1]+\ldots \ldots \ldots+[1-1]+1$
=1

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a non-real cube root of unity and ${\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega ;a,b \in R,$ then a and b are respectively the numbers :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega {/tex} is a non-real cube root of unity and {tex}{\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega ;a,b \in R,{/tex} then a and b are respectively the numbers :
questions.explanation:
We have $(1+\omega)^{9}$ = $\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{9}$ = $(-1)^{9}\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{9}$ = $-\omega^{18}$ = $-\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{6}$ = (-1)^6 = -1 [$\because$ $\omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}$ = 0]
Given ${\left( {1 + \omega } \right)^9} = a + b\omega $ 
$\Rightarrow$ -1 = a + b$\omega$ 
$\Rightarrow$ -1 + 0.$\omega$ = a + b$\omega$
$\Rightarrow$ a = -1, b = 0

questions.question: If |z| = 1, then $\frac{z-1}{z+1}, z \neq-1$ is
questions.question_raw: If |z| = 1, then {tex}\frac{z-1}{z+1}, z \neq-1{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Given |z| = 1 $\Rightarrow \sqrt { { x }^{ 2 }+{ y }^{ 2 } } =1$ $\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 = 1
Now, $\frac{Z-1}{Z+1}=\frac{x+i y-1}{x+i y+1}=\frac{x-1+i y}{x+1+i y}$ 
= $\frac{x-1+i y}{x+1+i y} \cdot \frac{x+1-i y}{x+1-i y}$ = $\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)+y^{2}+i[y(x+1)-y(x-1)]}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ 
= $\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-1+2 i y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ = $\frac{1-1+2 i y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ [$\because$ x^2 + y^2 = 1] 
$\therefore$ $\frac{Z-1}{Z+1}$ = 0 + $\frac{2 y}{(x+1)^{2}+(y)^{2}} i$
Which is purely imaginary number.
Also when y = 0, the number will become zero

questions.question: The equation |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5| represents :
questions.question_raw: The equation |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5| represents :
questions.explanation:
Given |4z - 1| = 4|z - 5|
Let Z = x + iy 
$\Rightarrow$ |4(x + iy) - 1| = 4|x + iy - 5|
$\Rightarrow$ |(4x - 1) + 4iy| = 4(x - 5) + iy|
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(4 x-1)^{2}+(4 y)^{2}}$ = $4 \sqrt{(x-5)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ 
Squaring on both sides we get
(4x - 1)^2 + (4y)^2 = 16[(x - 5)^2 + (y)^2]
$\Rightarrow$ 16x^2 + 1 - 8x + 16y^2 = 16[x^2 + 25 - 10x + y^2]
$\Rightarrow$ 16x^2 + 1 - 8x + 16y^2 - 16x^2 - 400 + 160x - 16y^2 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 152x - 399 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{399}{152}$
We have x = a constant is a straight line parallel to Y-axis.

Now 
$(-1+\sqrt{3} i)^{n}+(-1-\sqrt{3} i)^{n}$ = $(2 \omega)^{n}+\left(2 \omega^{2}\right)^{n}$ = $2^{n}\left[(\omega)^{n}+\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{n}\right]$

questions.question: The locus of the equation $\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|$ = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the equation {tex}\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|{/tex} = 1 is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Now $\left|\frac{3 z-1}{z-1}\right|$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ |3z - 1| = |z - 1|
$\Rightarrow$ |(3 (x + iy) - 1| = |(x + iy) - 1|
$\Rightarrow$ |(3x - 1) + 3iy| = |(x - y) + iy| 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(3 x-1)^{2}+(3 y)^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (3x - 1)^2 + (3y)^2 = (x - 1)^2 + (y)^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 9x^2 + 1 - 6x + 9y^2 = x^2 + 1 - 2x + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 8x^2 + 8y^2 - 4x = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 - $\frac{1}{2}$x = 0
We have this is a circle with centre ($\frac{1}{4}$, 0) and radius = $\frac{1}{4}$.

questions.question: $\sqrt { - 8} \sqrt { - 18} \sqrt { - 4} $ is

questions.question: If $\alpha = \frac z {\overline z}$, then ${\text{|}}\alpha {\text{|}}$ is equal to:

questions.question: The smallest positive integer n for which $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n=$ 1 is :
questions.question_raw: The smallest positive integer n for which {tex}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n={/tex} 1 is :
questions.explanation:
We have $\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } =\frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \times \frac { 1+i }{ 1+i } =\frac { { \left( 1+i \right) }^{ 2 } }{ 1+1 } $$=\frac { 1+2i-1 }{ 2 } =\frac { 2i }{ 2 } =i$
Therefore $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^n=i^n$
By inspection we have the smallest positive integer such that ${{i} ^n}=1$ is n=4.

questions.question: If $\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^5$, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{i}{2}\right)^5{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
$\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}=r(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta) \Rightarrow r \cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, $ r \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore r^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)=\frac{3+1}{4}=1 $ $\Rightarrow r^2=1 \Rightarrow r=1$
$\cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore$ Amplitude = $\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}$
$\frac { \sqrt { 3 } +i }{ 2 } =1\left( cos\frac { \pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { \pi }{ 6 } \right) ={ e }^{ \frac { i\pi }{ 6 } }$ ...(i)
Since $\frac { \sqrt { 3 } -i }{ 2 } $ lies in the fourth quadrant, the amplitude $=-\theta =-\frac { \pi }{ 6 } $
$\frac { \sqrt { 3 } -i }{ 2 } =1\left( cos\frac { -\pi }{ 6 } +isin\frac { -\pi }{ 6 } \right) ={ e }^{ \frac { -i\pi }{ 6 } }$ ...(i)
$z=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}-i}{2}\right)^5$
$\Rightarrow z=\left(e^{\frac{ { i\pi }}{6}}\right)^5+\left(e^{\frac{-i \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}\right)^5=e^{\frac{i 5 \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}+e^{-\frac{i 5 \mathrm{\pi}}{6}}$ = $=\cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{-5 \pi}{6}\right)$$+i \sin \left(\frac{-5 \pi}{6}\right)=2 \cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)$
Which is purely real [imaginary part of z = 0]

questions.question: If the cube roots of unity are 1, $\omega ,{\omega ^2},$ then roots of the equation (x - 1)^3 + 8 = 0 are :
questions.question_raw: If the cube roots of unity are 1, {tex}\omega ,{\omega ^2},{/tex} then roots of the equation (x - 1)3 + 8 = 0 are :
questions.explanation:
(x - 1)^3 + 8 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (x - 1)^3 = -8
$\Rightarrow$ (x - 1)^3 = (-2)^3
$\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{x-1}{-2}\right)^{3}$ = 1
Let $\frac { x-1 }{ -2 } $ = m (say)
So we get m^3 = 1 $\Rightarrow m=1, \omega ,{\omega ^2}$ [cube roots of unity]
Now m = 1 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-1}{-2}$ = 1 $\Rightarrow$ x - 1 = -2 $\Rightarrow$ x = -1
m = $\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-1}{-2}=\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ x - 1 = -2$\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ x = 1 - 2$\omega$ 
Also m = $\omega^2$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-1}{-2}=\omega^{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ x -1 = $-2 \omega^{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ x = 1 - $2 \omega^{2}$ 
Hence the roots of the given equation are $ - 1,1 - 2\omega ,1 - 2{\omega ^2}$

questions.question: The value of arg (x) when x < 0 is:
questions.question_raw: The value of arg (x) when x < 0 is:
questions.explanation:
Given x $\in $ R and x $<$ 0, therefore the number is $-x+0i$ and this will be a point inthe second quadrant as x is negative 
We have arg($-x+0i$)=$\theta =$ $\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ -1 } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ 0 } =0$
We have in the second quadrant the principal value of argument= $\Pi -\theta =\Pi -0 =\Pi$
Hence Arg. ( x ) , x $\in $ R and x $<$ 0 is $\pi $

questions.question: The number of solutions of the equation Im (z^2) = 0, |z| = 2 is
questions.question_raw: The number of solutions of the equation Im (z2) = 0, |z| = 2 is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy, then we have $\bar { z }$ = x - iy 
x^2 = (x + iy)^2 = x^2 - y^2 + 2xyi 
$\therefore$ im(z^2) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 2xy = 0 .....(i)
|z| = 2 $\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ = 2 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 = 4 .....(ii)
(x^2 - y^2)^2 = (x^2 + y^2)^2 - 4x^2y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ (x^2 - y^2)^2 = 16
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 - y^2 = $\pm$ 4 .....(iii)
When x^2 - y^2 = 4 from (ii) and (iii) we get 2x^2 = 8 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 = 4 $\Rightarrow$ x = $\pm$ 2
Now from (i) we get y = 0
Hence z = $\pm 2$ 
Now when x^2 - y^2 = -4 from (ii) and (iii) we get 2y^2 = 8 $\Rightarrow$ y^2 = 4 $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm$ 2
Now from (i) we get x = 0
Hence z = $\pm 2i$ 
Therefore the solutions of the equation Im (z^2) = 0, |z| = 2 are z = $\pm 2i$ and z = $\pm 2$ and so number of solutions = 4.

questions.question: Amp. (z - 2 - 3i) = $\frac{\pi}{4}$ then locus of z is
questions.question_raw: Amp. (z - 2 - 3i) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then locus of z is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Then z - 2 - 3i = (x + iy) - 2 - 3i = (x - 2) + i(y - 3)
Let $\theta $ be the amplitude of z.
Then Amp (z - 2 - 3i) = $\frac{\pi}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ tan^-1$\left(\frac{y-3}{x-2}\right)$ = $\frac{\Pi}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{y-3}{x-2}=\tan \frac{\Pi}{4}$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ y - 3 = x - 2
$\Rightarrow$ x - y + 1 = 0

questions.question: If z_1 and z_2 are non-real complex numbers such that |z_1| = |z_2| and Amp. z_1 + Amp. z_2 = $\pi$, then z_1 =
questions.question_raw: If z1 and z2 are non-real complex numbers such that |z1| = |z2| and  Amp. z1 + Amp. z2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}, then z1 =
questions.explanation:
Let z_1 = $r_{1}\left(\cos \theta_{1}+i \sin \theta_{1}\right)$ and z_2 = $r_{2}\left(\cos \theta_{2}+i \sin \theta_{2}\right)$, where r_1 = |z_1|, r_2 = |z_2| and $\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}$ are the amplitudef of the complex numbers.
Given |z_1| = |z_2| and$Amp.{z_{1}} + Amp.{z_2} = \pi $ $\Rightarrow$ r_1 = r_2 and $ { \theta }_{ 1 }+{ \theta }_{ 2 }=\pi $ 
Now z_1 = ${ r }_{ 1 }\left( cos{ \theta }_{ 1 }+isin{ \theta }_{ 1 } \right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ z_1 = $r_{2}\left[\cos \left(\pi-\theta_{2}\right)+i \sin \left(\pi-\theta_{2}\right)\right]$ 
$\Rightarrow$ z_1 = $r_{2}\left(-\cos \theta_{2}+i \sin \theta_{2}\right)$ = $-\left[r_{2}\left(\cos \theta_{2}-i \sin \theta_{2}\right)\right]$ = $-\overline{z_{2}}$

questions.question: The complex numbers sinx + i cos2x and cosx – i sin2x are conjugate to each other, for
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers sinx + i cos2x and cosx – i sin2x are conjugate to each other, for
questions.explanation:
Let
z_1 = sinx + icos2x and z_2 = cosx–isin2x
$\Rightarrow$$\overline z$_1 = sinx − icos2x, $\overline z$_2 = cosx + isin2x
Given that z_1 = $\overline z$_2 and z_2 = $\overline z$_1
$\Rightarrow$ sinx + icos2x = cosx + isin2x and cosx − isin2x = sinx − icos2x
$\Rightarrow$ sinx = cosx......(i) and cos2x = sin2x........(ii)
Now cos2x = sin2x $\Rightarrow$ 2cos2x − 1 = 2sinx cosx
$\Rightarrow$ 2sin^2x − 2sin^2x = 1using(i)
$\Rightarrow$ 0 = 1
Which is not possible.
Hence we can say there is no solution for the system of equations

questions.question: The value of ${\left( { - 1 + \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2}$is
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}{\left( { - 1 + \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt { - 3} } \right)^2}{/tex}is
questions.explanation:
We have $\sqrt { -3 } =\sqrt { -1.3 } =\sqrt { -1 } \sqrt { 3 } = \sqrt { 3 }i$
$\therefore-1+\sqrt{-3}=-1+i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega \quad \text { and } \quad-1-\sqrt{-3}=-1-i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega^{2}$
$\left[\because \omega=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \omega^{2}=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$
$(-1+\sqrt{-3})^{2}+(-1-\sqrt{-3})^{2}$ $=(2 \omega)^{2}+\left(2 \omega^{2}\right)^{2}=4 \omega^{2}+4 \omega^{4}=4\left(\omega^{2}+\omega^{3} \cdot \omega\right)$
=$4\left(\omega^{2}+\omega\right)=4 \times-1=-4$ $\left[\because \omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\right]$

questions.question: If $\alpha $ is a complex number such that ${\alpha ^2} + \alpha + 1 = 0$ then ${\alpha ^{31}}$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha {/tex} is a complex number such that {tex}{\alpha ^2} + \alpha + 1 = 0{/tex} then {tex}{\alpha ^{31}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
${\alpha ^2} + \alpha + 1 = 0$ $\Rightarrow \alpha =\frac { -1\pm \sqrt { 1-4 } }{ 2 } =\frac { -1\pm i\sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } =\omega ,{ \omega }^{ 2 }$ 
When $\alpha=\omega \Rightarrow \alpha^{31}=\omega^{31}$ = $\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{10} \cdot \omega=1 \cdot \omega=\omega=\alpha$ 
Also when $\alpha=\omega^{2} \Rightarrow \alpha^{31}=\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{31}$ = $\omega^{62}=\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{20} \cdot \omega^{2}=1 . \omega^{2}=\omega^{2}=\alpha$ 
Hence ${\alpha ^{31}}$ = $\alpha $

questions.question: If n is any integer, then i^n is

i^0 = 1, i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = i^2.i = -i
Now we can see for n = 0, 4, 8....... the value of i^n = 1
For n = 1, 5, 9....... the value of i^n = i

questions.question: The points z = x + iy which satisfy the equation |z| = 1 lie on

options.label: C
options.text: the circle whose centre is origin and radius = 1

correct_option_texts: the circle whose centre is origin and radius = 1
questions.explanation:
z = x + iy
Now |z| = 1 $\Rightarrow$ |x + iy| = 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ = 1 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 = 1
Which is the equation of a circle whose centre is origin and radius = 1

questions.question: The equation z^2 = $ \bar z$ has
questions.question_raw: The equation z2 = {tex} \bar z{/tex} has
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
z^2 = $\bar z$ $\Rightarrow$ (x + iy)^2 = x - iy 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 - y^2 + 2xyi = x - iy
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 - y^2 = x .....(i) and 2xy = -y .....(ii)
From (ii) we get x = $\frac { -1 }{ 2 } $ 
Now from (i) we have y^2 = $\frac { 1 }{ 4 } +\frac { 1 }{ 2 } =\frac { 3 }{ 4 } \Rightarrow \pm \frac { \sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } $ 
Hence z = $\frac { -1 }{ 2 } \pm i\frac { \sqrt { 3 } }{ 2 } $

questions.question: The points of the complex plane given by the condition arg. (z) = (2n + 1) $\pi $, n $\in $ I lie on
questions.question_raw: The points of the complex plane given by the condition arg. (z) = (2n + 1) {tex}\pi {/tex}, n {tex}\in {/tex} I lie on

options.label: A
options.text: the negative real semi axis z = x, x < 0

options.label: B
options.text: the positive real semi axis z = x, x > 0

options.label: C
options.text: the imaginary semi axis z = iy, y > 0

options.label: D
options.text: the imaginary semi axis z = iy, y < 0

correct_option_texts: the negative real semi axis z = x, x < 0
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy then value of $\theta$ which satisfies the equation $tan\theta =\frac { y }{ x } $ is called an argument or amplitude.
Then Arg (z)= $\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left( \frac { y }{ x } \right) } $ 
Now arg. (z) = (2n + 1) $\pi $ $\Rightarrow\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left( \frac { y }{ x } \right) } = ( 2n + 1 ) \pi$ 
$\Rightarrow$ tan (2n + 1)$\pi $ = $\frac{y}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\sin (2 n+1) \pi}{\cos (2 n+1) \pi}$ = $\frac{y}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{0}{(-1)^{2 n+1}}$ = $\frac{y}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{0}{-1}$ = $\frac{y}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ -y = 0
Hence the points lie on the negative real semi-axis z = x, x < 0

questions.question: If z = x + yi ; x ,y $\in $ R, then locus of the equation $\bar bz + b\overline {z} = c$, where c $\in $ R and b $\in $ C, b $ \ne $ 0 are fixed, is
questions.question_raw: If z = x + yi ; x ,y {tex}\in {/tex} R, then locus of the equation {tex}\bar bz + b\overline {z} = c{/tex}, where c {tex}\in {/tex} R and b {tex}\in {/tex} C, b {tex} \ne {/tex} 0 are fixed, is
questions.explanation:
Let z_1 = $\bar{b} z$ then $\overline{z_{1}}=b \bar{z}$ 
Now $\bar{b} z+b \bar{z}$ = c $\Rightarrow$ $z_{1}+\overline{z_{1}}$ = c
$\Rightarrow$ 2Rez_1 = c $\Rightarrow$ Rez_1 = $\frac{c}{2}$ 
Which is a straight line.

questions.question: The complex number z which satisfies the condition $\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$, lies on
questions.question_raw: The complex number z which satisfies the condition {tex}\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1{/tex}, lies on
questions.explanation:
$\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$
$\Rightarrow|i+z|=|i-z|$
$\Rightarrow|i+x+i y|=|i-(x+i y)|$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(1+y)^2+x^2}=\sqrt{-(x)^2+(1-y)^2}$
$\Rightarrow(1+y)^2+x^2=-(x)^2+(1-y)^2$
$\Rightarrow y^2+2 y+1+x^2=x^2+y^2-2 y+1$
$\Rightarrow$ 4y = 0
$\Rightarrow$ y = 0
which is the X-axis.

questions.question: (1 + i)^2n + (1 - i)^2n, n $ \in$ N, is
questions.question_raw: (1 + i)2n + (1 - i)2n, n {tex} \in{/tex} N, is
questions.explanation:
(1 + i)^2n + (1 - i)^2n = [(1 + i)^2]^n + [(1 - i)^2]^n = [1 + 2i - 1]^n + [1 - 2i - 1]^n = (2i)^n + (-2i)^n = 2^n[(i)n + (-i)n]
When n is an odd natural number say n = 2k + 1, (-i)^n = -(i)^2k+1 so that (i)^n + (-i)^n = (i)^2k+1 - (i)^2k+1 = 0
When n is an even natural number say n = 2k, (-i)^n = (-i)^2k = (i)^2k so that, (i)^n + (-i)^n = (i)^2k + (i)^2k = 2(i^2)^k = 2(-1)^k, k $\in$ N
Hence (1 + i)^2n + (1 - i)^2n is always a purely real number.

questions.question: $A m p \cdot\left(\sin \frac{6 \pi}{5}+i\left(1+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{5}\right)\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}A m p \cdot\left(\sin \frac{6 \pi}{5}+i\left(1+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{5}\right)\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have for a complex number x + iy the argument or amplitude is a value of $\theta $ satisfying $tan\theta =\frac { y }{ x } $
z = x + it = $\sin \frac{6 \pi}{5}+i\left(1+\cos \frac{6 \pi}{5}\right)$
$\therefore \tan \theta=\frac{y}{x}$ $\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{1+\cos \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{5}}{\sin \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{5}}=\frac{2 \cos ^2 \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}}{2 \sin \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10} \cos \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}}=\tan \frac{6 \mathrm{\pi}}{10}$ $\left[\because 1+\cos \theta=2 \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}, \sin \theta=2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{6 \pi}{10}$

questions.question: If z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}$, then z^69 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If z = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}{/tex}, then z69 is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given z = $\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}$ 
iz = $i\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{2}\right]$ = $\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}=\omega$ 
$\Rightarrow$ z = $\frac{\omega}{i}$ 
Then z^69 = $\left[\frac{\omega}{i}\right]^{69}$ = $\frac{\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{23}}{\left(i^{2}\right)^{34} \cdot i}$ = $\frac{1}{(-1)^{34} . i}=\frac{1}{i}$ = -i [$\because$ $\omega^3$ = 1, i^2 = -1]

questions.question: If $\omega $ is a cube root of unity, then the linear factors of x^3 + y^3 in complex numbers are
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\omega {/tex} is a cube root of unity, then the linear factors of x3 + y3 in complex numbers are
questions.explanation:
We have x^3 + y^3 = (x + y)(x^2 - xy + y^2)
Now consider x^2 - yx + y^2 = 0 which is a quadratic equation in x with a = 1, b = -y and c = y^2
Hence the roots are 
x = $\frac{-(-y) \pm \sqrt{(-y)^{2}-4 \cdot 1 \cdot y^{2}}}{2 \cdot 1}$ = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{-3 y^{2}}}{2}$ = $\frac{y \pm i y \sqrt{3}}{2}$ = $\frac{y(1 \pm i \sqrt{3})}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = y$\omega$ or y$\omega^2$ 
Hence, x^3 + y^3 = (x + y)(x^2 - xy + y^2) = (x + y)(x + y$\omega$)(x + y$\omega^2$)

questions.question: $\left( {z + 1} \right)\left( {\overline z + 1} \right)$ can be expressed as
questions.question_raw: {tex}\left( {z + 1} \right)\left( {\overline z + 1} \right){/tex} can be expressed as
questions.explanation:
We have $z\bar { z } ={ \left| z \right| ^{ 2 } }$ 
Now $\left( { z+1 } \right) \left( { \overline { z } +1 } \right) =\left( { z+1 } \right) \left( \overline { z+1 } \right) $ = |z + 1|^2

questions.question: If $\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $ are non-real cube roots of unity then $\alpha \beta $ + ${\alpha ^5} + {\beta ^5}$ equals
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta {/tex} are non-real cube roots of unity then {tex}\alpha \beta {/tex} + {tex}{\alpha ^5} + {\beta ^5}{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
If $\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $ are non-real cube roots of unity then we have either $\alpha=\omega, \beta=\omega^{2}$ or $\alpha=\omega^{2}, \beta=\omega$ 
$\alpha=\omega, \beta=\omega^{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha^{5}+\beta^{5}+\alpha \cdot \beta$ = $\omega^{5}+\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{5}+\omega \cdot \omega^{2}$ = $\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{2}+\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{3} \cdot \omega^{1}+\omega^{3}$ = $1 . \omega^{2}+1 . \omega+1$ = 0
$\alpha=\omega^{2}, \beta=\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha^{5}+\beta^{5}+\alpha . \beta$ = $\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{5}+\omega^{5}+\omega . \omega^{2}$ = $\left(\omega^{3}\right)^{3} \cdot \omega^{1}+\omega^{3} \cdot \omega^{2}+\omega^{3}$ = $1 . \omega+1 . \omega^{2}+1$ = 0 [$\because$ $\omega^{3}=1,1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0$ ]

questions.question: The complex numbers z = x + iy; x , y $\in $ R which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1$ lies on
questions.question_raw: The complex numbers z = x + iy; x , y {tex}\in {/tex} R which satisfy the equation {tex}\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1{/tex} lies on

options.label: A
options.text: the line parallel to y axis

questions.explanation:
Let z=x+iy
Now $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3}\right|=1$
$\Rightarrow|z-3 i|=|z+3|$
$\Rightarrow|(x+i y)-3 i|=|x+i y+3|$
$\Rightarrow|x+i(y-3)|=|(x+3)+i y|$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(y-3)^{2}+x^{2}}=\sqrt{(x+3)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow(y-3)^{2}+x^{2}=(x+3)^{2}+(y)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow y^{2}-6 y+9+x^{2}=x^{2}+6 x+9+y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 6 x+6 y=0$
$\Rightarrow x+y=0$

questions.question: If z_1 = 2+3i and z_2 = 5+2i, then find z_1-z_2.
questions.question_raw: If z1 = 2+3i and z2 = 5+2i, then find z1-z2.
questions.explanation:
In subtracting one complex number from other, difference of corresponding parts of two complex numbers is calculated. So,
z_1-z_2 will be:
= (2-5) + (3-2) i
= -3+1i.

questions.question: If z = $\bar z$, then
questions.question_raw: If z = {tex}\bar z{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Now z = $\bar{z} \Rightarrow$ x + iy = x - iy $\Rightarrow$ 2iy = 0 $\Rightarrow$ y = 0
Which means z is purely real.

questions.question: The least value of n for which ${ \left( \frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \right) }^{ n }$ is a positive integer is
questions.question_raw: The least value of n for which {tex}{ \left( \frac { 1+i }{ 1-i } \right) }^{ n }{/tex} is a positive integer is
questions.explanation:
$\frac{1+i}{1-i}$ = $\frac{1+i}{1-i} \cdot \frac{1+i}{1+i}$ = $\frac{(1+i)^{2}}{1+1}=\frac{1-1+2 i}{1+1}=\frac{2 i}{2}$ = i
$\therefore$ $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{n}$ = i^n 
We have i^4 = 1 which is positive.
Hence the least value of n is 4.

questions.question: If (x + iy)(p + iq) = (x^2 + y^2)i, then
questions.question_raw: If (x + iy)(p + iq) = (x2 + y2)i, then
questions.explanation:
(x + iy)(p + iq) = (x^2 + y^2)i 
$\Rightarrow$ (xp - yq) + i(xq + yp) = (x^2 + y^2)i 
$\Rightarrow$ xp - yp = 0 and xq = + yp = x^2 + y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ p = $\frac{y q}{x}$ ....(i) and xq + yp = x^2 + y^2 ...(ii)
Substituting (i) in (ii), we get 
xq + $y\left(\frac{y q}{x}\right)$ = x^2 + y^2 $\Rightarrow$ x^2q + y^2q = x(x^2 + y^2)
$\Rightarrow$ q(x^2 + y^2) = x(x^2 + y^2) $\Rightarrow$ q = x
Now from (i), we get p = y

questions.question: If z = x + yi and w = $\frac{1-i z}{z-i}$, then |w| = 1 implies that, in the complex plane
questions.question_raw: If z = x + yi and w = {tex}\frac{1-i z}{z-i}{/tex}, then |w| = 1 implies that, in the complex plane

options.label: A
options.text: z lies on the imaginary axis

options.label: B
options.text: z lies on the unit circle

options.label: C
options.text: z lies on the unit square

options.label: D
options.text: z lies on the real axis

correct_option_texts: z lies on the real axis
questions.explanation:
$\left| { { w } } \right| =1\Rightarrow \left| \frac { 1-iz }{ z-i } \right| =1$
$\Rightarrow \left| { { 1-iz } } \right| =\left| { { z-i } } \right|$
$\Rightarrow \left| { { 1-i\left( x+iy \right) } } \right| =\left| { \left( x+iy \right) -i } \right|$
$\Rightarrow \left| { { \left( 1+y \right) -ix } } \right| =\left| { { x+i\left( y-1 \right) } } \right|$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt { { \left( 1+y \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 } } =\sqrt { { \left( y-1 \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 } }$
$\Rightarrow { \left( 1+y \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }={ \left( y-1 \right) }^{ 2 }+{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }$
$ \Rightarrow 1+{ y }^{ 2 }+2y+{ x }^{ 2 }=1+{ y }^{ 2 }-2y+{ x }^{ 2 }$
$\Rightarrow y=0$
Which is the real axis.

questions.question: If p^2 + p + 1 = 0, then, the value of p^3n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If p2 + p + 1 = 0, then, the value of p3n is equal to
questions.explanation:
p^2 + p + 1 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ p = $\frac{-(1) \pm \sqrt{1^{2}-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}}{2}$ = $\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{3} i}{2}=\omega, \omega^{2}$ 
Now p = $\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ p^3n = $(\omega^3)^n$ = 1^n = 1 or -1, depending n is even or odd
Also p = $\omega^2$ $\Rightarrow$ p^3n = $(\omega^2)^{3n}$ = $(\omega^3)^{2n}$ = 1^2n = 1
Hence p = 1, -1

questions.question: The solution of the equation |z| = z + 1 + 2i is
questions.question_raw: The solution of the equation |z| = z + 1 + 2i is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Now 
|z| = z + 1 + 2i
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow$ x + 1 + i(y + 2)
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ = x + 1 and y + 2 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ y = -2 and x^2 + 4 = (x + 1)^2 
$\Rightarrow$ y = -2 and x = $\frac{3}{2}$ 
Hence z = $\frac{3}{2}$ - 2i

questions.question: a + ib $ \leq $ c + id is meaningful only when
questions.question_raw: a + ib {tex} \leq {/tex} c + id is meaningful only when
questions.explanation:
We have in the complex numbers system it is meaningless to say one is smaller or bigger than the other since the order relation does not exist in complex numbers.
So a + ib $ \leq$ c + id will be meaningful only when these numbers are real which means their imaginary parts are zero.
$\Rightarrow$ b = 0 and d = 0 $\Rightarrow$ b^2 + d^2 = 0

questions.question: Square roots of i are
questions.question_raw: Square roots of i are
questions.explanation:
Let $\sqrt { i }$ = x + iy
Squaring both sides, we get
i = (x + iy)^2 = x^2 - y^2 + 2xyi 
Subtracting (i) from (iii), we get
2y^2 = 1 $\Rightarrow$ y^2 = $\frac{1}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ 
Comparing real and imaginary parts, we get x^2 - y^2 = 0 ....(i) and 2xy = 1
We have (x^2 - y^2)^2 = (x^2 + y^2)^2 - 4x^2y^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 0 = (x^2 + y^2)^2 - (2xy)^2 = (x^2 + y^2)^2 - 1
$\Rightarrow$ (x^2 + y^2)^2 = 1
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 = 1 .....(iii)
Adding (i) and (iii) we get 
2x^2 = 1 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 = $\frac{1}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ x = $\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ 
Hence $\sqrt { i }$ = x + iy = $\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1+i)$

questions.question: The value of $\frac{1}{1+\omega}+\frac{1}{1+\omega^2}$ =
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}\frac{1}{1+\omega}+\frac{1}{1+\omega^2}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
$1,\omega ,{ \omega }^{ 2 }$ are the three cube roots of unity and we have $1+\omega +{ \omega }^{ 2 }=0 , { \omega }^{ 3 }=1$
${1 \over {1 + \omega }} + {1 \over {1 + {\omega ^2}}} = $$ {1 \over { - {\omega ^2}}}+{1 \over {-\omega }} = $ $-1\left(\frac{1}{\omega^{2}}+\frac{1}{\omega}\right)=-1\left(\frac{\omega+\omega^{2}}{\omega^{2} \cdot \omega}\right)$ = $-1\left(\frac{-1}{\omega^{3}}\right)=\frac{1}{\omega^{3}}=1$ [$\because$ $\omega+\omega^{2}=-1, \omega^{3}=1$]

questions.question: The values of x and y $\in $ R for which the numbers -3 + x^2yi, x^2 + y + 4i are conjugates of each other are
questions.question_raw: The values of x and y {tex}\in {/tex} R for which the numbers -3 + x2yi, x2 + y + 4i are conjugates of each other are
questions.explanation:
Let z_1 = -3 + x^2yi and z_2 = x^2 + y + 4i $\Rightarrow$ $\bar z_{1}$ = -3 - x^2yi, $\bar z_{2}$ = x^2 + y - 4i
Given the numbers are conjugates of each other $\overline{z_{1}}=z_{2}$ and $\overline{z_{2}}=z_{1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ -3 - x^2yi = x^2 + y + 4i and x^2 + y - 4i = -3 + x^2yi
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y = -3 and x^2y = -4
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-4}{y}$ + y = -3
$\Rightarrow$ y^2 + 3y - 4 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ (y + 4)(y - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ y = 1, -4
When y = 1 we get x^2 = 4 $\Rightarrow$ x = $\pm \sqrt{-4}=\pm 2 i$, which is not possible since given x is a real number.
When y = $-4$ we get $x^{ 2 }=1\Rightarrow x=\pm \sqrt { 1 } =\pm 1 $ 
Hence the possible values of x and y are $\pm 1,-4$

questions.question: If $x = {\omega ^2} - \omega - 3,\omega $ being a non real cube root of unity, then the value of x^4 + 6x^3 + 10x^2 - 12x - 19 is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}x = {\omega ^2} - \omega - 3,\omega {/tex} being a non real cube root of unity, then the value of x4 + 6x3 + 10x2 - 12x - 19 is
questions.explanation:
x = $\omega^{2}-\omega-3$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x + 3 $=\omega^{2}-\omega$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 6x + 9 = $\omega^{4}-2 \omega^{3}+\omega^{2}$ = $\omega-2+\omega^{2}$ = -3
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 6x + 12 = 0
Now x^4 + 6x^3 + 10x^2 - 12x - 19 = (x^2 - 2)(x^2 + 6x + 12) + 5 = (x^2 - 2) $\times$ 0 + 5 = 5

questions.question: The region of Argand’s plane represented by the inequality |1 - z| $\leq $ |1 + z| is
questions.question_raw: The region of Argand’s plane represented by the inequality |1 - z| {tex}\leq {/tex} |1 + z| is
questions.explanation:
Let Z = x + iy
Given |1 - z| $ \leq$ |1 + z| 
$\Rightarrow$ |1 - (x + iy)| $ \leq$ |1 + x + iy|
$\Rightarrow$ |(1 - x) - iy| $ \leq$ |(1 + x) + iy|
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{(1-x)^{2}+y^{2}}$ $ \leq$ $\sqrt{(1+x)^{2}+(y)^{2}}$ 
Squaring on both sides we get 
(1 - x)^2 + y^2 $ \leq$ (1 + x)^2 + (y)^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 + x^2 - 2x + y^2 $ \leq$ 1 + x^2 + 2x + y^2
$\Rightarrow$ -2x $ \leq$ 2x 
$\Rightarrow$ 4x $ \geq$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \geq$ 0
Hence we get x = Re (z) $≥$ 0

questions.question: If z is purely real and Re ( z) > 0, then Amp. (z) is
questions.question_raw: If z is purely real and Re ( z) > 0, then Amp. (z) is
questions.explanation:
To find the amplitude of a complex number Z = x + iy first find a value of $\theta $ which satisfy the equation $\theta =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } ,0\le \theta \le \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } $ 
Now depending on the complex number lies in the first, second, third or fourth quadrants the amplitudes will be $\theta ,\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \theta \right) $ respectively.
Given z is purely real and Re (z) > 0
$\therefore$ Z = x + 0i, x > 0
Now $\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ 1 } \right| } $ = tan^-1 0 = 0
Also Z = x + 0i, x > 0 lies in the first quadrant 
Hence Amp(Z) = 0

questions.question: If z is purely real and Re(z) < 0, then Arg.(z) is
questions.question_raw: If z is purely real and Re(z) < 0, then Arg.(z) is
questions.explanation:
To find the amplitude of a complex number Z = x + iy first find a value of $\theta $ which satisfy the equation $\theta =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } ,0\le \theta \le \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } $ 
Now depending on the complex number lies in the first, second, third or fourth quadrants the amplitudes will be $\theta ,\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \Pi -\theta \right) ,-\left( \theta \right) $ respectively.
Given z is purely real and Re (z) $<$ 0
$\therefore$ Z = -x + 0i, x > 0
Now $\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { y }{ x } \right| } =\tan ^{ -1 }{ \left| \frac { 0 }{ -1 } \right| }$ = tan^-1 0 = 0
Also Z = -x + 0i, x > 0 lies in the second quadrant.
Hence Amp(Z) = $\Pi -\theta =\Pi -0=\Pi $

questions.question: Find argument of the complex number (0 + 0i)
questions.question_raw: Find argument of the complex number  (0 + 0i)
questions.explanation:
Let Z = 0 + 0i = $r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) $ 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get 
r cos $\theta$ = 0 and r sin $\theta$ = 0
$\therefore$ r^2 (cos^2 $\theta$ + sin^2 $\theta$) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ r = 0
$\Rightarrow$ cos $\theta$ = 0 and sin $\theta$ = 0 
Which is not possible for any value of $\theta$.

questions.question: Find the Amplitude of i
questions.question_raw: Find the Amplitude of i
questions.explanation:
Let Z = i = $r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) $ 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get
r cos $\theta$ = 0 and r sin $\theta$ = 1
$\therefore$ r^2(cos^2 $\theta$ + sin^2 $\theta$) = 1
$\Rightarrow$ r^2 = 1 $\Rightarrow$ r = 1
$\therefore$ cos $\theta$ = 0 and sin $\theta$ = 1 $\left[ cos\left( \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) = 0, sin\left( \frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) =1 \right] $ [Format of amplitude is $\left( \Pi -\theta \right) $in the second quadrant]
Since $\theta$ lies in the second quadrant, we have the principal value of the argument (Amplitude) = $\left( \Pi -\theta \right) =\left( \Pi -\frac { \Pi }{ 2 } \right) =\frac { \Pi }{ 2 }$

questions.question: 1/z is _________________ for complex number z.
questions.question_raw: 1/z is _________________ for complex number z.
questions.explanation:
multiplicative inverse
On multiplying reciprocal of complex number (1/z) to complex number z, we get multiplying inverse one i.e. z*1=z.

questions.question: Find the Amplitude of -1 - i
questions.question_raw: Find the Amplitude of -1 - i
questions.explanation:
Let Z = -1 - i = $r\left( cos\theta +isin\theta \right) $ 
Then comparing the real and imaginary parts, we get
r cos $\theta$ = -1 and r sin $\theta$ = -1
$\therefore$ r^2(cos^2 $\theta$ + sin^2 $\theta$) = 2
$\Rightarrow$ r^2 = 2 $\Rightarrow$ r = $\sqrt{2}$ 
$\therefore$ $\sqrt{2} \cos \theta$ = -1 and $\sqrt{2} \sin \theta$ = -1
$\Rightarrow$ $\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\sin \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$ [$\operatorname{cos}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$]
Since both sin $\theta$ and cos $\theta$ are negative and we have sin $\theta$ and cos $\theta$ are negative in the third quardrant, the principal value of the argument (Amplitude) = $-\left( \pi -\frac { \pi }{ 4 } \right) =-\frac { 3\pi }{ 4 } $

questions.question: If z is any complex number, then $\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}$ is
questions.question_raw: If z is any complex number, then {tex}\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let z = x + iy
Then $\bar {z }$ = x - iy
$\therefore$ z - $\bar { z}$ = (x + iy) - (x - iy) = 2iy
Now $\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}$ = y
Hence $\frac{z-\bar{z}}{2 i}$ is purely real.

questions.explanation: There is no linear ordering in the set of complex numbers.Hence it is not possible to say one complex number is smaller or bigger than the other

questions.question: Identify the solution set for –( x – 3 ) < 5 – 2x
questions.question_raw: Identify the solution set for –( x – 3 ) < 5 – 2x
questions.explanation:
$-(x-3)<5-2 x$
$\Rightarrow-x+3<5-2 x$
$\Rightarrow-x+3+x<5-2 x+x$
$\Rightarrow 3<5-x$
$\Rightarrow 3-5<5-x-5$
$\Rightarrow-2<-x$
$\Rightarrow x \in(-\infty, 2)$

questions.question: What is the solution set for $\frac{2(x-1)}{5} \leq \frac{3(2+x)}{7}$?
questions.question_raw: What is the solution set for {tex}\frac{2(x-1)}{5} \leq \frac{3(2+x)}{7}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{2(x-1)}{5} \leq \frac{3(2+x)}{7}$ 
Multiplying both sides by LCM (5, 7) = 35, we get
$35 \cdot \frac{2(x-1)}{5} \leq 35 \cdot \frac{3(2+x)}{7}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 14(x - 1) $\le$ 15(2 + x)
$\Rightarrow$ 14x - 14 $\le$ 30 + 15x
$\Rightarrow$ 14x - 15x $\le$ 30 + 14
$\Rightarrow$ -x $\le$ 44
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ -44
Solution set is $\left[ -44,\infty \right) $

questions.question: Identify the solution set for $\frac{(x-1)}{3}+4<\frac{(x-5)}{5}-2$
questions.question_raw: Identify the solution set for {tex}\frac{(x-1)}{3}+4<\frac{(x-5)}{5}-2{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\frac{(x-1)}{3}+4<\frac{(x-5)}{5}-2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-1+12}{3}<\frac{x-5-10}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x+11}{3}<\frac{x-15}{5}$
Multiplying both sides by LCM (3, 5) = 15, we get
$\Rightarrow$ 5(x + 11) < 3(x - 15)
$\Rightarrow$ 5x + 55 < 3x - 45
$\Rightarrow$ 2x < -100
$\Rightarrow$ x < -50
So solution set is$( - \infty , - 50)$

questions.question: What is the solution set for $\frac{2 x-3}{3 x-7}>0$?
questions.question_raw: What is the solution set for {tex}\frac{2 x-3}{3 x-7}>0{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
$\frac{2 x-3}{3 x-7}>0$
$\Rightarrow$ (2x - 3 > 0 and 3x - 7 > 0) or (2x - 3 < 0 and 3x - 7 < 0)
$\Rightarrow$ (x > $\frac { 3 }{ 2 }$ and x > $\frac { 7 }{ 3 }$) or (x < $\frac { 3 }{ 2 }$ and x < $\frac { 7 }{ 3 }$)
$\Rightarrow x>\frac { 7 }{ 3 }$ or $x<\frac { 3 }{ 2 }$
$\Rightarrow x\in \left( \frac { 7 }{ 3 } ,\infty \right) $ or $x\in \left( -\infty ,\frac { 3 }{ 2 } \right)$
Hence the solution set is $\left(-\infty, \frac{3}{2}\right) \cup\left(\frac{7}{3}, \infty\right)$

questions.question: Identify the solution set for $\frac{7 x-5}{8 x+3}>4$
questions.question_raw: Identify the solution set for {tex}\frac{7 x-5}{8 x+3}>4{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\frac{7 x-5}{8 x+3}>4$
$\Rightarrow \frac{7 x-5}{8 x+3}-4>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{7 x-5-4(8 x+3)}{8 x+3}>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{7 x-5-32 x-12}{8 x+3}>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-(25 x+17)}{8 x+3}>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(25 x+17)}{8 x+3}<0$
$\Rightarrow$ (25x + 17 > 0 and 8x + 3 < 0) or (25x + 17 < 0 and 8x + 3 > 0)
[$\because$ $\frac{a}{b}<0$ $\Rightarrow$ (a > 0 and b < 0) or (a < 0 and b > 0)]
$\Rightarrow\left(x>\frac{-17}{25} \text { and } x<\frac{-3}{8} \right)$ or $\left(x<\frac{-17}{25} \text { and } x>\frac{-3}{8}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-17}{25}<x<\frac{-3}{8}$ [Since x < $\frac{-17}{25}$ and x > $\frac{-3}{8}$ is not possible]

questions.question: What is the solution set for $0<\frac{-x}{2}<3$
questions.question_raw: What is the solution set for {tex}0<\frac{-x}{2}<3{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$0<\frac{-x}{2}<3$ 
Multiplying throughout by 2, we get
0 < -x < 6
$\Rightarrow$ 0 > x > -6
So solution set is (-6, 0)

questions.question: Identify solution set for |4 - x| + 1 < 3?
questions.question_raw: Identify solution set for |4 - x| + 1 < 3?
questions.explanation:
|4 - x| + 1 < 3
$\Rightarrow$ |4 - x| < 2
$\Rightarrow$ -2 < 4 - x < 2
$\Rightarrow$ -2 - 4 < -x < 2 - 4
$\Rightarrow$ -6 < -x < -2
$\Rightarrow$ 6 > x > 2
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ (2, 6)
So solution set is (2, 6)

questions.question: What is the solution set for $\frac{|x-2|}{x-2}>0$?
questions.question_raw: What is the solution set for {tex}\frac{|x-2|}{x-2}>0{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
We have |x - 2| $\ge$ 0 [ $\because $ |x| or absolute value of x is always positive or zero but never negative]
Now $\frac{|x-2|}{x-2}>0$ $\Rightarrow $ x - 2 > 0 [$\because$ $\frac{a}{b}$ > 0, a $\ge$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ b > 0] 
$\Rightarrow $ x > 2 
$\Rightarrow x\epsilon \left( 2,\infty \right) $

questions.question: Identify the solution set for $\frac{1}{|x|-3}<\frac{1}{2}$?
questions.question_raw: Identify the solution set for {tex}\frac{1}{|x|-3}<\frac{1}{2}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
$\frac{1}{|x|-3}<\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{|x|-3}-\frac{1}{2}<0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2-|x|+3}{2(|x|-3)}<0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{5-|x|}{(|x|-3)}<0$
$\Rightarrow$ (5 - |x| < 0 and |x| - 3 > 0) or (5 - |x| > 0 and |x| - 3 < 0)
$\Rightarrow$ (|x| > 5 and |x| > 3) or (|x| < 5 and |x| < 3)
$\Rightarrow$ |x| > 5 or |x| < 3 [$\because$ |x| > a $\Rightarrow$ x < -a or x > a and |x| < a $\Rightarrow$ -a < x < a]
$\Rightarrow$ (x < -5 or x > 5) or -3 < x < 3
$\Rightarrow$ x $\epsilon$ ($-\infty$, -5) or x $\epsilon$ (5, $\infty$) or x $\epsilon$ (-3, 3)
$\Rightarrow x \epsilon(-\infty,-5) \cup(5, \infty) \cup(-3,3)$

questions.question: What is the solution set for $\left|\frac{2 x-1}{x-1}\right|>2$ ?
questions.question_raw: What is the solution set for {tex}\left|\frac{2 x-1}{x-1}\right|>2{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
$\left|\frac{2 x-1}{x-1}\right|>2$
$\Rightarrow { { { \frac { 2x-1 }{ x-1 } >2 } } }\quad or\quad \frac { 2x-1 }{ x-1 } <-2\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \left[ \because \left| x \right| >a\Rightarrow x<-a\quad or\quad x>a \right] $
First consider $ \frac { 2x-1 }{ x-1 } >2$
$ \frac { 2x-1 }{ x-1 } -2>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 x-1-2(x-1)}{x-1}>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 x-1-2 x+2}{x-1}>0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x-1}>0$
$\Rightarrow x-1>0$
$\Rightarrow x>1$
$\Rightarrow x \epsilon(1, \infty)$
Now $ \frac { 2x-1 }{ x-1 } <-2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 x-1}{x-1}+2<0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 x-1+2(x-1)}{x-1}<0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{4 x-3}{x-1}<0$
$\Rightarrow(4 x-3>0 \text { and } x-1<0) \text { or }(4 x-3<0 \text { and } x-1>0)$
$\left[\because \frac{a}{b}<0 \Rightarrow(a>0 \text { and } b<0) \quad \text { or } \quad(a<0 \text { and } \quad b>0)\right]$
$\Rightarrow\left(x>\frac{3}{4} \quad \text { and } \quad x<1 \quad\right) \quad \text { or } \quad\left(x<\frac{3}{4} \quad \text { and } \quad x>1\right)$
$\left[\text { Since } \quad x<\frac{3}{4} \quad \text { and } \quad x>1 \text { is } \quad \text { not } \quad \text { possible }\right]$
$\Rightarrow x \in\left(\frac{3}{4}, 1\right)$

questions.question: In the first three papers each of 100 marks , Rishi got 95 , 72 , 83 marks . If he wants an average of greater than or equal to 75 marks and less than 80 marks, find the range of marks he should score in the 4th paper .
questions.question_raw: In the first three papers each of 100 marks , Rishi got 95 , 72 , 83 marks . If he wants an average of greater than or equal to 75 marks and less than 80 marks, find the range of marks he should score in the 4th paper .
questions.explanation:
Let the marks scored by Rishi in the fourth paper be x.
Then$75\le \frac { 95+72+83+x }{ 4 } <80$ 
$\\ \Rightarrow 75\le \frac { 250+x }{ 4 } <80$
Multipling the inequality throughout by 4 ,we get
$300\le 250+x<320$
$\\ \Rightarrow 300-250\le 250+x-250<320-250$
$\\ \Rightarrow 50\le x<70$

questions.question: A man wants to cut three lengths from a single piece of board of length 91 cm. The second length is to be 3 cm longer than the shortest and third length is to be twice as long as the shortest. What are the possible lengths for the shortest board if the third piece is to be at least 5 cm longer than the second?
questions.question_raw: A man wants to cut three lengths from a single piece of board of length 91 cm. The second length is to be 3 cm longer than the shortest and third length is to be twice as long as the shortest. What are the possible lengths for the shortest board if the third piece is to be at least 5 cm longer than the second?
questions.explanation:
Let the length of the shortest piece be x cm. Then we have the length of the second and third pieces are x + 3 and 2x centimeters respectively.
According to the question,
x + (x + 3) + 2x $\le$ 91
$\Rightarrow$ 4x + 3 $\le$ 91
$\Rightarrow$ 4x $\le$ 88
$\Rightarrow$ x $\le$ 22
Also 2x $\ge$ (x + 3) + 5
$\Rightarrow$ 2x $\ge$ x + 8
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ 8
$\Rightarrow$ 8 $\le$ x $\le$ 22
Hence the shortest piece may be atleast 8 cm long but it cannot be more than 22cm in length.

questions.question: Find all pairs of consecutive odd natural numbers, both of which are larger than 10, such that their sum is less than 40.
questions.question_raw: Find all pairs of consecutive odd natural numbers, both of which are larger than 10, such that their sum is less than 40.
questions.explanation:
Let the consecutive odd natural numbers be x and x + 2.
According to the question, x > 10 and x + (x + 2) < 40 
Now x + (x + 2) < 40 $\Rightarrow$ 2x < 38 $\Rightarrow$ x < 19
Hence the maximum value of x is 17 and minimum value is 11.
So the possible pairs of odd natural numbers are (11, 13), (13, 15), (15, 17), (17, 19)

questions.question: The marks scored by Rohit in two tests were 65 and 70. Find the minimum marks he should score in the third test to have an average of atleast 65 marks.
questions.question_raw: The marks scored by Rohit in two tests were 65 and 70. Find the minimum marks he should score in the third test to have an average of atleast 65 marks.
questions.explanation:
Then
$\frac{65+70+x}{3} \geq 65$
$\Rightarrow 135+x \geq 195$
$\Rightarrow x \geq 60$
Hence Rohit should get a minimum of 60 marks to get an average of atleast 65 marks.

questions.question: A solution is to be kept between 30^o C and 35^o C. What is the range of temperature in degree Fahrenheit. What is the range of temperature in degree Fahrenheit if conversion formula is given by C = $\frac { 5 }{ 9 } \left( F-32 \right) $ where C and F represent the temperature in degree Celcius and degree Fahrenheit?
questions.question_raw: A solution is to be kept between 30o C and 35o C. What is the range of temperature in degree Fahrenheit. What is the range of temperature in degree Fahrenheit if conversion formula is given by C = {tex}\frac { 5 }{ 9 } \left( F-32 \right) {/tex} where C and F represent the temperature in degree Celcius and degree Fahrenheit?
questions.explanation:
According to the question 30 < C < 35
Since C = $\frac { 5 }{ 9 } \left( F-32 \right) $, we get 
30 < $\frac{5}{9}(F-32)$ < 35
$\Rightarrow$ $30 . \frac{9}{5}$ < (F - 32) < $35 . \frac{9}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 54 < (F - 32) < 63
$\Rightarrow$ 54 + 32 < (F - 32) + 32 < 63 + 32
$\Rightarrow$ 80 < F < 95
Hence the range of temperature in degree Fahrenheit is between 86^o F and 95^o F

questions.question: The longest side of a triangle is three times the shortest side and the third side is 2 cm shorter than the longest side if the perimeter of the triangles is at least 61 cm, find the minimum length of the shortest side.
questions.question_raw: The longest side of a triangle is three times the shortest side and the third side is 2 cm shorter than the longest side if the perimeter of the triangles is at least 61 cm, find the minimum length of the shortest side.
questions.explanation:
Let the shortest side of a triangle be x cm. Then the length of the longest side is 3x cm and the length of the third side is (3x - 2) cm.
Given the perimeter of the triangles at least 61cm
$\Rightarrow$ x + 3x + (3x - 2) $\ge$ 61
$\Rightarrow$ 7x - 2 $\ge$ 61
$\Rightarrow$ 7x $\ge$ 63
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ 9 
Hence the minimum length of the shortest side = 9 cm

questions.question:

questions.question: The solution for the simultaneous linear inequalities 2x - 3 $\leq$ 7 and -x $ \leq$ 2 is :
questions.question_raw: The solution for the simultaneous linear inequalities 2x - 3 {tex}\leq{/tex} 7 and -x {tex} \leq{/tex} 2 is :
questions.explanation:
2x - 3 $ \leq$ 7 
$\Rightarrow$ 2x - 3 + 3 $ \leq$ 7 + 3
$\Rightarrow$ 2x $ \leq$ 10
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \leq$ 5
Now -x $ \leq$ 2
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \geq$ -2
Therefore the solution of the simultaneous linear equalities is $ - {\text{ 2}} \leq {\text{x}} \leq {\text{5}}$.

questions.question: Given that x is an integer, find the values of x which satisfy the simultaneous linear inequalities ${\text{2 }} + {\text{ x }} < {\text{ 6 and }} {\text{2}} - {\text{3x }} < {\text{ }} - {\text{ 1}}$.
questions.question_raw: Given that x is an integer, find the values of x which satisfy the simultaneous linear inequalities {tex}{\text{2 }} + {\text{ x }} < {\text{ 6 and }} {\text{2}} - {\text{3x }} < {\text{ }} - {\text{ 1}}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
We have ${\text{2 }} + {\text{ x }} < {\text{ 6}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 2 + x − 2 < 6 − 2 
$\Rightarrow x<4$ 
Now 
2 - 3x < -1
$\Rightarrow2-3x-2<-1-2$
$\Rightarrow$ -3x < -3
$\\ \\ \Rightarrow \frac { -3x }{ -3 } >\frac { -3 }{ -3 } $
$\Rightarrow $ x > 1
Hence the solution of the given system of equations is given by 1 < x < 4, $ x\in Z\quad $$\Rightarrow$ x = 2, 3

questions.question: Solve : 30x < 200, when x is a natural number :

$\Rightarrow$ x < $\frac{20}{3}$ = $6\frac{2}{3}$, x $\in$ N
$\Rightarrow$ x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6

questions.question: The solution set for: 3x - 7 > x + 3.
questions.question_raw: The solution set for: 3x - 7 > x + 3.
questions.explanation:
3x - 7 > x + 3
$\Rightarrow$ 3x - 7 + 7 > x + 3 + 7
$\Rightarrow$ 3x > x + 10
$\Rightarrow$ 2x > 10
$\Rightarrow$ x > 5

questions.question: The solution set for (x + 3) + 4 > -2x + 5:
questions.question_raw: The solution set for (x + 3) + 4 > -2x + 5:
questions.explanation:
(x + 3) + 4 > -2x + 5
$\Rightarrow$ x + 7 > -2x + 5
$\Rightarrow$ x + 7 + 2x > -2x + 5 + 2x
$\Rightarrow$ 3x + 7 > 5
$\Rightarrow$ 3x + 7 - 7 > 5 - 7
$\Rightarrow$ 3x > -2
$\Rightarrow$ x > $\frac{-2}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ x\in\left( \frac { -2 }{ 3 } ,\infty \right) $

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities 2x + 5 $\le$ 0, x - 3 $\le$ 0.
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities 2x + 5 {tex}\le{/tex} 0, x - 3 {tex}\le{/tex} 0.
questions.explanation:
2x + 5 $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ 2x < -5
$\Rightarrow$ x $\le$ $\frac{-5}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $x \epsilon\left(-\infty, \frac{-5}{2}\right]$
Now x - 3 $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $\le$ 3
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(-\infty, 3]$
Hence the solution set is $\left( -\infty ,\frac { -5 }{ 2 } \right] \cap { \left( -\infty ,3 \right] } $ = $\left( -\infty ,\frac { -5 }{ 2 } \right]$ 
$\Rightarrow$$ x ≤ - {\frac 52}$

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities x - 2 > 0, 3x < 18
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities x - 2 > 0, 3x < 18
questions.explanation:
x - 2 > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x > 2
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(2, \infty)$ 
Now 3x < 18
$\Rightarrow$ x < 6
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(-\infty, 6)$ 
So solution set is $\left( 2,\infty \right) \cap { \left( -\infty ,6 \right) }$ = (2, 6)
$\Rightarrow$ 2 < x < 6

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities ${\text{4x }} + {\text{ 3 }} \geq {\text{ 2x }} + {\text{ 17 }},{\text{ 3x }}-{\text{ 5 }} < {\text{ }} - {\text{ 2}}$ , for the values of x, then
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities {tex}{\text{4x }} + {\text{ 3 }} \geq {\text{ 2x }} + {\text{ 17 }},{\text{ 3x }}-{\text{ 5 }} < {\text{ }} - {\text{ 2}}{/tex} , for the values of x, then
questions.explanation:
We have given: $4 x+3 \geq 2 x+17$
$\Rightarrow 4 x-2 x \geq 17-3 \Rightarrow 2 x \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow x \geq \frac{14}{2}$ [Dividing by 2 on both sides]
$\Rightarrow$ $x \geq 7$ ..... (i)
Also we have 3x - 5 < -2
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x<-2+5 \Rightarrow 3 x<3$
$\Rightarrow \quad x<1$
On combining (i) and (ii), we see that solution is not possible because nothing is common between these two solutions.(i.e., x < 1, x $\geq$ 7)

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities (x + 5) - 7(x - 2) $\ge$ 4x + 9, 2(x - 3) - 7(x + 5) $\le$ 3x - 9
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities (x + 5) - 7(x - 2) {tex}\ge{/tex} 4x + 9, 2(x - 3) - 7(x + 5) {tex}\le{/tex} 3x - 9
questions.explanation:
(x + 5) - 7(x - 2) $\ge$ 4x + 9
$\Rightarrow$ x + 5 - 7x + 14 $\ge$ 4x + 9
$\Rightarrow$ -6x + 19 $\ge$ 4x + 9
$\Rightarrow$ -6x - 4x $\ge$ 9 - 19
$\Rightarrow$ -10x $\ge$ -10
$\Rightarrow$ x $\le$ 1
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(-\infty, 1]$ 
2(x - 3) - 7(x + 5) $\le$ 3x - 9
$\Rightarrow$ 2x - 6 - 7x - 35 $\le$ 3x < 9 
$\Rightarrow$ -5x - 41 $\le$ 3x - 9
$\Rightarrow$ -5x - 3x $\le$ 41 - 9
$\Rightarrow$ -8x $\le$ 32
$\Rightarrow$ -x $\le$ $\frac{32}{8}$ = 4
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ -4
$\Rightarrow$ $x \epsilon[-4, \infty)$
Hence the solution set is $\left[ -4,\infty \right) \cap { \left( -\infty ,1 \right] }$ = [-4, 1]
Which means $ - {\text{ 4 }} \leq {\text{ x }} \leq {\text{ 1}}$

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities $ - {\text{ 4x }} + {\text{ 1 }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }},{\text{ 3 }}-{\text{ 4x }} < {\text{ }}0$
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities {tex} - {\text{ 4x }} + {\text{ 1 }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }},{\text{ 3 }}-{\text{ 4x }} < {\text{ }}0{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$- {\text{ 4x }} + {\text{ 1 }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }}$
$\Rightarrow -4x\ge -1\\ \Rightarrow \frac { -4x }{ -4 } \le \frac { -1 }{ -4 } \\ \Rightarrow x\le \frac { 1 }{ 4 } \\ \Rightarrow x\epsilon \left( -\infty ,\frac { 1 }{ 4 } \right]$
${\text{ 3 }}-{\text{ 4x }} < {\text{ }}0$
$\Rightarrow -4x<-3\\ \Rightarrow \frac { -4x }{ -4 } >\frac { -3 }{ -4 } \\ \Rightarrow x>\frac { 3 }{ 4 } \\ \Rightarrow x\epsilon \left( \frac { 3 }{ 4 } ,\infty \right) $
Hence solution set is $\left( -\infty ,\frac { -5 }{ 2 } \right] \bigcap { \left( \frac { 3 }{ 4 } ,\infty \right) } =\Phi $
which means no solution exist.

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities: x - 5 > 0, $\frac{2 x-4}{x+2}$ < 2
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities: x - 5 > 0, {tex}\frac{2 x-4}{x+2}{/tex} < 2
questions.explanation:
x - 5 > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x > 5
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(5, \infty)$ 
Now $\frac{2 x-4}{x+2}$ < 2
$\frac{2 x-4}{x+2}$ - 2 < 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 x-4-2(x+2)}{x+2}$ < 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 x-4-2 x-4}{x+2}$ < 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-8}{x+2}$ < 0
$\Rightarrow$ x + 2 > 0 [$\because$ $\frac{a}{b}$ < 0, a < 0 $\Rightarrow$ b > 0]
$\Rightarrow$ x > -2
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(-2, \infty)$ 
Hence the solution set is $\left( 5,\infty \right) \cap \left( -2,\infty \right) =\left( 5,\infty \right) $ 
Which means x > 5.

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities: $\frac{x+7}{x-8}>2, \frac{2 x+1}{7 x-1}>5$
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities: {tex}\frac{x+7}{x-8}>2, \frac{2 x+1}{7 x-1}>5{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\frac {x + 7} {x - 8} > 2$ 
$\Rightarrow \frac {x + 7} {x - 8} -2> 0$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x+7-2(x-8)}{x-8}$ > 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x+7-2 x+16}{x-8}$ > 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(23-x)}{x-8}$ > 0 [$\because$ $\frac{a}{b}$ > 0 $\Rightarrow$ (a > 0 and b > 0) or (a < 0 and b < 0)]
$\Rightarrow$ (23 - x > 0 and x - 8 > 0) or (23 - x < 0 and x - 8 < 0)
$\Rightarrow$ (x < 23 and x > 8) or (x > 23 and x < 8)
$\Rightarrow$ 8 < x < 23 [Since x > 23 and x < 8 is not possible]
$\Rightarrow x\epsilon \left( 8,23 \right) $
Now ${{2x + 1} \over {7x - 1}}$ > 5 
$\Rightarrow \frac {2x + 1} {7x - 1}$ - 5 > 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 x+1-5(7 x-1)}{7 x-1}$ > 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2 x+1-35 x+5}{7 x-1}$ > 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(6-33 x)}{7 x-1}$ > 0 [$\because$ $\frac{a}{b}$ > 0 $\Rightarrow$ (a > 0 and b > 0) or (a < 0 and b < 0)]
$\Rightarrow$ (6 - 33x > 0 and 7x - 1 > 0) or (6 - 33x < 0 and 7x - 1 < 0)
$\Rightarrow$ (x < $\frac{6}{33}$ and x > $\frac{1}{7}$) or (x > $\frac{2}{11}$ and x < $\frac{1}{7}$)
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{7}<x<\frac{2}{11}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in\left(\frac{1}{7}, \frac{2}{11}\right)$ [Since x > $\frac{2}{11}$ and x < $\frac{1}{7}$ is not possible]
Hence, the solution of the system $\frac{x+7}{x-8}>2, \frac{2 x+1}{7 x-1}>5$ will be $\left( 8,23 \right) \cap \left( \frac { 1 }{ 7 } ,\frac { 2 }{ 11 } \right) =\phi $

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities -2 $ \leq$ 6x - 1 < 2
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities -2 {tex} \leq{/tex} 6x - 1 < 2
questions.explanation:
-2 $ \leq$ 6x - 1 < 2
$\Rightarrow$ -2 + 1 $ \leq$ 6x - 1 + 1 < 2 + 1
$\Rightarrow$ -1 $ \leq$ 6x < 3
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-1}{6} \leq \frac{6 x}{6}<\frac{3}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-1}{6} \leq x<\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities -2 < 1 - 3x < 7
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities -2 < 1 - 3x < 7
questions.explanation:
-2 < 1 - 3x < 7 
$\Rightarrow$ -2 - 1 < 1 - 3x - 1 < 7 - 1
$\Rightarrow$ -3 < -3x < 6
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-3}{-3}>\frac{-3 x}{-3}>\frac{6}{-3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 > x > -2
$\Rightarrow$ -2 < x < 1

questions.question: Solve the system of inequalities: $-15<\frac{3(x-2)}{5} \leq 0$
questions.question_raw: Solve the system of inequalities: {tex}-15<\frac{3(x-2)}{5} \leq 0{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$-15<\frac{3(x-2)}{5} \leq 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $-15 \cdot \frac{5}{3}<\frac{3(x-2)}{5} \cdot \frac{5}{3} \leq 0 \cdot \frac{5}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ -25 < (x - 2) $\le$ 0 + 2
$\Rightarrow$ -25 + 2 < x - 2 + 2 $\le$ 2
$\Rightarrow$ -23 < x $\le$ 2

questions.question: The solution set for |3x - 2| $\leq \frac{1}{2}$
questions.question_raw: The solution set for |3x - 2| {tex}\leq \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
|3x - 2| $\le$ $\frac{1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-1}{2} \leq 3 x-2 \leq \frac{1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-1}{2}+2 \leq 3 x-2+2 \leq \frac{1}{2}+2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{3}{2} \leq 3 x \leq \frac{5}{2}$ [$\because$ $|x| \leq a \Leftrightarrow-a \leq x \leq a$]
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \leq 3 x \cdot \frac{1}{3} \leq \frac{5}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3}$ 
 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{5}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in\left[\frac{1}{2}, \frac{5}{6}\right]$

questions.question: The solution set for |x| > 7
questions.question_raw: The solution set for |x| > 7
questions.explanation:
|x| > 7
$\Rightarrow$ -7 > x > 7
$\Rightarrow$ x < -7 or x > 7 [$\because$ |x| > a $\Leftrightarrow$ x < -a or x > a]
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(-\infty,-7)$ or $x \in(7, \infty)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $x \epsilon(-\infty,-7) \cup(7, \infty)$

questions.question: The solution set for: $\frac{|x|-1}{|x|-2} \geq 0, x \neq \pm 2$
questions.question_raw: The solution set for: {tex}\frac{|x|-1}{|x|-2} \geq 0, x \neq \pm 2{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given$\frac{|x|-1}{|x|-2} \geq 0, x \neq \pm 2$ 
$\frac{|x|-1}{|x|-2} \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow$ |x| - 1 $\ge$ 0 and |x| - 2 $\ge$ 0 or |x| - 1 $\le$ 0 and |x| - 2 $\le$ 0 [$\because$ $\frac{a}{b} \geq 0$ $\Rightarrow$ (a $\ge$ 0 and b $\ge$ 0) or (a $\le$ 0 and b $\le$ 0)]
$\Rightarrow$ |x| $\ge$ 1 and |x| $\ge$ 2 or |x| $\le$ 1 and |x| $\le$ 2
$\Rightarrow$ |x| $\ge$ 2 or |x| $\le$ 1 [$\because$ |x| $\ge$ a $\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ a or x $\le$ -a and |x| $\le$ a $\Rightarrow$ $-a \leq x \leq a$ ]
$\Rightarrow$ x $\ge$ 2 or x $\le$ -2 or $-1 \leq x \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(2, \infty)$ or $x \in(-\infty,-2)$ or $x \epsilon(-1,1)$
$\Rightarrow$ $x \in(2, \infty) \cup(-\infty,-2) \cup(-1,1)$

questions.question: The solution set for: $\left|\frac{2(3-x)}{5}\right|<\frac{3}{5}$
questions.question_raw: The solution set for: {tex}\left|\frac{2(3-x)}{5}\right|<\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\left|\frac{2(3-x)}{5}\right|<\frac{3}{5}$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{3}{5}<\frac{2(3-x)}{5}<\frac{3}{5}$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{5}{2}<\frac{2(3-x)}{5} \cdot \frac{5}{2}<\frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{5}{2}$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{3}{2}<3-x<\frac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{3}{2}-3<3-x-3<\frac{3}{2}-3$ $[\because|x|<a \Leftrightarrow-a<x<a]$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{9}{2}<-x<\frac{-3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{9}{2}>x>\frac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow x \in\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{9}{2}\right)$

questions.question: Find all pairs of consecutive even positive integers, both of which are larger than 5, such that their sum is less than 23.
questions.question_raw: Find all pairs of consecutive even positive integers, both of which are larger than 5, such that their sum is less than 23.
questions.explanation:
Let the consecutive even positive integers be x and x + 2.
By data, x > 5 and x + (x + 2) < 23
Now x + (x + 2) < 23
$\Rightarrow$ 2x + 2 < 23
$\Rightarrow$ 2x < 21
$\Rightarrow$ x < $\frac{21}{2}$ = $10\frac{1}{2}$
So we have the least possible value of x is 6 and the maximum value of x is 10.
Therefore the possible pairs of consecutive even positive integers are (6, 8), (8, 10), (10, 12).

questions.question: Solve: 3x + 5 < x - 7, when x is a real number
questions.question_raw: Solve: 3x + 5 < x - 7, when x is a real number
questions.explanation:
3x + 5 < x - 7
$\Rightarrow$ 3x + 5 - x < x - 7 - x
$\Rightarrow$ 2x + 5 < -7
$\Rightarrow$ 2x + 5 - 5 < -7 - 5
$\Rightarrow$ 2x < -12
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{2x}{2}$ < - $\frac{12}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x < -6

questions.question: If x belongs to set of integers, A is the solution set of 2(x - 1) < 3x - 1 and B is the solution set of 4x - 3 $\leq $ 8 + x, find A $\cap$ B
questions.question_raw: If x belongs to set of integers, A is the solution set of 2(x - 1) < 3x - 1 and B is the solution set of 4x - 3 {tex}\leq {/tex} 8 + x, find A {tex}\cap{/tex} B
questions.explanation:
Given 2(x - 1) < 3x - 1 
$\Rightarrow$ 2x - 2 < 3x - 1
$\Rightarrow$ 2x - 2 + 2 < 3x - 1 + 2
$\Rightarrow$ 2x < 3x + 1
$\Rightarrow$ 2x - 3x < 3x + 1 - 3x 
$\Rightarrow$ -x < + 1
$\Rightarrow$ x > -1 but x $\in$ Z
Hence A = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ....}
Now 4x - 3 $\leq$ 8 + x
$\Rightarrow$ 4x - 3 + 3 $\leq$ 8 + x + 3
$\Rightarrow$ 4x $\leq$ 11 + x
$\Rightarrow$ 4x - x $\leq$ 11 + x - x
$\Rightarrow$ 3x $\leq$ 11
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{3x}{3}$ $\leq$ $\frac{11}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\leq$ $\frac{11}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\leq$ $3\frac{2}{3}$, but x $\in$ Z
Therefore B = {....., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3}
Hence A $\cap$ B = {0, 1, 2, 3}

questions.question: The solution set of the inequation: $\frac{2 x-1}{3}-\frac{3 x}{5}+1<0, x \in W$ is:
questions.question_raw: The solution set of the inequation: {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{3}-\frac{3 x}{5}+1<0, x \in W{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
$\frac{2 x-1}{3}-\frac{3 x}{5}+1<0$ 
$\Rightarrow 15 \cdot \frac{2 x-1}{3}-15 \cdot \frac{3 x}{5}+15<0$ [Multiply the inequality throughout by the L.C.M]
$\Rightarrow$ 5(2x - 1) -3(3x) + 15 < 0
$\Rightarrow$ 10x - 5 - 9x + 15 < 0
$\Rightarrow$ x + 10 < 0
$\Rightarrow$ x < -10, but given x $\in$ W
Hence the solution set will be null set.

questions.question: The solution set of 6x - 1 > 5 is :
questions.question_raw: The solution set of 6x - 1 > 5 is :
questions.explanation:
6x - 1 > 5
$\Rightarrow$ 6x - 1 + 1 > 5 + 1
$\Rightarrow$ 6x > 6
$\Rightarrow$ x > 1
Hence the solution set is {x : x > 1, x $\in$ R}

questions.question: If |x - 2| = p, where x < 2, then x - p =
questions.question_raw: If |x - 2| = p, where x < 2, then x - p =
questions.explanation:
Since x < 2 we have x - 2 < 0 and hence |x - 2| = -(x - 2) = 2 - x 
Now |x - 2| = p $\Rightarrow$ 2 - x
$\Rightarrow$ x = 2 - p
$\therefore$ x - p = (2 - p) - p = 2 - 2p

questions.question: A pack of coffee powder contains a mixture of x grams of coffee and y grams of choco. The amount of coffee is greater than that of choco and each coffee powder pack is atmost 10 grams. Which of the following inequations describe the conditions given?
questions.question_raw: A pack of coffee powder contains a mixture of x grams of coffee and y grams of choco. The amount of coffee is greater than that of choco and each coffee powder pack is atmost 10 grams. Which of the following inequations describe the conditions given?
questions.explanation:
Given in the mixture, amount of coffee = x grams
Amount of choco = y grams
Amount of coffee is greater than that of choco $\Rightarrow $ x > y
Each coffee powder pack is atmost 10 grams $\Rightarrow $ x + y $\le$10

questions.question: Graph of the system of inequations x $\ge$ 0, y $\le$ 0 is
questions.question_raw: Graph of the system of inequations x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\le{/tex} 0 is
questions.explanation:
The solution region of ${\text{x }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }}$will be half of XY –plane which lies on the right of the y-axis, including the points on y-axis. [First and Fourth quadrants]
The solution region of ${\text{ y }} \leq {\text{ }}0$ will be the half of XY –plane which lies under x-axis, including the points on the x-axis. [Third and Fourth quadrants]
Hence the solution region of ${\text{x }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }},{\text{ y }} \leq {\text{ }}0$ will be the intersection of the above two regions, which is the fourth quadrant.

questions.question: The region of the XOY-plane represented by the inequalities x $\ge$ 6, y $\ge$ 2, 2x + y $\le$ 10 is

questions.explanation:
Since x $ \geq$ 6 we have 2x $\ge$ 12
Also given y $\geq$ 2
Hence we have 2x + y $\ge$ 12 + 2 $\Rightarrow$ 2x + y $\ge$ 14
Which means the minimum value of 2x + y is 14.
Now since 2x + y $\le$ 10 is not possible, the system x $\ge$ 6, y $\ge$ 2, 2x + y $\le$ 10 have no solution.

$\Rightarrow $ - 3x < - 30
$\Rightarrow \frac { -{ { 3x } } }{ -{ { 3 } } } >\frac { -3{ 0 } }{ -{ { 3 } } } $
$\Rightarrow$ x > 10
$\Rightarrow x \in (10,\infty )$

 $\Rightarrow x-1<-5\quad or\quad x-1>5\\ \Rightarrow x-1+1<-5+1\quad or\quad x-1+1>5+1\\ \Rightarrow x<-4\quad or\quad x>6$ $\left[ \because \left| x \right| >a\Leftrightarrow x<-a\quad or\quad x>a \right] $ 
$\Rightarrow x \in \left( { - \infty , - 4} \right)\mathop \cup \nolimits^ (6,\infty )$

questions.question: The graph of the inequations x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 3x + 4y $\le$ 12 is
questions.question_raw: The graph of the inequations x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, 3x + 4y {tex}\le{/tex} 12 is

options.label: B
options.text: interior of a triangle including the points on the sides

correct_option_texts: interior of a triangle including the points on the sides
questions.explanation:
We have the graph of the system ${\text{x }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }},{\text{ y }} \geq {\text{ }}0$ is the region in the first quadrant including all the points on the positive axes( X-axis and Y-axis)
Now consider 3x + 4y = 12 
If we put x = 0 in the above equation we get y = $\frac{12}{4}$ = 3 and if we put y = 0 in the above equation we get x = $\frac{12}{3}$ = 4
Which means the graph of 3x + 4y = 12 is a straight line passing through the points (4, 0) and (0, 3).
Now if we put (0, 0) in the inequality 3x + 4y $\le$ 12 we get $0 \leq{\text{ 12}}$ which is true and hence the graph of this will be the region in the plane which contain the origin including all the points on this line.
Now we can see the X-axis, Y-axis and the line ${\text{ 3x }} + {\text{ 4y }} ={\text{ 12}}$ will make a small right-angled triangle in the first quadrant with their vertices at (0, 0), (4, 0) and (0, 3) 
Hence, the solution of the system x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 3x + 4y $\le$ 12 will be the interior of this triangle including the points on the sides.

questions.question: The graph of the inequalities x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 2x + y + 6 $\le$ 0 is
questions.question_raw: The graph of the inequalities x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, 2x + y + 6 {tex}\le{/tex} 0 is

We have ${\text{x }} \geq {\text{ }}0\Rightarrow 2 {\text{x }}\geq {\text{ }}0$ 
Also given ${\text{ y }} \geq {\text{ }}0{\text{ }}$ 
Hence 2x + y $\ge$ 0, which means the minimum value possible for 2x + y is zero.
Now 2x + y + 6 $\le$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ 2x + y $\le$ -6 which is not possible 
Hence the system x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 2x + y + 6 $\le$ 0 has no solution.

questions.question: The graph of the inequalities x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 2x + y + 6 $\ge$ 0 is
questions.question_raw: The graph of the inequalities x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, 2x + y + 6 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 is

options.label: A
options.text: the 1^st quadrant including the points on the positive axes

options.label: B
options.text: the 2nd quadrant including the points on the negative axes

options.label: C
options.text: that line of the XOY plane which lies below the line 2x + y + 6 = 0

options.label: D
options.text: that line of the XOY plane which lies above the line 2x + y + 6 = 0

correct_option_texts: the 1^st quadrant including the points on the positive axes
questions.explanation:
Consider the equation 2x + y + 6 = 0, put x = 0 we get y = -6 and put y = 0 we get x = -3.
Hence we have the solution of 2x + y + 6 = 0 will be a straight line which passes through the points (-3, 0) and (0, -6) and will be dividing the plane into two regions.
If we put x = 0, y = 0 in 2x + y + 6 $\ge$ 0, we get 6$\ge$ 0 which is true.
Hence the solution of 2x + y + 6 $\ge$ 0 will be the region in the plane which contain the origin
Also, we have the solution region of x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0 is the first quadrant including the points on the positive axes.
Hence the solution region of the system x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, 2x + y + 6 $\ge$ 0 will be the intersection of the above two regions which will be the first quadrant including the points on the positive axes.
