questions.question: If a and b are coefficients of x^n and x^n+1 respectively in the expansion of (1 + x)^2n+1, then
questions.question_raw: If a and b are coefficients of xn and xn+1 respectively in the expansion of (1 + x)2n+1, then
questions.explanation:
(1 + x)^2n+1 = $1+^{2 n+1} C_{1}(x)+^{2 n+1} C_{2} (x)^{2}$ + ... + (x)^2n+1 
Given that coefficient of xn = a $\Rightarrow$ $^{2 n+1} C_{n}$ = a ....(i)
And coefficient of x^n+1 = b $\Rightarrow$ $^{2 n+1} C_{n+1}$ = b ....(ii)
Now $\frac{^{2 n+1} C_{n+1}}{^{2 n+1} C_{n}}=\frac{b}{a}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(2 n+1) !}{(n+1) ! \cdot n !} \times \frac{(n+1) ! \cdot n !}{(2 n+1) !}$ = $\frac{b}{a}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 = $\frac{b}{a}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ a = b

questions.question: ${(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} + {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}$is
questions.question_raw: {tex}{(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} + {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}{/tex}is
questions.explanation:
We have $(a+b)^{n}+(a-b)^{n}$
$=\left[{}^nC_0a^n+^nC_1\quad a^{n-1}b+^nC_2\quad a^{n-2}b^2+^nC_3\quad a^{n-3}b^3+\dots\dots\dots\dots+^nC_n\quad b^n\right]+$
$\begin{bmatrix}{}^nC_0&a^n-^nC_1a^{n-1}b+^nC_2&a^{n-2}b^2-^nC_3&a^{n-3}b^3+\dots\dots+(-1)^n\cdot^nC_n\end{bmatrix}^n$
$\\ =2\left[ ^{ n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }{ a }^{ n }+^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ a }^{ n-2 }{ b }^{ 2 }+.............. \right] $
Let $a=\sqrt{3}$ and b=1 and n=2n
${ (\sqrt { 3 } +1)^{ 2n } }+{ (\sqrt { 3 } -1)^{ 2n } }=2\left[ ^{ 2n }{ C_{ 0 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 2 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n-2 }{ 1 }^{ 2 }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 4 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n-4 }{ 1 }^{ 4 }+......\right]$
$ \\ =2\left[ ^{ 2n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n-1 }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 4\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n-2 }+.............. \right] $
=2(a positive integer)
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$} is always an even positive integer

questions.explanation:
We have (a + b)^n - (a - b)^n 
= $\left[^{n} C_{0}a^{n}+^{n} C_{1}a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2}a^{n-2} b^{2}\right.$ + ..... + $^{n} C_{n}b^{n}$] - [$^{n} C_{0}a^{n}-^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}$ - $^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .... + $(-1)^{n} \cdot^{n} C_{n} b^{n}$]
= 2[$^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .....]
Let a = $\sqrt{3}$ and b = 1 and n = 2n
Now we get $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$ = $2\left[^{2 n} C_{1} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-1} \cdot 1+^{2 n} C_{3} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-3} 1^{3}\right.$ + $^{2 n} C_{5} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-5} 1^{5}$ + ....]
Since odd powers of $\sqrt{3}$ will make it an irrational number.
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$ is always an irrational number and it will be positive.

questions.question: If n is a positive integer, then ${(\sqrt 3 + i)^n} + {(\sqrt 3 - i)^n}$ is
questions.question_raw: If n is a positive integer, then {tex}{(\sqrt 3 + i)^n} + {(\sqrt 3 - i)^n}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have (a + b)^n + (a - b)^n 
= $\left[^{n} C_{0} a^{n}+^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b\right.$ + $^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}+^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + ..... + $^{n} C_{n} b^{n}$] + $\left[^{n} C_{0} a^{n}-^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}\right.$ - $^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .....$\left.(-1)^{n} \cdot^{n} C_{n} \quad b^{n}\right]$
= 2[$^{n} C_{0} a^{n}+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}$ + ....]
Let a = $\sqrt{3}$ and b = i
Now we get $(\sqrt{3}+i)^{n}+(\sqrt{3}-i)^{n}$ = 2[$^{n} C_{0}(\sqrt{3})^{n}+^{n} C_{2}(\sqrt{3})^{n-2} i^{2}$ + $^{n} C_{4}(\sqrt{3})^{n-4} i^{4}$ + ....]
Since i^2n = (-1)^n = $\pm 1$ 
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+i)^{n}+(\sqrt{3}-i)^{n}$ is always a real number and it will be positive or negative depending on the value of n is even or odd.

questions.question: Find the coefficient of x^6y^3 in the expansion of ${ \left( { x+2y } \right) ^{ 9 } }$
questions.question_raw: Find the coefficient of  x6y3 in the expansion of {tex}{ \left( { x+2y } \right) ^{ 9 } }{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have the general term of $(x+a)^{n} \quad \text { is } \quad T_{r+1}=^{n} C_{r} \quad(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$
Now consider $(x+2 y)^{9}$
Here $T_{r+1}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(x)^{9-r}(2 y)^{r}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(2)^{r}(x)^{9-r}(y)^{r}$
Comparing the indices of x as well as y in $x^{6} y^{3}$ and in $T_{r+1}$ , we get 
$(x)^{9-r}(y)^{r^{2}}=x^{6} y^{3}$
$\Rightarrow r=3$
$\therefore \quad T_{4}=T_{3+1}=^{9} C_{3} \quad(x)^{9-3}(2 y)^{3}$
Hence coefficient of $x^{6} y^{3}=8 .^{9} C_{3}=672$

questions.question: The greatest term in the expansion of ${(3 + 2x)^9}$,when x = 1, is
questions.question_raw: The greatest term in the expansion of {tex}{(3 + 2x)^9}{/tex},when x = 1, is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term in the expansion of $(3+2 x)^{9}$ is given by 
$T_{r+1}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}$
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}}=\frac{^{9} C_{r} \quad(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}}{^{9} C_{r-1} \quad(3)^{10-r}(2 x)^{r-1}}$
$=\frac{9 !(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}}{(9-r) ! \cdot r !} \times \frac{(10-r) !(r-1) !}{9 !(3)^{10-r}(2 x)^{r-1}}$
$=\frac{(10-r) \cdot 2 x}{3 r}$
$=\frac{2(10-r)}{3 r}$ since given x=1
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}} \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(20-2 r)}{3 r} \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow 20-2 r \geq 3 r$
$\Rightarrow r \leq \frac{20}{5}$
$\Rightarrow r \leq 4$
Hence the maximum value of r is 4
Now $T_{5}=T_{4+1}=^{9} C_{4} \quad(3)^{9-4}(2 x)^{4}=126 \times 243 \times 16(x)^{4}=489888$

$\Rightarrow$ 1111111 $\times$ x
Where x = (10^7)^12 + (10^7)^11 + ... + 1

a_n = a + (n - 1)d
= -8 - 6i + (n - 1)2i

For real n, imaginary part = 0
So 2n - 8 = 0
Which gives, n = 4

questions.question: If a, 4, b are in A.P.; a, 2, b are in G.P.; then a, 1, b are in
questions.question_raw: If a, 4, b are in A.P.; a, 2, b are in G.P.; then a, 1, b are in
questions.explanation:
As a, 4, b are in AP so, a + b = 8 ....(i)
Also a, 2, b are in GP so, ab = 4 ....(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
a + b = 2ab
$\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}$ = 2 
$\frac{2}{\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}}$ = 1
Hence a, 1, b are in HP.

questions.question: In an A.P., sum of first p terms is equal to the sum of first q terms. Sum of its first p + q terms is
questions.question_raw: In an A.P., sum of first p terms is equal to the sum of first q terms. Sum of its first p + q terms is
questions.explanation:
S_p = S_q
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{p}{2}$(2a + (p - 1)d) = $\frac{q}{2}$(2a + (q - 1)d)
$\Rightarrow$ 2ap + (p^2 - p)d = 2aq + (q^2 - q)d
$\Rightarrow$ 2a(p - q) + (p^2 - p - q^2 + q) d = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 2a(p - q) + (p^2 - q - (p - q))d = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (p - q)(2a + (p + q - 1)d) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (2a + ( p + q - 1)d) = 0
Now S_p+q = $\frac{p+q}{2}$ (2a(p + q - 1)d) = 0

questions.question: The sum of 40 A.M.’s between two number is 120. The sum of 50 A.M.’s between them is equal to
questions.question_raw: The sum of 40 A.M.’s between two number is 120. The sum of 50 A.M.’s between them is equal to
questions.explanation:
Sum of n number of A.M's between two numbers a and b is given by
Sum = $ n(\frac{{a + b}}{2})$ 
So, 120 = $40\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)$ = 3
Therefore sum of 50 A.M's between a and b is, 
Sum = $n(\frac{{a + b}}{2}) $ = 50 $\times$ 3 = 150

questions.question: In an A.P., sum of first p terms is q and sum of first q terms is p. Sum of its p + q terms is
questions.question_raw: In an A.P., sum of first p terms is q and sum of first q terms is p. Sum of its p + q terms is
questions.explanation:
We know that, S_n = $\frac{n}{2}$(2a + (n - 1)d)
S_p = $\frac{p}{2}$(2a + (p - 1)d) = q $\Rightarrow$ (2a + (p - 1)d) = $\frac {2q}p$ ...(i)
S_q = $\frac{q}{2}$(2a + (q - 1)d) = q $\Rightarrow$ (2a + (q - 1)d) = $\frac {2p}q$ ...(ii)
subtract equation(i) from (ii), we get
(q - p)d = $\frac {2p}{q} -\frac {2q}p$
$\Rightarrow$ (q - p)d = $\frac{2 p^{2}-2 q^{2}}{p q}=\frac{2(p-q)(p+q)}{p q}$
$\Rightarrow$ d = $\frac{-2(p+q)}{p q}$ ...(iii)
Now we have S_p + q = $\frac{p+q}2$[2a + (p + q - 1)d]
$\Rightarrow$ S_p + q = $\frac{p+q}2$[2a + (p - 1)d + qd]
$\Rightarrow$ S_p + q = $\frac{p+q}2\left[\frac{2 q}{p}+q\left(\frac{-2(p+q)}{p q}\right)\right]$ using (i) and (ii)
$\Rightarrow$ S_p + q = $\frac{p+q}2$(-2) = -(p + q)

questions.question: pth term of an A.P. is q and qth term is p, its (p + q)th term is
questions.question_raw: pth term of an A.P. is q and qth term is p, its (p + q)th term is
questions.explanation:
Using the general term formula for A.P
$T_{p}=a+(p-1) d=q$ .........(i)
$T_{q}=a+(q-1) d=p$.....(ii)
subtract equation (i) from (ii), we get 
$(q-p) d=p-q$
$\Rightarrow d=-1$ .......(iii)
Now from (i) and (iii), we get
a-(p-1)=q
$\Rightarrow a=q+p-1$....(iv)
Now we have 
$T_{p+q}$= a + (p + q-1) d=(q + p-1) + (p + q-1)(-1)
$\Rightarrow T_{p+q}=0$

questions.question: pth term of an H.P. is qr and qth term is pr, then rth term of the H.P. is
questions.question_raw: pth term of an H.P. is qr and qth term is pr, then rth term of the H.P. is
questions.explanation:
Using the general term of H.P,
a_p = $ \frac{1}{{a + (p - 1)d}}$ = qr $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{q r}$ = a + ( p - 1)d = qr ....(i)
a_q = $\frac{1}{{a + (q - 1)d}} $ = pr $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{p r}$ = a + (q - 1)d = pr ....(ii)
Solving equation (i) and (ii), we get
a = $\frac{1}{p q r}$ 
d = $\frac{1}{p q r}$ 
So the rth term is,
a_r = $ \frac{1}{{a + (r - 1)d}} = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{pqr}} + (r - 1)\frac{1}{{pqr}}}}$ = pq

questions.question: For what value of b, will the roots of the equation cos x = b, $– 1 \le b \le 1$ when arranged in ascending order of their magnitudes, form an A.P?
questions.question_raw: For what value of b, will the roots of the equation cos x = b, {tex}– 1 \le b \le 1{/tex} when arranged in ascending order of their magnitudes, form an A.P?
questions.explanation:
Given cos x = b ...(i)
According to the question, the values of x should be in A.P
Now cos x = -1 $\Rightarrow$ x = (2n + 1)$\pi$, n $\in$ Z 
When n = 0, we get x = $\pi$, when n = 1 we get x = 3$\pi$ and when n = 2, we have x = 5$\pi$ and we have $\pi, 3 \pi, 5 \pi$ are in A.P.
Hence the value of b is -1

questions.question: The number of numbers between x and x^2 which are divisible by x is
questions.question_raw: The number of numbers between x and x2 which are divisible by x is
questions.explanation:
The required sequence is 2x, 3x, 4x ... (x - 1)x
Hence we get T_1 = a = 2x and T_n = (x - 1)x
So using the nth term formula for A.P
T_n = a + (n - 1)d 
$\Rightarrow$ x(x - 1) = 2x + (n - 1)x
$\Rightarrow$ x - 1 = (n + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ n = x - 2
Hence the number of numbers between x and x^2 which are divisible by x is x - 2.

questions.question: The number of terms common to the Arithmetic progressions 3, 7, 11, …., 407 and 2, 9, 16, …., 709 is
questions.question_raw: The number of terms common to the Arithmetic progressions 3, 7, 11, …., 407 and 2, 9, 16, …., 709 is
questions.explanation:
Consider the arithmetic progressions 3, 7, 11,15,19,23,27,31,35,39,43,47,51, …., 407 and 2, 9, 16, 23,30,37,44,51…., 709
By inspection we have the common terms in the two sequences are, $23,51,79........$which is again an A.P with $a = 23,d = 28$
But the last term of this A.Pshould not exceed 407, therefore
$a_{n} \leq 407$
$23+(n-1) 28 \leq 407$
$28 n-5 \leq 407$
$28 n \leq 412$
$n \leq 412 / 28$
$n \leq 14 \frac{20}{28}$
n=14

questions.explanation:
a,b,c are in A.P, $2b = a + c$...(i)
a,b,c are in G.P, ${b^2} = ac$...(ii)
from (i) and (ii), we get
$\left(\frac{a+c}{2}\right)^{2}=a c$
$\Rightarrow(a+c)^{2}-4 a c=0$
$\Rightarrow(a-c)^{2}=0$$\Rightarrow a=c$ 
using a=c in (ii)
2b = c + c
 $\Rightarrow b=c$ 
so, a = b = c

questions.question: The ratio of first to the last of n A.P.’s between 5 and 35 is 1: 4. The value of n is
questions.question_raw: The ratio of first to the last of n A.P.’s between 5 and 35 is 1: 4. The value of n is
questions.explanation:
The sequence is 5, A_1, A_2, ....An, 35
Here a = 5 and b = 35
We know that, d = $ \frac{{b - a}}{{n + 1}} = \frac{{35 - 5}}{{n + 1}} = \frac{{30}}{{n + 1}}$ 
According to question, 
$\frac{A_{1}}{A_{n}}=\frac{1}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a+d}{a+n d}=\frac{1}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 4a + 4d = a + nd
$\Rightarrow$ 3a = d(n - 4)
$\Rightarrow$ 3 $\times$ 5 = $\left(\frac{30}{n+1}\right)(n-4)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (n + 1) = 2(n - 4)
$\Rightarrow$ n + 1 = 2n - 8
$\Rightarrow$ n = 9

questions.question: If A, G, H denote respectively the A.M., G.M. and H.M. between two unequal positive quantities then
questions.question_raw: If A, G, H denote respectively the A.M., G.M. and H.M. between two unequal positive quantities then
questions.explanation:
Let the numbers be a and b, then we have
A.M = A = $\frac{a+b}{2}$, G.M = G = $\sqrt{a b}$ and H.M = H = $\frac{2 a b}{a+b}$
Now,
$(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})^{2}$ = a + b - $2 \sqrt{a b}$
$\Rightarrow$ a+ b - $2 \sqrt{a b}$ > 0 [Square of any number is always non-negative]
$\Rightarrow$ a + b > $2 \sqrt{a b}$
 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a+b}{2}>\sqrt{a b}$
$\Rightarrow$ A > G ...(i)
Also,
a + b > $2 \sqrt{a b}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{a b} \cdot a+b>\sqrt{a b} \cdot 2 \sqrt{a b}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{a b} \text { . }$(a + b) > 2ab
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{a b}>\frac{2 a b}{a+b}$
$\Rightarrow$ G > H ...(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii), we get H < G < A

questions.question: If A and G denote respectively, the A.M. and G.M. between two positive numbers a and b, then A - G is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A and  G denote respectively, the A.M. and G.M. between two positive numbers a and b, then A - G is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given the numbers are a and b, then we have
$A.M=A=\frac { { a+b } }{ 2 } ,G.M=G=\sqrt { ab } $
Then $A-G=\frac { { a+b } }{ 2 } -\sqrt { ab } =\frac { { { { (\sqrt { a } -\sqrt { b } ) }^{ 2 } } } }{ 2 } $

questions.question: If a, b, c, d are in H.P., then ab + bc + cd is
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c, d are in H.P., then ab + bc + cd is
questions.explanation:
Using the property of H.M.
$\frac{2 a c}{a+c}$ = b
$\frac{2 b d}{b+d}$ = c
Multiplying the above equations, we get
$\frac{4 a b c d}{(a+c)(b+d)}$ = bc
$\Rightarrow$ 4abcd = bc(ab + bc + ad + cd)
$\Rightarrow$ 4abcd = ab^2c + (bc)^2 + abcd + bc^2d
$\Rightarrow$ 3abcd = ab^2c + (bc)^2 + bc^2d
Now dividing by bc, 
3ad = ab + bc + cd

Now a_n = a + (n - 1)d = 97
$\Rightarrow$ 10 + (n - 1)3 = 97
$\Rightarrow$ n = 30
We have S_n = $\frac{n}{2}(a+l)$ 
S_30 = $\frac{30}{2}(4+97)$ = 15 $\times$ 101 = 1605

questions.question: If the numbers a, b, c, d, e form an A.P. then the value of a – 4b + 6c – 4d + e is
questions.question_raw: If the numbers a, b, c, d, e form an A.P. then the value of a – 4b + 6c – 4d + e is
questions.explanation:
Let the common difference of the A.P. be d
Then we have if
a = x 
b = x + d
c = x + 2d
d = x + 3d and e = x + 4d
$\therefore$ a - 4b + 6c - 4d + e = x - 4(x + d) + 6(x + 2d) - 4(x + 3d) + (x + 4d)
= (x - 4x + 6x - 4x + x) + (-4d + 12d - 12d+ 4d) = 0

questions.question: The sum of all non-reducible fractions with the denominator 3 lying between the numbers 5 and 8 is
questions.question_raw: The sum of all non-reducible fractions with the denominator 3 lying between the numbers 5 and 8 is
questions.explanation:
We have 5 = $\frac{15}{3}$ and 8 = $\frac{24}{3}$ 
Hence the fractions between 5 and 8 with 3 as denominator will be $\frac{16}{3}, \frac{17}{3}, \frac{18}{3}, \frac{19}{3}, \frac{20}{3}, \frac{21}{3}, \frac{22}{3}$ and $\frac{23}{3}$ in this only 6 = $\frac{18}{3}$ and 7 = $\frac{21}{3}$ are reducible.
Now $\frac{16}{3}, \frac{17}{3}, \frac{18}{3}, \frac{19}{3}, \frac{20}{3}, \frac{21}{3}, \frac{22}{3}$ and $\frac{23}{3}$ is an A.P. with first term a = $\frac{16}{3}$ and d = $\frac{1}{3}$ 
Hence, sum = $\frac{n}{2}(a+l)=\frac{8}{2}\left(\frac{16}{3}+\frac{23}{3}\right)$ = 4(13) = 52
Hence, the sum of non redubile fractions = 52 - (6 + 7) = 52 - 13 = 39

questions.question: The next term of the sequence 1, 1, 2, 4, 7, 13,…. is
questions.question_raw: The next term of the sequence 1, 1, 2, 4, 7, 13,…. is

The given sequence can be expressed as T_1 = T_2 = 1
T_n = T_n-1 + T_n-2 + T_n-3, n $\ge$ 3
$\therefore$ T_7 = T_6 + T_5 + T_4 = 13 + 7 + 4 = 24

questions.question: An A.P. consists of n (odd) terms and its middle term is m. Then the sum of the A.P. is
questions.question_raw: An A.P. consists of n (odd) terms and its middle term is m. Then the sum of the A.P. is
questions.explanation:
Let a be the first term, d be the common difference and n be the number of terms of the A.P.
Then we have S_n = $\frac{n}{2}[2 a+(n-1) d]$ = $n\left[a+\frac{n-1}{2} d\right]$ ....(i)
As n is odd $\frac{n-1}{2}$ will give the number of terms just before the middle term.
Hence $a+\frac{n-1}{2} d$ will give the middle term, but given middle term is m.
Hence we get m = $a+\frac{n-1}{2} d$ .... (ii)
Now from (i) and (ii) we get S_n = nm

questions.question: If the numbers a, b, c, d, e are in A.P then find the value of a - 4b + 6c - 4d + e
questions.question_raw: If the numbers a, b, c, d, e are in A.P then find the value of a - 4b + 6c - 4d + e
questions.explanation:
Given a, b, c, d, e are in A.P
Let D be the common difference of the A.P
Then we have b = a + D
c = b + D = a + 2D
d = c + D = a + 3D
e = d + D = a + 4D
Hence a - 4b + 6c - 4d + e = a - 4(a + D) + 6(a + 2D) - 4(a + 3D) + a + 4D = 0

questions.question: If the numbers a, b, c are in A.P., b, c, d are in G.P. and c, d , e are in H.P., then a, c, e are in
questions.question_raw: If the numbers a, b, c are in A.P., b, c, d are in G.P. and c, d , e are in H.P., then a, c, e are in
questions.explanation:
If the numbers a, b, c are in A.P., we have b = $\frac{a+c}{2}$ ....(i)
Since b, c, d are in G.P. we get c^2 = bd ....(ii)
Now c, d, e are in H.P implies d = $\frac{2 c e}{c+e}$ .....(iii)
Substituting (i) and (iii) in equation (ii), we get c^2 = $\frac{a+c}{2} \cdot \frac{2 c e}{c+e}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ c = $\frac{a e+c e}{c+e}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ c^2 + ce = ae + ce 
$\Rightarrow$ c^2 = ae
$\Rightarrow$ a, c, e are in G.P.

questions.question: If x, y, z are in A.P., then (x + 2y – z) (x + z – y) (z + 2y – x) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If x, y, z are in A.P., then (x + 2y – z) (x + z – y) (z + 2y – x) is equal to
questions.explanation:
If the numbers x, y, z are in A.P., we have y = $\frac{x+z}{2}$ ....(i)
(x + 2y - z) (x+ z - y) (z + 2y - x)
= (x + x + z - x) (x + z - $\frac{x+z}{2}$) (z + x + z - x) [using (i)]
= $(2 x)\left(\frac{x+z}{2}\right)(2 z)$ 
= 4xyz [using (i)]

questions.question: If a, b , c are in A.P. and also $\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}$ are in A. P., then
questions.question_raw: If a, b , c are in A.P. and also {tex}\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}{/tex} are in A. P., then
questions.explanation:
If a, b, c are in A.P we have b - a = c - b ...(i)
If $\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}$ are in A.P we have $\frac{1}{b}-\frac{1}{a}=\frac{1}{c}-\frac{1}{b}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a-b}{a b}=\frac{b-c}{b c}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{q b}=\frac{1}{b c}$ [using (i)]
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a}=\frac{1}{c}$
$\Rightarrow a=c$ ...(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii), we get b - a = a - b
$\Rightarrow 2 b=2 a$
$\Rightarrow b=a$ ...(iii)
Now from (i) and (ii), we get a = b = c

questions.question: The sum of terms equidistant from the beginning and end in A.P. is equal to
questions.question_raw: The sum of terms equidistant from the beginning and end in A.P. is equal to

options.label: C
options.text: sum of the first and the last terms

correct_option_texts: sum of the first and the last terms
questions.explanation:
Let the first term of the A.P be a , last term be l and the common difference be d 
Now the A.P will be of the form $a, a+d, a+2 d, \ldots \ldots \ldots ., l-2 d$ ,l-d,l
Sum of two term equidistant from the beggining and end (say r + 1 t term) = a + r d + b-r d=a + b = sum of the first term and last ter

questions.question: Sum of first 5 terms of an A.P. is one fourth of the sum of next five terms. If the first term = 2, then the common difference of the A.P. is
questions.question_raw: Sum of first 5 terms of an A.P. is one fourth of the sum of next five terms. If the first term = 2, then the common difference of the A.P. is
questions.explanation:
Given a = 2
Let d be the common difference of the A.P.
Given S_5 = $\frac{1}{4}\left[S_{10}-S_{5}\right]$ ....(i)
We have $\frac{n}{2}[2 a+(n-1) d]$ 
$\therefore$ S_5 = $\frac{5}{2}[4+4 d]$ = 20 + 45d ....(iii)
So S_10 - S_5 = 10 + 35d ....(ii)
From (i) and (ii) we get 10 + 10d = $\frac{1}{4}[10+35 d]$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 40 + 40d - 10 - 35d = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 5d = -30
$\Rightarrow$ d = -6

questions.question: The first, second and last terms of an A.P. are a, b and 2 a. The number of terms in the A.P. is
questions.question_raw: The first, second and last terms of an A.P. are a, b and 2 a. The number of terms in the A.P. is
questions.explanation:
Given $a_{1}=a, a_{2}=b$ and $a_{n}=2 a$ 
Hence d=b-a
Now $a_{n}=a+(n-1) d $ $\Rightarrow$ 2a = a + (n - 1)(b - a)
$\Rightarrow a=(n-1)(b-a)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b-a}+1=n$
$\Rightarrow n=\frac{b}{b-a}$

questions.question: The sum of all the two-digit numbers is
questions.question_raw: The sum of all the two-digit numbers is
questions.explanation:
The two digit numbers forms on A.P with a = 10, a_n = 99 and d = 1
Now a_n = a + (n - 1)d $\Rightarrow$ 99 = 10 + (n - 1) $\Rightarrow$ n = 99 - 9 = 90
We have S_n = $\frac {n}{2}$[2a + (n - 1)d]
$\Rightarrow$ S_90 = $\frac {n}{2}$[20 + (90 - 1)1]
$\Rightarrow$ S_90 = $\frac {90}{2}$ $\times$ 109 = 4905

questions.question: Sum of all 2 digit odd numbers is
questions.question_raw: Sum of all 2 digit odd numbers is
questions.explanation:
The two digit odd numbers forms an A.P with a = 11, an = 99 and d = 2
Now an = a + (n - 1)d $\Rightarrow$ 99 = 11 + (n - 1)2 $\Rightarrow$ n = $\frac {90}{2}$ = 45
We have S_n = $\frac {n}{2}$[2a + (n - 1)d]
$\Rightarrow$ S_45 = $\frac {45}{2}$[22 + (45 - 1)$\cdot$2]
$\Rightarrow$ S_45 = $\frac {45}{2}$$​​\cdot$110 = 45.55 = 2475

questions.question: The number of terms common to the two A.P.s 2, 5, 8, 1 … , 98 and 3, 8, 13, 18, 23, … , 198
questions.question_raw: The number of terms common to the two A.P.s 2, 5, 8, 1 … , 98 and 3, 8, 13, 18, 23, … , 198
questions.explanation:
Consider the progressions 2, 5, 8, 11, 14, 17, 20, 23 ...., 98 and 3, 8, 13, 18, 23, ...., 198
for the first A.P. d = 3 and for the second A.P d = 5
Now the first term common to both the progessions = 8
Also L.C.M (3, 5) = 15
Hence the terms common to both the progressions is an A.P. with a = 8 and d = 15 which is 8, 23, 38, ... 98 
Now a_n = a + (n - 1)d $\Rightarrow$ 98 = 8 + (n - 1)15
$\Rightarrow$ 90 = 15(n - 1)
$\Rightarrow$ n - 1 = $\frac{90}{15}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ n = $\frac{90}{15}$ + 1 = 7

questions.question: The values of x for which the solutions of the equation cos $\theta = x,\theta \ge 0$ form an A.P. are
questions.question_raw: The values of x for which the solutions of the equation cos {tex}\theta = x,\theta \ge 0{/tex} form an A.P. are
questions.explanation:
We have cos $\theta$ is a periodic function whose value oscillates between -1 and 1.
Also cos $\theta$ = 1 when $\theta$ = 0, cos $\theta$ = 0 when $\theta$ = 90^o and cos $\theta$ = -1 when $\theta$ = 180^o 
But we have 1, 0, -1 is an A.P.
Hence the values of x are 1, 0, -1

questions.question: If $a\; \in \;\;R,$ then the roots of the equation tan x = a are in G.P for what values of a
questions.question_raw: If {tex}a\; \in \;\;R,{/tex} then the roots of the equation tan x = a are in G.P  for what values of a
questions.explanation:
We have $\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \tan 45^{\circ}=1$ and $\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$
Also we have $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, 1, \sqrt{3}$ are in G.P

questions.question: The values of a for which the roots of the equation sin $\theta $ = a in A.P. are
questions.question_raw: The values of a for which the roots of the equation sin {tex}\theta {/tex} = a in A.P. are
questions.explanation:
We have sin $\theta$ is a periodic function whose value oscillates between -1 and 1.
Also sin $\theta$ = 0 when $\theta$ = 0^o, sin $\theta$ = 1 when $\theta$ = 90^o, and sin $\theta$ = -1 when $\theta$ = 270^o 
But we have -1, 0, 1 is an A.P
Hence the values of x are -1, 0, 1

questions.question: The sum of first four terms of an A. P. is 56 and sum of last four terms is 112. If the first term is 11, then the number of terms is
questions.question_raw: The sum of first four terms of an A. P. is 56 and sum of last four terms is 112. If the first term is 11, then the number of terms is
questions.explanation:
Given a = 11 and S_4 = 56
Let d be the common difference of the A.P
We have S_n = $\frac{n}{2}[2 a+(n-1) d]$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 56 = $\frac{4}{2}[22+3 d]$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 56 - 44 = 6d
$\Rightarrow$ d = $\frac{12}{6}$ = 2
Last four terms of the A.P. can be taken as a_n, a_n-1, a_n-2, a_n-3 
Now an = a + (n - 1)d 
$\Rightarrow$ a_n = 11 + (n - 2)2, a_n-1 = 11 + (n - 2)2, a_n-2 = 11 + (n - 3)2, a_n-3 = 11 + (n - 4)2
Hence sum of the last four terms = a_n + a_n-1 + a_n-2 + a_n-3 = 112
$\Rightarrow$ 11 + (n - 1)2 + 11 + (n - 2)2 + 11 + (n - 3)2 + 11 + (n - 4)2 
$\Rightarrow$ 44 + 2[n - 1 + n - 2 + n - 3 + n - 4] = 112
$\Rightarrow$ 2[4n - 10] = 68
$\Rightarrow$ n = $\frac{88}{8}$ = 11

questions.question: The ratio of the ${{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{th}}}}$ to the (n - 1)th mean between 1 and 31, when n arithmetic means are inserted between them, is 5 : 9. The value of n is
questions.question_raw: The ratio of the {tex}{{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{th}}}}{/tex} to the (n - 1)th mean between 1 and 31, when n arithmetic means are inserted between them, is 5 : 9. The value of n is
questions.explanation:
Let A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4 ..., A_n be the n arithmetic means inserted between 1 and 31.
The we have 1, A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4 ..., A_n, 31 is an A.P with a = 1, T_n = 31 and number of terms = n + 2
Now, T_n = 31 $\Rightarrow$ 1 + [(n + 2) - 1]d = 31
$\Rightarrow$ d = $\frac{30}{n+1}$
Hence we get T_7 = a + 7d = 1 + 7$\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]$ ...(i)
And T_n - 1 = a(n - 1) + d = 1 + (n - 1)$\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]$ ...(ii)
Given $\frac{T_{7}}{T_{n-1}}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+7\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]}{1+(n-1)\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{n+1+210}{n+1}}{\frac{31 n-29}{n+1}}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow$ 9n + 1899 = 155n - 145
$\Rightarrow$ 2044 = 146 n
$\Rightarrow$ n = $\frac{2044}{146}$ = 14

questions.question: If a, b, c, d, e are in G. P., then $\frac e c$ equals
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c, d, e are in G. P., then {tex}\frac e c{/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
If a, b, c, d, e are in G.P
we have $\frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}=\frac{d}{c}=\frac{e}{d}$
$\therefore \frac{c}{b}=\frac{e}{d} \Rightarrow \frac{e}{c}=\frac{d}{b}$

questions.question: The product of first n odd terms of a G.P. whose middle term is m is
questions.question_raw: The product of first n odd terms of a G.P. whose middle term is m is
questions.explanation:
Let the terms in a GP be a,ar,$a, a r, a r^{2}, a r^{3}, \dots \dots \dots$
Product of odd number of terms ( let number of terms be 'n' , n is odd ) 
$=a \cdot a r \cdot a r^{2} \cdot a r^{3} \dots a r^{n-1}$
$=a^{n} \cdot\left(1 \cdot r \cdot r^{2} \cdot r^{3} \dots r^{n-1}\right)$
$=a^{n}, r^{1+2+3+\dots + n-1}$
$=a^{n} \cdot r^{\frac{(n-1) n}{2}}$ [The sum of first n natural numbers = $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$]
We have the middle term $=m=a r^{\frac{(n-1)}{2}}$ ......(i)
$\therefore$ Product of odd number of terms = $a^{n} \cdot r^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}=\left(a r^{\frac{(n-1)}{2}}\right)^{n}$= $m^{n}$ [using (i)]

questions.question: The fourth term of a G. P. is 2, then product of first 7 terms is
questions.question_raw: The fourth term of a G. P. is 2, then product of first 7 terms is
questions.explanation:
Let a be the first term and r be the common ratio of the G.P
Given T_4 = 2 $\Rightarrow$ ar^3 = 2
Then product of the first 7 terms = $a \cdot a r \cdot a r^{2} \cdot a r^{3} \cdot a r^{4} \cdot a r^{5} \cdot a r^{6}$ = a^7r^21 = (ar^3)^7 = 2^7 = 128

questions.question: If three positive numbers a, b, c are in G. P. then log a, log b, log c are in
questions.question_raw: If three positive numbers a, b, c are in G. P. then log a, log b, log c are in
questions.explanation:
If a, b, c are in G.P. we have $\frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}$ 
So log$\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$ = log$\left(\frac{c}{b}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ log b - log a = log c - log b [$\because$ $\log \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)$ = log a - log b]
$\Rightarrow$ 2log b = log c + log a 
$\Rightarrow$ log a, log b, log c are in A.P.

questions.question: The nth term of the sequence 5 + 55 + 555 + …. is
questions.question_raw: The nth term of the sequence 5 + 55 + 555 + …. is
questions.explanation:
5+55+555+.........
$=\frac{5}{9}\{9+99+999+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots .\}$
$=\frac59\left\{[10-1\right]+\left[10^2-1\right]+\left[10^3-1\right]$+........+ [10^n-1] +........}
Hence by inspection we get nth term is $\\\frac59\left[10^n-1\right]$

questions.question: The sum of first three terms of a G.P. is to the sum of next three terms is 125 : 27. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.question_raw: The sum of first three terms of a G.P. is to the sum of next three terms is 125 : 27. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{T_{1}+T_{2}+T_{3}}{T_{4}+T_{5}+T_{6}}=\frac{125}{27}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{T_{1}+T_{2}+T_{3}}{T_{1}+T_{2}+T_{3}+T_{4}+T_{5}+T_{6}}=\frac{125}{152}$ [If $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{c}{d}$ then $\frac{a}{a+b}=\frac{c}{c+d}$]
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{S_{3}}{S_{6}}=\frac{125}{152}$ 
We have for a G.P., Sn = $\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{r-1}$, r > 1
$\therefore$ $\frac{\frac{a\left(r^{3}-1\right)}{r-1}}{\frac{a\left(r^{6}-1\right)}{r-1}}$ = $\frac{125}{152}$, r - 1 $\ne$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{r^{3}-1}{r^{6}-1}=\frac{125}{152}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^6 - 152r^3 - 27 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^6 - 125r^3 - 27r^3 - 27 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^3(r^3 - 1) - 27(r^3 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r^3 - 1)(125r^3 - 27) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^3 = 1 or $\frac{27}{125}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ r = 1, $\frac{3}{5}$ 
Since r - 1 $\ne$ 0, r cannot be 1
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\frac{3}{5}$

questions.question: Sum of an infinitely many terms of a G.P. is 3 times the sum of even terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.question_raw: Sum of an infinitely many terms of a G.P. is 3 times the sum of even terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Consider the infinite G . P $a, a r, a r^{2}, a r^{3}, \dots \dots \dots \dots, \dots$ with first term a and common ratio r
Then the even terms $a r, a r^{3}, a r^{5}, \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots .$ is again an infinite G.P with first term ar and common ratio $r^{2}$
W e have $S_{\infty}=\frac{a}{1-r}$ 
Given $S_{\infty}=3$ . Sum of even terms
$\Rightarrow a+a r+a r^{2}+a r^{3}+$..........$=3 .\left[a r+a r^{3}+a r^{5}+\right.$............]
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{1-r}=3 \cdot \frac{a r}{1-r^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-r}=3 \cdot \frac{r}{(1-r)(1+r)}$
$\Rightarrow 1(1+r)=3 . r$
$\Rightarrow 2 r=1$$\Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: If $\frac{a^n+b^n}{a^{n-1}+b^{n-1}}$ be the arithmetic mean between two distinct positive reals a and b then the value of n is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{a^n+b^n}{a^{n-1}+b^{n-1}}{/tex} be the arithmetic mean between two distinct positive reals a and b then the value of n is
questions.explanation:
We have A.M of a and b is $\frac{a+b}{2}$ 
Given $\frac{a^{n}+b^{n}}{a^{n-1}+b^{n-1}}=\frac{a+b}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 2(a^n + b^n) = (a + b)(a^n-1 + b^n-1)
$\Rightarrow$ 2a^n + 2b^n = a^n + ab^n-1 + ba^n-1 + b^n
$\Rightarrow$ a^n + b^n = ab^n-1 + ba^n-1
$\Rightarrow$ a^n - ba^n-1 = ab^n-1 - b^n
$\Rightarrow$ a^n-1(a - b) = b^n-1(a - b)
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a^{n-1}}{b^{n-1}}$ = 1 = $\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{0}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ n - 1 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ n = 1

questions.question: If $\frac{a+b x}{a-b x}=\frac{b+c x}{b-c x}=\frac{c+d x}{c-d x}, x \neq 0$ then a, b, c, d are in
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{a+b x}{a-b x}=\frac{b+c x}{b-c x}=\frac{c+d x}{c-d x}, x \neq 0{/tex} then a, b, c, d are in
questions.explanation:
Let $\frac{a+b x}{a-b x}=\frac{b+c x}{b-c x}=\frac{c+d x}{c-d x}$ = k(say)
Now consider $\frac{a+b x}{a-b x}$ = k
Applying componendo and dividendo, we get
$\frac{a+b x+a-b x}{a+b x-a+b x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a}{b x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ .....(i)
Now applying componendo and dividendo, on $\frac{b+c x}{b-c x}$ = k we get
$\frac{b+c x+b-c x}{b+c x-b+c x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{b}{c x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ ...(ii)
Again applying componendo and dividendo, on $\frac{c+d x}{c-d x}$ = k we get
$\frac{c+d x+c-d x}{c+d x-c+d x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{c}{d x}=\frac{k-1}{k+1}$ ....(iii)
From equations (i), (ii) and (iii) we get $\frac{a}{b x}=\frac{b}{c x}=\frac{c}{d x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{b}{c}=\frac{c}{d}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ a, b, c and d are in G.P.

questions.question: The A.M. between two positive numbers a and b is twice the G.M. between them. The ratio of the numbers is
questions.question_raw: The A.M. between two positive numbers a and b is twice the G.M. between them. The ratio of the numbers is
questions.explanation:
Given a and b are two positive numbers 
Also given A.M=2.G.M
$\frac{a+b}{2}=2 \sqrt{a b}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a+b}{2 \sqrt{a b}}=\frac{2}{1}$
Applying componendo dividendo
$\frac{a+b+2 \sqrt{a b}}{a+b-2 \sqrt{a b}}=\frac{2+1}{2-1}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^{2}}{(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})^{2}}=\frac{3}{1}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}}{\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1}$
Applying componendo dividendo again we get
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sqrt{a}}{2 \sqrt{b}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2}}=\frac{4+2 \sqrt{3}}{4+2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{2(2+\sqrt{3})}{2(2-\sqrt{3})}=\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{2-\sqrt{3}}$

questions.question: If A, G, H denote respectively the A.M., G.M. and H.M. between two unequal positive numbers, then
questions.question_raw: If A, G, H denote respectively the A.M., G.M. and H.M. between two unequal positive numbers, then
questions.explanation:
Given the number are a and b, then we have
A.M = A = $\frac{a+b}{2}$, G.M. = G = $\sqrt{a b}$ and H.M = H = $\frac{2ab}{a + b}$ 
Now AH = $\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2 a b}{a+b}\right)$ = ab = $(\sqrt{a b})^{2}$ = G^2

questions.question: The arithmetic, harmonic and geometric means between two +ve numbers are $\frac {144} {15},15$ and 12 but not necessarily in this order, then the H.M. G.M. and A.M. respectively are
questions.question_raw: The arithmetic, harmonic and geometric means between two +ve numbers are {tex}\frac {144} {15},15{/tex} and 12 but not necessarily in this order, then the H.M. G.M. and A.M. respectively are
questions.explanation:
We have H.M < G.M < A.M
Also given their values are $\frac{144}{15}$ = 9, 6, 12, 15
Hence we can say H.M = 9.6, G.M = 12, A.M = 15

questions.question: The sum of first eight terms of a G.P. is 82 times the sum of first four terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.question_raw: The sum of first eight terms of a G.P. is 82 times the sum of first four terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Let d be the common ratio of the G.P.
Given S_8 = 82 S_4 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{S_{8}}{S_{4}}$ = 82
We have S_n = $\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{r-1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\frac{a\left(r^{8}-1\right)}{r-1}}{\frac{a\left(r^{4}-1\right)}{(-1)}}=82, r-1 \neq 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{r^{8}-1}{r^{4}-1}$ = 82
$\Rightarrow$ r^8 - 1 - 82r^4 + 82 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r^4)^2 - 82r^4 + 81 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r^4)^2 - 82r^4 - 1r^4 + 81 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^4(r^4 - 1) - 81(r^4 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r^4 - 81)(r^4 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^4 = 81 or r^4 = 1
Since r - 1 $\ne$ 0, r cannot be 1
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\sqrt[4]{81}$ = 3

questions.question: The sum of first 10 terms of a G.P. is equal to 244 times the sum of its first five terms. Then the common ratio is
questions.question_raw: The sum of first 10 terms of a G.P. is equal to 244 times the sum of its first five terms. Then the common ratio is
questions.explanation:
Let r be the common ratio of the G.P.
Given S_10 = 244 S_5 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{S_{10}}{S_{5}}$ = 244
We have S_n = $\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{r-1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\frac{a\left(r^{10}-1\right)}{r-1}}{\frac{a\left(r^{5}-1\right)}{r-1}}=244, r-1 \neq 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{r^{10}-1}{r^{5}-1}$ = 244
$\Rightarrow$ r^10 - 1 - 244r^5 + 244 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r^5)^2 - 244r^5 + 243 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (r5)^2 - 243r^5 - 1r^5 + 243 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^5(r^5 - 1) - 243(r^5 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^5 = 243 or r^5 = 1
Since r - 1 $\ne$ 0, r cannot be 1
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\sqrt[5]{243}$ = 3

questions.question: The product of terms equidistant from the beginning and end in a G.P. (finite) is equal to
questions.question_raw: The product of terms equidistant from the beginning and end in a G.P. (finite) is equal to

options.label: B

options.label: D
options.text: Sum of the first and last terms

correct_option_texts: product of the first and last terms
questions.explanation:
Let the G.P be a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ....., ar^n-3, ar^n-2, ar^n-1 with first term a and common ratio r and let the last term l = T_n = ar_n-1 
Now we have T_1T_n = a.ar^n-1 = a^2r^n-1 
T_2.T_n-1 = ar.ar^n-2 = a^2r^n-1
T_3.T_n-2 = ar^2.ar^n-3 = a^2r^n-1 ........
T_m.T_n-(m-1) = ar^m-1. ar^n-m = a^2r^n-1 
Hence we have the product of terms equidistant from the beginning and end = first term. last term

questions.question: If x, 2 x + 2, 3 x + 3, … are in G.P., then the fourth term of this sequence is
questions.question_raw: If x, 2 x + 2, 3 x + 3, … are in G.P., then the fourth term of this sequence is
questions.explanation:
If x, 2x + 2, 3x + 3, .... are in G.P., then we get $\frac{2 x+2}{x}=\frac{3 x+3}{2 x+2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (2x + 2)^2 = x(3x + 3)
$\Rightarrow$ 4x^2 + 8x + 4 = 3x^2 + 3x
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 5x + 4 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (x + 4)(x+ 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = -4, -1
When x = -4 the sequence is -4, -6, -9.... and then r = $\frac{-6}{-4}=\frac{-9}{-6}=\frac{3}{2}$ 
when x = -1 the sequence is -1, 0, 0, 0 .... and then r = $\frac{0}{-1}$ = 0 (this sequence can be discarded as r = 0)
Hence the fourth term is $-9 \cdot \frac{3}{2}=\frac{-27}{2}$ = -13.5 when r = $\frac{3}{2}$

questions.question: If a, b, c are three positive real numbers then (a + b) (b + c) (c + a) is not less than
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c are three positive real numbers then (a + b) (b + c) (c + a) is not less than
questions.explanation:
Given the numbers are a and b, then we have
A.M = A = $\frac{a+b}{2}$, G.M. = G = $\sqrt{a b}$ and A.M > G.M
Now we have $\frac{a+b}{2}>\sqrt{a b}$ ...(i)
$\frac{b+c}{2}>\sqrt{b c}$ ....(ii)
$\frac{c+a}{2}>\sqrt{c a}$ ....(iii)
Multiply (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
$\frac{a+b}{2} \cdot \frac{b+c}{2} \cdot \frac{c+a}{2}>\sqrt{a b} \cdot \sqrt{b c} \cdot \sqrt{c a}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (a + b)(b + c)(c + a) > 8abc

questions.question: In an infinite G.P., each term is equal to the sum of all the terms which follow it. The common ratio of this G.P. is
questions.question_raw: In an infinite G.P., each term is equal to the sum of all the terms which follow it. The common ratio of this G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Let the infinite G.P be $a, a r, a r^{2}, a r^{3}$ ....... with first term a and common ratio r
According to the question $a=a r+a r^{2}+a r^{3}+$....... to $\infty$
$\Rightarrow a=\frac{\bar{a} r}{1-r}$
$\Rightarrow 1-r=r$
$\Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: Sum of an infinite G.P. is $\frac 54$ times the sum of all the odd terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.question_raw: Sum of an infinite G.P. is {tex}\frac 54{/tex} times the sum of all the odd terms. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Consider the infinite G.P a, ar, ar^2, ar^3,.... is a G.P with first term a and common ratio r
Then the odd terms a, ar^2, ar^4,.... is again a G.P with first term a and common ratio r^2 
We have $S_{\infty}=\frac{a}{1-r}$ 
Given $S_{\infty}=\frac{5}{4}$. Sum of odd terms
$\Rightarrow$ a + ar + ar^2 + ar^3 + .... = $\frac{5}{4}$[a + ar^2 + ar^4 + ...]
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{a}{1-r^{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{1-r}=\frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{a}{(1-r)(1+r)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 4(1 + r) = 5
$\Rightarrow$ 4r = 1
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\frac{1}{4}$

questions.question: If a, b, c are in G.P., then
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c are in G.P., then
questions.explanation:
Since a, b, c are in G.P. we get b^2 = ac ....(i)
Consider a(b^2 + c^2)
Now a(b^2 + c^2) = a(ac + c^2) = a^2c + ac^2 = c(a^2 + ac) = c(a^2 + b^2) [using (i)]

questions.question: Two positive numbers are in the ratio$\left( {2 + \sqrt 3 } \right):\left( {2 - \sqrt 3 } \right)$. The ratio of their A.M. to G.M. is :
questions.question_raw: Two positive numbers are in the ratio{tex}\left( {2 + \sqrt 3 } \right):\left( {2 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}. The ratio of their A.M. to G.M. is :
questions.explanation:
Let the two positive numbers be x and y
Now given $\frac{x}{y}=\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{2-\sqrt{3}}$ 
So let x = $(2+\sqrt{3}) k$ and y = $(2-\sqrt{3}) k$ 
$\therefore$ $\begin{array}{lllll} A . M \ of\ \text {and} \ y \\ \hline G . M \ o f \ x \ \text {and} \ y \end{array}$ = $\frac{\frac{x+y}{2}}{\sqrt{x y}}=\frac{x+y}{2 \sqrt{x y}}=\frac{(2+\sqrt{3}) k+(2-\sqrt{3}) k}{2 \sqrt{(2+\sqrt{3}) k \cdot(2-\sqrt{3}) k}}$ 
= $\frac{4 k}{2 \sqrt{(4-3) k^{2}}}=\frac{4 k}{2 k}=\frac{2}{1}$ [$\because$ (a + b) (a - b) = a^2 - b^2]

questions.question: All the terms of a G.P. are squared. The new sequence thus formed is
questions.question_raw: All the terms of a G.P. are squared. The new sequence thus formed is
questions.explanation:
We have a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ....., ar^n-1 is a G.P with first term a and common ratio r
Now if we square each term of this we get the sequence a^2, a^2r^2, a^2r^4, a^2r^6...., a^2r^n-1, which is again a G.P with first term a^2 and common ratio r^2

questions.question: The least value of n for which 1 + 3 + 3^2 + 3^3 + .... + 3^n-1 > 1000 is
questions.question_raw: The least value of n for which 1 + 3 + 32 + 33 + .... + 3n-1 > 1000 is
questions.explanation:
We have 1, 3, 3^2, 3^3, ...., 3^n-1 is a G.P with first term 1 and common ratio r = 3
Now 1 + 3 + 3^2 + 3^3 + .... + 3^n-1 = S_n = $\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{r-1}=\frac{1\left(3^{n}-1\right)}{3-1}$ 
Hence 1 + 3 + 3^2 + 3^3 + ... + 3^n-1 > 100 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{3^{n}-1}{2}$ > 1000
$\Rightarrow$ 3^n > 2001
But we have 3^6 = 729 and 3^7 = 2187
Therefore least value of n = 7

questions.question: The cotangents of $\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{\pi}{6}$ are in
questions.question_raw: The cotangents of {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} are in
questions.explanation:
We have $\cot \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, $\cot \frac{\pi}{4}=1$ and $\cot \frac{\pi}{3}=\sqrt{3}$ 
Clearly $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, 1, \sqrt{3}$ are terms of a G.P. with first term $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and common ratio r = $\sqrt{3}$

questions.question: If one A.M. A and two geometric means B and C be inserted between two numbers, then ${B^3} + {C^3}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If one A.M. A and two geometric means B and C be inserted between two numbers, then {tex}{B^3} + {C^3}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the 2 numbers be x and y.
Since A is the A.M between the numbers x and y, we get A = $\frac{x+y}{2}$ ...(i)
Since B and C are the geometric means between x and y, we get x = x, B = xr and C = xr^2, y = xr^3 where r is the common ratio of the G.
Now $\frac{B^{3}+C^{3}}{B C}=\frac{B^{2}}{C}+\frac{C^{2}}{B}$ = $\frac{(x r)^{2}}{x r^{2}}+\frac{\left(x r^{2}\right)^{2}}{x r}$ = x + xr^3 = x + y = 2A from equation (i)
Hence we get B^3 + C^3 = 2ABC

questions.question: In a G.P. the ratio of the sum of first three terms to the sum of first six terms is 125 : 152. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.question_raw: In a G.P. the ratio of the sum of first three terms to the sum of first six terms is 125 : 152. The common ratio of the G.P. is
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{S_{3}}{S_{6}}=\frac{125}{152}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{\frac{a\left(r^{3}-1\right)}{r-1}}{\frac{a\left(r^{6}-1\right)}{r-1}}=\frac{125}{152}, r-1 \neq 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{r^{3}-1}{r^{6}-1}=\frac{125}{152}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 152r^3 - 15^2 = 125r^6 - 125
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^6 - 152r^3 + 27 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^6 - 125r^3 - 27r^3 + 27 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 125r^3(r^3 - 1) - 27(r^3 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (125r^3 - 27)(r^3 - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ r^3 = $\frac{27}{125}$ or r^3 = 1
Since r - 1 $\ne$ 0, r cannot be 1
$\Rightarrow$ r = $\frac{3}{5}$

questions.question: In a G.P. (m + n)^th and (m – n)^th terms are respectively p and q. Its m^th term is
questions.question_raw: In a G.P. (m + n)th and (m – n)th terms are respectively p and q. Its mth term is
questions.explanation:
Let a be the first term and r be the common ratio of the G.P
Then since T_n = a^n-1, we get
T_m+n = p $\Rightarrow$ ar^m+n-1 = p ....(i)
T_m-n = q $\Rightarrow$ ar^m-n-1 = q ...(ii)
Now dividing equation (i) by (ii)
$\frac { ar^{ m+n-1 } }{ ar^{ m-n-1 } } =\frac { p }{ q } \Rightarrow$ r^(m + n - 1) - (m - n - 1) = $\frac pq$
$\Rightarrow$ $r2n = \frac { p }{ q }$
Taking square root on both sides we get r^n = $\sqrt { \frac { p }{ q } }$ ....(iii)
Substituting equation (iii) in (i), we get 
$a.\sqrt { \frac { p }{ q } } .r^{ m-1 }$ = p
$\Rightarrow a.r^{ m-1 }=p.\sqrt { \frac { q }{ p } } =\sqrt { pq } $

questions.question: If a, b, c are in A. P., a, mb, c are in G.P., then a, m^2b, c are in
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c are in A. P., a, mb, c are in G.P., then a, m2b, c are in
questions.explanation:
If the numbers a, b, c are in A.P. we have b = $\frac{a+c}{2}$ ....(i)
Since a, mb, c are in G.P. we get (mb)^2 = ac ....(ii)
Now m^2b = $\frac{2(m b)^{2}}{2 b}=\frac{2 a c}{a+c}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ a, m^2b, c are in H.P.

questions.question: If the sum of first n natural numbers is one-fifth of the sum of their squares, then n equals
questions.question_raw: If the sum of first n natural numbers is one-fifth of the sum of their squares, then n equals
questions.explanation:
According to question,
The sum of n natural no. = $\frac{1}{5}$ times of sum of their squares
So, 
$\frac{n(n+1)}{2}=\frac{1}{5} \frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 15 = 2n+ 1
$\Rightarrow$ n = 7

questions.question: The point which divides the joint of ( 1, 2 ) and ( 3,4 ) externally in the ratio 1 : 1 .

questions.explanation:
The point which divides the line in the ratio m:n externally is given by x =$m(x_2) -n(x_1)\over m - n$
Substituting the values we get,
x = $ 1(3) - 1(1)\over 1-1$ which is undefined.

questions.question: The distance of the point (x, y) from the origin is
questions.question_raw: The distance of the point (x, y) from the origin is
questions.explanation:
Distance between two points is given by $\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 +(y_2-y_1)^2}$ 
Therefore the distance from the origin to the point (x, y) is $\sqrt{(x-0)^2 +(y-0)^2}$ = $\sqrt{x^2 +y^2}$

questions.question: The distance of the point ($\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $) from X axis is

questions.question: The line through the points (a, b) and (- a, - b) passes through the point
questions.question_raw: The line through the points (a, b) and (- a, - b) passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Equation of a line passing through (x_1, y_1) and (x_2, y_2) is given by
$y-y_1 \over y_2-y_1$=$x - x_1\over x_2-x_1$ 
Substituting the given values we get,
$y-b\over -b-b$^ =^ $x-a \over -a-a$ 
That is $y-b \over -2b $ = $x-a \over -2a$ 
On cross multiplying and rearranging we get,
bx - ay = 0
If this passes through the given point (a^2, ab) then
b(a^2) - a(ab) = 0

questions.question: Projection (the foot of perpendicular) from (x, y) on the x-axis is
questions.question_raw: Projection (the foot of perpendicular) from (x, y) on the x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Let L be the foot of the perpendicular from the X-axis. Therefore its y coordinate is zero.
Therefore the coordinates of the point L is (x, 0).

questions.question: The image of the point ($\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta $) with respect to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The image of the point ({tex}\alpha {\rm{ }},{\rm{ }}\beta {/tex}) with respect to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line which is perpendicular to the given line x + y = 0 is x - y = 0
The point of intersection of these two lines is (0, 0) which is the origin
This point of intersection is the midpoint of the given point ($\alpha,\beta$) and its image, say (a, b)
Therefore $\alpha +a\over 2$ 
This implies a = $-\alpha$ 
Similarly $\beta+b\over 2$ = 0
This implies b = $-\beta$ 
Therefore $(-\alpha, -\beta)$ is the image.

questions.question: The distance between the lines 5x – 12y + 65 = 0 and 5x – 12y – 39 = 0
questions.question_raw: The distance between the lines 5x – 12y + 65 = 0 and 5x – 12y – 39 = 0
questions.explanation:
To find the distance between two parallel lines given by the equations $A x+B y+C_1=0$ and $A x+B y+C_2=0$, we can use the formula:
$ \text { Distance }=\frac{\left|C_2-C_1\right|}{\sqrt{A^2+B^2}} $
For the lines $5 x-12 y+65=0$ and $5 x-12 y-39=0$ : - Here, $A=5, B=-12, C_1=65$, and $C_2=-39$. Now, we calculate the distance:
$ \text { Distance }=\frac{|-39-65|}{\sqrt{5^2+(-12)^2}}=\frac{|-104|}{\sqrt{25+144}}=\frac{104}{\sqrt{169}}=\frac{104}{13}=8 $

options.label: A
options.text: parallel to the line x + y = 0

options.label: B
options.text: parallel to the line x – y = 0

questions.explanation:
The vertical lines which are parallel to Y-axis have undefined slopes. Hence the slope of the line 'm' will be undefined.
Therefore the above equation of the line will represent all lines through (x_1, y_1) except the line parallel to Y-axis.

questions.explanation:
Since the angle made with x-axis is zero since tan $\theta$ is the slope. Then tan $\theta$ = 0
Hence the slope of the line parallel to the X-axis is zero.

questions.question: The distance between the parallel lines 3x + 4y + 13 = 0 and 3x + 4y - 13 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The distance between the parallel lines 3x + 4y + 13 = 0 and 3x + 4y - 13 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Distance between parallel lines is given by $|C_1-C_2| \over \sqrt {A^2+B^2} $ 
Now substituting the values we get, $|13-(-13)|\over \sqrt{3^2+4^2}$ = $26\over 5$

questions.question: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
The lines formed by these lines is a right-angled, triangle.
Two lines are perpendicular to each other if the product of their slopes is -1.
Slope of the lines 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 are 3 and $\frac{-1}{3}$ respectively.
The product of these slopes is -1
Hence the lines 3x - y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 are perpendicular to each other.
Therefore the triangle formed by these lines is a right-angled triangle.

questions.question: The lines 2x – 3y = 5 and 6x – 9y – 7 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The lines 2x – 3y = 5 and 6x – 9y – 7 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
The given lines are parallel lines condition for the line to be parallel is $a_1\over a_2$= $b_1\over b_2$ $\ne $$C_1\over C_2$ 
Substituting the values,
$2\over 6$ =$-3\over -9$$\ne$$5\over 7$ 
Hence they are parallel lines.

questions.question: The equation of the line which passes through the point (1, - 2) and cuts off equal intercepts from the axis is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the line which passes through the point (1, - 2) and cuts off equal intercepts from the axis is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the line which has equal intercepts be 
$x\over a$ + $y\over a$ = 1
Substituting the values for x and y
1 - 2 = a
Therefore a = -1
Hence the equation of the line is x + y + 1 = 0

questions.question: If a line is drawn through the origin and parallel to the line x - 2y + 5 = 0, its equation is
questions.question_raw: If a line is drawn through the origin and parallel to the line x - 2y + 5 = 0, its equation is
questions.explanation:
If the line is parallel to the given line x - 2y + 5 = 0, then the required line will have the same slope.
Hence the equaion of the given line is x - 2y + k = 0
Since it passes through the origin,
0 + 0 + k = 0
Therefore k = 0
Hence the equation of the required line is x - 2y = 0 or x = 2y

questions.question: The line passing through (1, 1) and parallel to the line 2x – 3y + 5 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The line passing through (1, 1) and parallel to the line 2x – 3y + 5 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The required line is parallel to the given line, hence it has same slope.
Therefore the equation of the required line is 2x - 3y + k = 0
Since it passes through (1, 1)
2(1) - 3(1) + k = 0
Therefore k = 1
Hence the equation of the required line is 2x - 3y + 1 = 0

questions.question: The equation of the line which passes through the point (2, -3) and cuts off equal intercepts from the axis is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the line which passes through the point (2, -3) and cuts off equal intercepts from the axis is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line which cuts equal intercepts is x + y = a 
Since it passes through (2, -3), 2 - 3 = a
Hence a = -1
Hence the equation of the required line is x + y +1 = 0

questions.question: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
The triangle formed by these lines is a right-angled triangle.
If the lines are perpendicular to each other, then the product of their slopes is -1
The slope of lines 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 are -3 and $\frac{1}{3}$ respectively.
The product of the slopes is -1.
Hence these two lines are perpendicular to each other.
This infers that the triangle formed by these lines is a right angled triangle.

questions.question: The distance between the parallel lines 4x + 3y + 11 = 0 and 8x + 6y = 15 is
questions.question_raw: The distance between the parallel lines 4x + 3y + 11 = 0 and 8x + 6y = 15 is
questions.explanation:
Distance between two parallel lines is given by $|c_1-c_2|\over \sqrt{A^2+B^2}$ 
c_1 = 2 $\times$ 11 = 22 and c_2 = 15 and A = 8 and B = 6
Now substituting the values we get
$|22 - 15|\over\sqrt{36+64}$ = $\frac{7}{10}$

questions.question: The line which passes through the point ( 0 , 1 ) and perpendicular to the line x – 2y + 11 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The line which passes through the point ( 0 , 1 ) and perpendicular to the line x – 2y + 11 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The line which is perpendicular to the given line is 2x + y + k = 0
Since it passes through (0,1)
2(0) + 1 + k = 0
This implies k = -1
Hence the equation of the required line is 2x + y - 1 = 0

questions.question: The perpendicular distance of the origin from the line 3x + 4y + 1 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The perpendicular distance of the origin from the line 3x + 4y + 1 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The perpendicular distance from the origin to the line is given by $Ax_1 +By_1+C\over \sqrt{A^2+B^2}$ 
For the given line c = 1, A = 3 and B = 4 and since it passes through the origin,
Substituting the values we get,
$1\over \sqrt{9+16}$ = $\frac{1}{5}$

questions.question: The acute angle between the lines x – y = 0 and y = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The acute angle between the lines x – y = 0 and y = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Slope of the line x - y = 0 is 1. This implies the tan$\theta$ = 1 Hence, the angle made by the line with X-axis is 45^o
The angle made by the line y = 0 with the Y-axis is 90^o. Therefore the acute angle between the lines is 45^o.

questions.question: The vertices of a triangle are (0, 3), (-3, 0) and (3, 0). The orthocenter of the triangle is
questions.question_raw: The vertices of a triangle are (0, 3), (-3, 0) and (3, 0). The orthocenter of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
From the given points it is clear that these points form a right-angled isosceles triangle.
The orthocentre of all right angle lies on its right-angle vertex.
Here the right angle vertex is (0, 3)
Hence the orthocentre is (0, 3)

questions.question: The lines x + ( k - 1)y + 1 = 0 and 2x + k^2y - 1 = 0 are at right angles if
questions.question_raw: The lines x + ( k - 1)y + 1 = 0 and 2x + k2y - 1 = 0 are at right angles if
questions.explanation:
If the lines are at right angles to each other, then the product of their slopes = -1. Slope of any line = -(coefficient of x/coeffiecient of y)
Therefore the slope of line 1 = -$\frac{1}{k-1}$
The slope of line 2 = $\frac{-2}{k^2}$
Therefore $-1 \over (k-1)$ $\times$ $-2 \over k^2$ = - 1
That is k^2 (k - 1) = -2
i.e; k^3 - k^2 + 2 = 0
On factorizing we get (k + 1)(k^2 - 2k - 2) = 0
This imlpies k + 1 is a factor, hence k = -1
Hence they are at right angles if k = -1

questions.question: A line is equally inclined to the axis and the length of perpendicular from the origin upon the line is $\sqrt 2 $. A possible equation of the line is
questions.question_raw: A line is equally inclined to the axis and the length of perpendicular from the origin upon the line is {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex}. A possible equation of the line is
questions.explanation:
Since the line is equally inclined, the slope of the line should be -1, because it makes 135^o in the positive direction of the X-axis.
This implies the equation of the line is y = -x + c 
i.e; x + y - c = 0
distance of the line from the origin is given as $\sqrt 2$ 
Therefore $\sqrt 2$ = $|c|\over \sqrt{1^2+1^2}$ 
This implies c = 2
Hence, the equation of the line is x + y = 2

questions.question: The lines y = mx, y + 2x = 0, y = 2x + $\lambda $ and y = - mx + $\lambda $ form a rhombus if m =
questions.question_raw: The lines y = mx, y + 2x = 0, y = 2x + {tex}\lambda {/tex} and y = - mx + {tex}\lambda {/tex} form a rhombus if m =
questions.explanation:
Rhombus is a parallelogram in which the opposite sides are equal and parallel.
Therefore the lines y = mx and y = -2x are parallel, similarly y = 2x + $\lambda$ and y = -mx + $\lambda$ are parallel.
If two lines are equal, then their slopes are equal.
This implies m = -2

questions.question: Two points (a, 0) and (0, b) are joined by a straight line. Another point on this line is
questions.question_raw: Two points (a, 0) and (0, b) are joined by a straight line. Another point on this line is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the line joining the points (a, 0) and (0, b) is [$\frac{[b-0]}{[0-a]}$ = -($\frac{b}{a}$)
Hence the equation of the line is y = (-$\frac{b}{a}$)x + b
i.e; ay = -bx + ab
Substituting the x coordinate 3a in the place of x in the above equation we get y = -2b
Hence (3a, -2b) is another point on the line.

questions.question: The coordinates of the foot of perpendicular from (0, 0) upon the line x + y = 2 are
questions.question_raw: The coordinates of the foot of perpendicular from (0, 0) upon the line x + y = 2 are
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line perpendicular to the given line is x - y + k = 0
Since it passes through the origin, 
0 - 0 + k = 0
Therefore, k = 0
Hence the equation of the line is x - y = 0
On solving these two equations we get x = 1 and y = 1
The point of intersection of these two lines is (1, 1)
Hence the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 1)

questions.question: The area of the triangle whose sides are along the lines x = 0, y = 0 and 4x + 5y = 20 is
questions.question_raw: The area of the triangle whose sides are along the lines x = 0, y = 0 and 4x + 5y = 20 is
questions.explanation:
The equation 4x + 5y = 20 can be written as $x \over 5$ + $y \over4$ = 1
This implies the intercepts cut by this line on the X and Y axes are 5 and 4 respectively.
Hence the area of the triangle is $\frac{1}{2}$[5 $\times$ 4] = 10 square units.

questions.question: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is – 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.question_raw: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is – 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the given line is $y_2 - y_1 \over x_2-x_1$ = $6-4 \over 5 -3$ = 1
Therefore $4-(-1) \over 3-x$ = 1
That is 4 + 1 = 3 - x
Therefore x = -2

questions.question: The line x + y – 6 = 0 is the right bisector of the segment [PQ]. If P is the point (4, 3), then the point Q is
questions.question_raw: The line x + y – 6 = 0 is the right bisector of the segment [PQ]. If P is the point (4, 3), then the point Q is
questions.explanation:
Equation of the line which is perpendicular to the given line x + y = 6 is x - y + k = 0
Since it passes through the point (4, 3)
4 - 3 + k = 0
Therefore k = -1
Hence the equation of the line segment PQ is x - y - 1 = 0
On solving these two lines we get x = $\frac{7}{2}$ and y = $\frac{5}{2}$
This point of intersection is the midpoint of the line segment PQ.
That is $4+x \over 2$ = $\frac{7}{2}$ Hence x = 3
Similarly $3+y \over 2$ = $\frac{5}{2}$. Hence y = 2
Hence the coordinates of the point Q is (3, 2).

questions.question: The acute angle between the lines ax + by + c = 0 and (a + b)x = (a – b)y , a $ \ne $b , is
questions.question_raw: The acute angle between the lines ax + by + c = 0 and (a + b)x = (a – b)y , a {tex} \ne {/tex}b , is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the first line is -($\frac{a}{b}$) and slope of the second line is $a+b \over a-b$ 
Angle between the lines is $tan\theta$ = $|m_2 - m_1|\over 1+ m_1m_2$ 
Let m_1 = -($\frac{a}{b}$) and m_2 = $a+b \over a-b$ 
Hence $tan \theta$ =[ $(-a/b) - (a+b)/a-b)\over1 +(-a/b)(a+b)/(a-b)$]
On simplifying we get, 
tan $ \theta$ = 1
This implies $\theta$ = 45^o

questions.question: The lines x + 2y – 3 = 0, 2x + y – 3 = 0 and the line l are concurrent. If the line I passes through the origin, then its equation is
questions.question_raw: The lines x + 2y – 3 = 0, 2x + y – 3 = 0 and the line l are concurrent. If the line I passes through the origin, then its equation is
questions.explanation:
Equation of a line passing through the intersection of two lies is given by ax_1 + by_1 +c_1 + k(ax_2 + by_2 + c_2) = 0
Hence x + 2y - 3 + k(2x + y - 3) = 0
Since it passes through (0, 0)
-3 -3k = 0
This implies k = -1
Sustituting for k we get,
x + 2y - 3 +(-1)(2x + y - 3) = 0
-x + y = 0 or x - y = 0

questions.question: The triangle formed by the lines x + y = 1, 2x + 3y – 6 = 0 and 4x – y + 4 = 0 lies in
questions.question_raw: The triangle formed by the lines x + y = 1, 2x + 3y – 6 = 0 and 4x – y + 4 = 0 lies in
questions.explanation:
On solving line 1 and line 2 we get x = -3 and y = 4. Hence the point of intersection is (-3, 4)
On solving line 2 and line 3 we get x = ($\frac{-3}{7}$) and y = $\frac{16}{7}$. Hence the point of intersection is ($\frac{-3}{7}$, $\frac{16}{7}$)
On solving line 3 and line 1 we get x = $\frac{-3}{7}$ and y = $\frac{-8}{7}$. Hence the point of intersection is ($\frac{-3}{7}$, $\frac{-8}{7}$)
All the above points lie in the second quadrant. Hence the triangle formed by these lines also lie in the second quadrant.

questions.question: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.question_raw: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.explanation:
Area of the triangle formed by the coordinates (x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2) and (x_3, y_3) is given by
$1\over 2$[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)]
On solving the lines 1 and 2, the point of intersection is (0, 0)
On solving the lines 2 and 3, the point of intersection is (4, 8)
On solving the line 3 and 1 , the point of intersection is (-2, -2)
Now substituting the values to find the area of the triangle,
Area = $1\over 2$| [0 + 4(-2 - 0) + (-2)(0 - 8)] |
= 4 sq units

questions.question: If the points representing the complex numbers - 4 +3i, 2 – 3i and 0 + pi are collinear, then the value of p is
questions.question_raw: If the points representing the complex numbers - 4 +3i, 2 – 3i and 0 + pi are collinear, then the value of p is
questions.explanation:
Let us take the coordinates as (-4, 3), (2, -3) and (0, p).
If the points are collienear the $1\over 2$|x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)| = 0
Now substituting the values |-4(-3 - p) +2(p - 3) + 0(3 + 3)|= 0
12 + 4p + 2p - 6 + 0 = 0
6p +6 = 0
6p = -6
Therefore p = -1

questions.question: The lines lx + my + n = 0, mx + ny + l = 0 and nx + ly + m = 0 are concurrent if
questions.question_raw: The lines lx + my + n = 0, mx + ny + l = 0 and nx + ly + m = 0 are concurrent if
questions.explanation:
The required condition for concurrency is a_3(b_1c_2 - b_2c_1) + b_3(c_1a_2 - c_2a_1) + c_3(a_1b_2 - a_2b_1) = 0
Here a_1 = l, a_2 = m, a_3 = n and b_1 = m, b_2 = n, b_3 = l and c_1 = n, c_2 = l and c_3 = m
Substituting the values we get
n(ml - n^2) + l(nm - l^2) + m(ln - m^2) = 0
This implies l^3 + m^3 + n^3 - 3lmn = 0
That is (l + m+ n)(l^2 + m^2 + n^2 - lm - mn - nl) = 0
This implies l + m + n = 0

questions.question: The equations of the lines through (-1, -1) and making angles of 45^o with the line x + y = 0 are

The lines x + 1 = 0 and y + 1 = 0 are perpendicular to each other.

Hence the angle made by this line with respect to X-axis is 45^o
In other words, the angle made by this line with x + 1 = 0 is 45^o
Clearly the other line with which it can make 45^o is y + 1 = 0

questions.question: Given the 4 lines with equations x + 2y – 3 = 0, 2x + 3y – 4 = 0, 3x + 4y – 5 = 0, 4x + 5y – 6 = 0, then these lines are

Similarly solving lines 3 and 4, the point of intersection is (-1, 2)
On solving lines 1 and 4 the point of intersection is (-1, 2)
Since the point of intersection is the same for all the lines, the lines are concurrent.

questions.question: The orthogonal projection of the point (2, - 3) on the line x + y = 0 is

This implies k = -5

questions.question: The lines 8x + 4y = 1, 8x + 4y = 5, 4x + 8y = 3, 4x + 8y = 7 form a
questions.question_raw: The lines 8x + 4y = 1, 8x + 4y = 5, 4x + 8y = 3, 4x + 8y = 7 form a
questions.explanation:
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 1 and 4x + 8y = 3, we get the point of intersection as ($\frac{-1}{2}$, $\frac{5}{12}$)
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 5 and 4x + 8y = 7, we get the point of intersection as ($\frac{1}{4}$, $\frac{3}{4}$)
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 1 and 4x + 8y = 7, we get the point of intersection as ($\frac{-5}{12}$, $\frac{13}{12}$)
On solving the equations 8x + 4y = 5 and 4x + 8y = 3, we the point of intersection as ($\frac{7}{12}$, $\frac{1}{12}$)
Let the points A(($\frac{-1}{2}$, $\frac{5}{12}$), B($\frac{7}{12}$, $\frac{1}{12}$) C($\frac{1}{4}$, $\frac{3}{4}$) and D($\frac{-5}{12}$, $\frac{13}{12}$) be the vertices of the quadrilateral
Since the slopes of the opposite sides are equal the quadrilateral is a parallelogram
The slope of the diagonal AC is $5/12 - 3/4\over -1/2 - 1/4$ = 1
The slope of the diagonal BD is $1/2 - 13/12\over 7/12 - (-5/12)$ = -1
Since the product of the slopes is -1, the diagonals are perpendicular to each other.
Hence the parallelogram is a rhombus.

questions.question: The number of points on X-axis which are at a distance of c units (c < 3) from (2, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The number of points on X-axis which are at a distance of c units (c < 3) from (2, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Let (x, 0) be the point.
Then, by distance formula we have
(x - 2)^2 + 3^2 = c^2
x^2 - 4x + 4 + 9 - c^2 = 0
x^2 - 4x + 13 - c^2 = 0
x will be real if the b^2 - 4ac $\ge$0
i.e; 16 - 4(13 - c^2) $\ge$ 0
i.e; c^2 - 9 $\ge$ 0
i.e; |c| $\ge$ 3
Hence there is no point.

questions.question: The equations of the lines through (1, 1) and making angles of 45^o with the line x + y = 0

questions.question: The number of lines that are parallel to 2x + 6y – 7 = 0 and have an intercept 10 units between the coordinate axis is :
questions.question_raw: The number of lines that are parallel to 2x + 6y – 7 = 0 and have an intercept 10 units between the coordinate axis is :
questions.explanation:
The slope of the given line 2x + 6y = 7 is $\frac{-1}{3}$
Hence the line which is parallel to the above line is 
y = ($\frac{-1}{3}$)x + c
That is the y-intercept is (0, c) and the x-intercept is (3c, 0)
Using the distance formula
d^2 = (0 - 3c)^2 + (3c - 0)^2
= 10c^2
Since the distance is given as 10, then
100 = 10c^2
Therefore c = $ \pm$10
Since two values are possible, two lines can be drawn.

questions.question: The line (p + 2q)x + (p – 3q)y = p - q for different values of p and q passes through the fixed point
questions.question_raw: The line (p + 2q)x + (p – 3q)y = p - q for different values of p and q passes through the fixed point
questions.explanation:
Expanding the given equation
px + 2qx + py - 3qy = p - q
px + py + 2qx - 3qy = p - q
p(x + y) - q(- 2x + 3y) = p - q
Equating the coeffiecients of like terms
x + y = 1 and - 2x + 3y = 1
On solving both the equations we get,
x = $\frac{2}{5}$ and y = $\frac{3}{5}$
Hence the line passes through the fixed point ($\frac{2}{5}$, $\frac{3}{5}$)

questions.question: The area of the quadrilateral formed by the lines |x| + |y| = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The area of the quadrilateral formed by the lines |x| + |y| = 1 is

Equations of the lines are
x $\ge$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, then x + y = 1
x $ \le$ 0, y $\le$ 0, then x + y = -1
x $\ge$ 0, y $\le$ 0, then x - y = 2
x $\le$ 0, y $\ge$ 0, then x - y = -2
Clearly these lines form a square, whose coordinates are (1, 1), (1, -1),(-1, -1), (-1, 1)
Hence its area is 4 $\times$ [$\frac{1}{2}$]1 $\times$ 1 = 2 sq units

questions.question: The line segment joining the points (1, 2) and (-2, 1) is divided by the line 3x + 4y = 7 in the ratio

Let the line divide in the ratio k:1
By applying section formula $k.x_2 -1.x_1\over k+1$, $k.y_2-1.y_1\over k+1$
Substituting the values
$-2k+1\over k+1$, $k+2\over k+1$
This given line 3x + 4y = 7 passes through this point.
Hence, 3($-2k+1\over k+1$) + 4($k+2\over k+1$ )= 7
On simplifying, we get,

questions.question: A line L passes through the points (1, 1) and (2, 0) and another line M which is perpendicular to L passes through the point (1/2, 0). The area of the triangle formed by these lines with y axis is :
questions.question_raw: A line L passes through the points (1, 1) and (2, 0) and another line M which is perpendicular to L passes through the point (1/2, 0). The area of the triangle formed by these lines with y axis is :
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line joining the two points (x_1, y_1) and (x_2, y_2) is
$y - y_1 \over y_2 - y_1$ = $x-x_1\over x_2 - x_1$
The given points are (1, 1) and (2, 0)
On substituting the values we get
$y-1\over 0-1$ = $x-1\over 2-1 $
On simplifying we get,
x + y - 2 = 0
The line which is perpendicular to this line is x - y + k = 0
Since it passes through (1/2, 0)
(1/2) - 0 = k
This implies k = -1/2
Hence the equation of this line is x - y - 1/2 = 0
On solving these twolines we get the point of intersection as (5/4, 3/4)
The point which line x + y - 2 = 0 cuts the Y axis is (0, 2) and the point which the line x - y - 1/2 = 0 cuts the Y axis is (0, -1/2)
Hend e the area of the triangle = [1/2] x [5/4] x [5/4] = 25/16 squnits

questions.question: The medians AD and BE of a triangle with vertices A ( 0 , b ) , B ( 0, 0 ) and C ( a , 0 )are perpendicular to each other if
questions.question_raw: The medians AD and BE of a triangle with vertices A ( 0 , b ) , B ( 0, 0 ) and C ( a , 0 )are perpendicular to each other if
questions.explanation:
Let D be the midpoint of BC. Hence its midpoint is ($0+a\over2$,0) = ($a\over2$,0)
Let E be the midpoint of AC. Hence its midpoint is ($0+a\over2$,$0+b\over2$) = ($a\over2$,$b\over2$)
Slope of the median AD is $b-0\over (a/2)-0$ = -$2b\over a$
Sope of BE = $(a/2)-0\over(b/2)-0$= $b\over a$
If BE and AD are perpendicular to each other then their product is -1
i.e;- $2b\over a$ x $b\over a$ = -1
This implies 2b^2 = a^2
a = $\pm\sqrt2$b

questions.question: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x + 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is

On solving the lines 2 and 3 we get, the point of intersection as (2, -2)
On solving lines 3 and 1 we get the point of intersection as (1, 1)
Now using distance formula we get AB = $\sqrt{(2-(-2))^2+(-2-2)^2}$ = $\sqrt 8$ 
Similarly, BC = $\sqrt{(1-2)^2+(1-(-2))^2}$ = $\sqrt{10}$ 
Similarly, AC = $\sqrt{(-2-1)^2+(2-1)^2}$= $\sqrt {10}$ 
Since AB = AC, it is an isosceles triangle.

questions.question: The line which is parallel to X-axis and crosses the curve y = $\sqrt x $ at an angle of 45^o is:

Since it makes and angle of 45^o, m = 1
y^2 = x implies a = $\frac{1}{4}$
Hence the equation of the tangent is y = x + $\frac 14$
That is the y-intercept is $\sqrt \frac 14$ = $\frac{1}{2} $
Hence the equation of the line is y = $\frac{1}{2} $

questions.question: The points (- a, -b), (0, 0), (a, b) and (a^2, ab) are
questions.question_raw: The points (- a, -b), (0, 0), (a, b) and (a2, ab) are

Consider the points (a, b), (0, 0), (-a, -b)
It is clear that (0, 0) is the midpoint of (a, b) and (-a, -b)
This implies that these three points are collinear
Now consider the points,
(a, b), (a^2, ab) and (-a, -b)
If three points are collinear then the area of the triangle formed by these points is zero.
That is $1\over 2$[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)]= 0
Substituting the values
$1\over 2$[a(a + b) + a^2(-b - b) + (-a)(b - ab)]
On expanding and simplifying we get the value to be zero.
Hence these points are collinear.
