questions.question_raw: The ortho centre of the triangle formed by lines xy = 0 and x + y = 1 is :
questions.explanation: Given xy = 0 and x + y = 1, indicates that the straight line passes through (1, 0) and (0, 1). Hence it is a right-angled triangle whose vertex is the origin. Therefore the orthocenter is also at the origin (0, 0), this is one of the properties of right-angled triangle whose vertex is the origin.

questions.question: The straight-line 3x + y = 9 divides the line segment joining the points (1, 3) and (2, 7) in the ratio
questions.question_raw: The straight-line 3x + y = 9 divides the line segment joining the points (1, 3) and (2, 7) in the ratio
questions.explanation:
Let the point be (x, y) and the ratio it divides (1, 3) and (2, 7) be k : 1
By section formula, x = $2k+1\over k+1$, y = $7k+3\over k+1$ 
his point also lies on the given line so it would satisfy the equation 3x + y - 9 = 0
3(2k + 1) + (7k + 3) - 9(k + 1) = 0
k = $\frac{3}{4}$
Hence the ratio is ratio = 3 : 4

questions.question: In a $\Delta ABC,$ if A is the point (1, 2) and equations of the median through B and C are respectively x + y = 5 and x = 4, then B is
questions.question_raw: In a {tex}\Delta ABC,{/tex} if A is the point (1, 2) and equations of the median through B and C are respectively x + y = 5 and x = 4, then B is
questions.explanation:
Let F be the midpoint on AC
Hence the coordinate of F is $\frac{x_1 + 1}{2}$ and $\frac{5-x_1 + 2}{2}$
This point lies on x = 4
Therefore $\frac{x_1 + 1}{2}$ = 4
Therefore x_1 = 7 
Therefore y = 5 - 7 = -2
Hence the coordinates of B is (7, -2)

questions.question: The ratio of the ${{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{th}}}}$ to the (n - 1)th mean between 1 and 31, when n arithmetic means are inserted between them, is 5 : 9. The value of n is
questions.question_raw: The ratio of the {tex}{{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{th}}}}{/tex} to the (n - 1)th mean between 1 and 31, when n arithmetic means are inserted between them, is 5 : 9. The value of n is
questions.explanation:
Let A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4 ..., A_n be the n arithmetic means inserted between 1 and 31.
The we have 1, A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4 ..., A_n, 31 is an A.P with a = 1, T_n = 31 and number of terms = n + 2
Now, T_n = 31 $\Rightarrow$ 1 + [(n + 2) - 1]d = 31
$\Rightarrow$ d = $\frac{30}{n+1}$
Hence we get T_7 = a + 7d = 1 + 7$\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]$ ...(i)
And T_n - 1 = a(n - 1) + d = 1 + (n - 1)$\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]$ ...(ii)
Given $\frac{T_{7}}{T_{n-1}}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+7\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]}{1+(n-1)\left[\frac{30}{n+1}\right]}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{n+1+210}{n+1}}{\frac{31 n-29}{n+1}}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\Rightarrow$ 9n + 1899 = 155n - 145
$\Rightarrow$ 2044 = 146 n
$\Rightarrow$ n = $\frac{2044}{146}$ = 14

questions.question: The point on the axis of y which is equidistant from (- 1, 2) and (3, 4) is
questions.question_raw: The point on the axis of y which is equidistant from (- 1, 2) and (3, 4) is
questions.explanation:
Let (0, y) be the point on Y-axis which is equidistant from the points (-1, 2) and (3, 4)
By applying the distance formula,
(0 + 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = (3 - 0)^2 + (4 - y)^2
on simplifying we get
4y = 20
Therefore y = 5
Hence the point on the y-axis is (0, 5)

questions.question: Given the three straight lines with equations 5x + 4y = 0, x + 2y – 10 = 0 and 2x + y + 5 = 0, then these lines are
questions.question_raw: Given the three straight lines with equations 5x + 4y = 0, x + 2y – 10 = 0 and 2x + y + 5 = 0, then these lines are

options.label: A
options.text: the sides of a right angled triangle

options.label: B
options.text: the sides of an equilateral triangle

options.label: C
options.text: the sides of an isosceles triangle

questions.explanation:
The lines are said to be concurrent $\begin{vmatrix} 5 & 4 & 0\\ 1 & 2 & -10\\ 2 & 1 & 5\\ \end{vmatrix} $ = 0
On expanding we get 
5(10 + 10) -4(5 + 20) + 0 = 0
Hence the lines are concurrent.

questions.question: A line passes through (2,2) and is perpendicular to the line 3x+y=3, then its y intercept is
questions.question_raw: A line passes through (2,2) and is perpendicular to the line 3x+y=3, then its y intercept is
questions.explanation:
The line which is perpendicular to the given line is x-3y+k=0
This passes through the point (2,2)
Substituting the values,
2-3(2)+k=0
k = 4
Hence the equation of the line is x-3y+4=0
This can be written as $x\over-4$+$y\over4/3$ = 1
Hence the y intercept is 4/3

questions.question: The angle between the two straight lines 6y^2 - xy - x^2 + 30y + 36 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The angle between the two straight lines 6y2 - xy - x2 + 30y + 36 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Consider the equation -x^2 + xy + 6y^2 = 0 or x^2 - xy - 6y^2 = 0
On factorizing we get,
(x - 3y)(x + 2y) = 0
Hence, the equation of the pair of straight lines is given by 
x - 3y + l = 0 and x + 2y + m = 0
Hence, the slope of these lines are $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{-1}{2}$ respectively.
The angle between the straight lines is given by,
$tan \theta$ = $m_1 -m_2\over 1+m_1m_2$ = $1/3 - (-1/2)\over 1+(1/3)(-1/2)$ = 1
Hence, the angle is 45^o.

questions.question: Area of a triangle is 5 sq. units. Its two vertices are (2, 1) and (3, - 2). Third vertex is on the line y = x + 3. The coordinates of that vertex are
questions.question_raw: Area of a triangle is 5 sq. units. Its two vertices are (2, 1) and (3, - 2). Third vertex is on the line y = x + 3. The coordinates of that vertex are
questions.explanation:
Area of a triangle whose vertices are (x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2) and (x_3, y_3) is
$1\over 2$[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)
The given veritces are (x, y), (2, 1), (3, -2) and area is 5 sq units
Substituting the values we get
5 = $1\over 2$[(x(1 + 2) + 2(-2 - y) + 3(y - 1)]
That is 10 = 3x + y - 7
or 3x + y = 17
The third vertex passes lies on the line y = x + 3
On solving the equations we get,
x = $\frac{7}{2}$ and y = $\frac{13}{2}$
Hence, the third vertex is ($\frac{7}{2}$, $\frac{13}{2}$)

questions.question: The foot of the perpendicular from (2, 3) on the line 3x + 4y – 6 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The foot of the perpendicular from (2, 3) on the line 3x + 4y – 6 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line perpendicular to the given line 3x + 4y = 6 is 4x - 3y + k = 0
Since this line passes through (2, 3)
4(2) - 3(3) + k = 0
Therefore k = 1
Therefore the line which is perpendicular to the given line is 4x - 3y + 1 = 0
On solving both the equations we get, x = $\frac{14}{25}$ and y = $\frac{27}{25}$
Hence the foot of the perpendicular is ($\frac{14}{25}$, $\frac{27}{25}$)

questions.question: If the line 2x – y + $\lambda $ = 0 is a diameter of the circle x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 6y + 5 = 0 then $\lambda $ =
questions.question_raw: If the line 2x – y + {tex}\lambda {/tex} = 0 is a diameter of the circle x2 + y2 + 6x - 6y + 5 = 0 then {tex}\lambda {/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Equation of circle is x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 6y + 5 = 0 
x^2 + 6x + y^2 - 6y = -5
Applying completing the square method
(x + 3)^2 + (y - 3)^2 = $(\sqrt{13})^{2}$ 
Comparing the above equation with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = (r)^2 we get center as (-3, 3) and radius as $\sqrt{13}$
As the centre of the circle lies on diameter, it will satisfy the equation of diameter, so on putting (-3, 3) in the equation of diameter we get 
2(-3) - (3) + $\lambda$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ -6 - 3 - $\lambda$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\lambda$ = 9

questions.question: Length of common chord of the circles x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 6y = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 2y - 6 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: Length of common chord of the circles x2 + y2 + 2x + 6y = 0 and x2 + y2 - 4x - 2y - 6 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 6y = 0
x^2 + 2x + y^2 + 6y = 0
Applying the completing the square
(x + 1)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = $(\sqrt{10})^{2}$
Comparing the above equation with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2, we get its center as (-1, -3) and radius (r_1) =$\sqrt{10}$ 
x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 2y - 6 = 0
(x - 2)^2 + (y - 1)^2 = $(\sqrt{11})^{2}$
Comparing the above equation with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 we get its center as (2, 1) and radius (r_2) =$\sqrt{11}$ 
So distance(d) between the centers is ​$\sqrt{(2+1)^2+(1+3)^2}$​ = 5 units
After applying the general formula of the length of common chord as ${\sqrt \frac {(d^2-(r_1-r_2)^2)((r_1+r_2)^2-d^2)} {d}}$ and putting the values of r_1, r_2 and d we get length as ${\frac 25}\sqrt {106} $.

questions.question: The circles x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 6y = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 12x - 12y = 0
questions.question_raw: The circles x2 + y2 + 6x + 6y = 0 and x2 + y2 - 12x - 12y = 0
questions.explanation:
Circle touches externally if distance between the centers is equal to the sum of the radii.
x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 6y = 0
x^2 + 6x + y^2 + 6y = 0
After applying completing the square, we get
(x + 3)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = $(3 \sqrt{2})^{2}$ 
So the center is (-3, -3) and radius is $ 3\sqrt{2}$ 
x^2 + y^2 - 12x - 12y = 0
x^2 - 12x + y^2 - 12y = 0
After applying completing the square
(x - 6)^2 + (y - 6)^2 = $(6 \sqrt{2})^{2}$ 
So the center is (6, 6) and radius is $6\sqrt{2}$ 
Distance between centres ​$\sqrt{(6+3)^2+(6+3)^2}=9\sqrt{2}$​ and sum of radii ($3\sqrt {2}+6\sqrt {2} =9\sqrt{2}$ ) are equal.
Hence the circle touches externally.

questions.question: The equation x^2 + y^2 = 0 represents
questions.question_raw: The equation x2 + y2 = 0 represents
questions.explanation:
The above circle can be written as (x - 0)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = (0)^2 
Here the center is (0, 0) and radius is also 0 units.
So it is a degenerate circle as a degenerate circle is a circle( a point) where the radius is zero units.

questions.question: The equation (x^2 + y^2) + 5x - 7y - 2 = 0 represents

questions.explanation: The general equation of the circle is x^2 + y^2 - 2gh - 2fy + c = 0. Since the given equations satisfy the general equation, it represents the equation of the circle.

questions.question: Circumcentre of the triangle, whose vertices are (0, 0), (6, 0) and (0, 4) is
questions.question_raw: Circumcentre of the triangle, whose vertices are (0, 0), (6, 0) and (0, 4) is
questions.explanation:
Circumcentre of a right-angled triangle ABC right angled at A is $b+c\over2$ as circumcentre of right-angled triangle lies on the mid point of the hypotenuse.
So mid point of BC = ($6+0\over2$,$0+4\over2$) i.e.(3, 2)

questions.question: If (x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = c^2 represents a circle, then
questions.question_raw: If (x - a)2 + (y - b)2 = c2 represents a circle, then
questions.explanation: (x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = c^2 here (a, b) is center and c is the radius and radius cannot be zero because if radius is zero it will become a point or degenerate circle so c $ \ne $ 0.

questions.question: The length of the chord joining the point ( 4 cos $\theta $, 4 sin $\theta $) and 4 ( cos($\theta $+${60^o}$), 4 sin($\theta $ + ${60^o}$)) of the circle ${x^2} + {y^2} = 16$ is
questions.question_raw: The length of the chord joining the point ( 4 cos {tex}\theta {/tex}, 4 sin {tex}\theta {/tex}) and 4 ( cos({tex}\theta {/tex}+{tex}{60^o}{/tex}), 4 sin({tex}\theta {/tex} + {tex}{60^o}{/tex})) of the circle {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = 16{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
using distance formulae $\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$ =\sqrt{[(4cos\theta - 4cos(\theta + 60)^2 + (4sin\theta - 4sin(\theta+60)^2]}$
on simplifying we get, $\sqrt{16 + 16 -32.-(1/2)}$$$ = 4

questions.question: Two perpendicular tangents to the circle ${x^2} + {y^2} = {r^2}$ meet at P. The locus of P is
questions.question_raw: Two perpendicular tangents to the circle {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {r^2}{/tex} meet at P. The locus of P is
questions.explanation: locus of P is a circle with centre at origin and radius $\sqrt{2r^2}$. This is known as the director circle of the circle ${x^2} + {y^2} = {r^2}$

questions.question: The number of tangents to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 8x - 6y + 9 = 0, which pass through the point (3, - 2), is
questions.question_raw: The number of tangents to the circle x2 + y2 - 8x - 6y + 9 = 0, which pass through the point (3, - 2), is
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 - 8x - 6y + 9 = 0
After completing the square, we get
(x - 4)^2 + (y - 3)^2 = (4)^2 
So the center is (4, 3) and radius is 4.
Distance between the center and the given point $\sqrt{(4-3)^2+(3+2)^2}=\sqrt{26}$ which is greater than 4.
Hence point lies outside the circle.
Since point lies outside of the circle there will be 2 tangents since two tangents can be drawn from external point to a circle.

questions.question: The value of k, such that the equation 2x^2 + 2y^2 - 6x + 8y + k = 0 represents a point circle, is
questions.question_raw: The value of k, such that the equation 2x2 + 2y2 - 6x + 8y + k = 0 represents a point circle, is
questions.explanation:
​2x^2 + 2y^2 - 6x + 8y + k = 0​
x^2 - 3x + y^2 + 4y = $\frac{-k}{2}$ 
(x - 1.5)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = $\frac{-k}{2}+\frac{9}{4}+4$ 
Which gives radius = $\sqrt{\frac{-k}{2}+\frac{9}{4}+4}$ 
For a point circle radius should be zero.
Hence $\frac{-k}{2}+\frac{9}{4}+4$ = 0
Solving which we get k = $\frac {25} {2}$

questions.question: The equation $a{x^2} + b{y^2} + 2hxy + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$ represents a circle only if
questions.question_raw: The equation {tex}a{x^2} + b{y^2} + 2hxy + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0{/tex} represents a circle only if
questions.explanation:
Recalling the general form of a circle as
 It is a quadratic equation in both x and y.
 Coefficient of $x^2 $
 = Coefficient of $y^2 $ .
 There is no term containing xy i.e., the coefficient of xy is zero.
 radius is $\mathrm{\frac{1}{a}\sqrt{g^{2} + f^{2} - ca}}$ 
To satisfy (i) and (ii) condition we have $a = b \ne 0$ 
To satisfy (iii) condition we have $h = 0$
To satisfy (iv) and as we cant have a negative sign in root so g^2 + f^2 - ac > 0

questions.question: x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 8y - 11 = 0 is a circle. The points (0, 0) and (1, 8) lie
questions.question_raw: x2 + y2 - 6x + 8y - 11 = 0 is a circle. The points (0, 0) and (1, 8) lie

options.label: B
options.text: one outside the circle and one inside

options.label: D
options.text: one on the circle and the other outside

correct_option_texts: one outside the circle and one inside
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 8y - 11 = 0
x^2 - 6x + y^2 + 8y = 11
(x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = (6)^2
So the center is (3, -4) and radius is 6 units.
Distance between center and (0, 0) is $\sqrt{3-0)^2+(-4-0)^2}$ = 5 units which is less than the radius so it lies inside the circle.
Whereas the distance between center and (1, 8) is $\sqrt{(3-1)^2 +(8-(-4))^2}$ = $\sqrt{148}$ which is greater than the radius so it lies outside the circle

questions.question: The length of tangent from the point (2, -3) to the circle 2x^2 + 2y^2 = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The length of tangent from the point (2, -3) to the circle 2x2 + 2y2 = 1 is
questions.explanation:
2x^2 + 2y^2 = 1
Applying completing the square method, we get
(x - 0)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = $ \frac{1}{2}$
So it has center (0, 0) and radius $\frac {1}{\sqrt{2}} $ 
Let O = (0, 0) be the center, A = (2, -3) be the external point and B be the point of contact of tangent and circle.
OAB forms a triangle with angle OBA = 90° (as The angle between the tangent and radius is 90°) and distance OA =${\sqrt{13}} $ units (using distance formula) and OB = $\frac {1}{\sqrt{2}} $ units(radius)
Length of tangent = AB
Using Pythagoras theorem,
OA^2 = OB^2 + AB^2
$\Rightarrow$ AB^2 = OA^2 - OB^2
Putting the values of OA and OB = AB^2 = 13 - $\frac 12$ = $\frac {25}{2}$
Therefore AB = $\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} $

questions.question: Which one of the following lines is farthest from the centre of the circle x^2 + y^2 = 10?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following lines is farthest from the centre of the circle x2 + y2 = 10?
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 = 10
After completing the square, we get
(x - 0)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = $(\sqrt{10})^{2}$ 
So center is (0, 0) and equation of given line is $x + \sqrt 3 \;y + 7 = 0$ 
By applying formula of the distance of a point from a given line i.e.,$Ax_1+By_1+C\over{\sqrt{A^2+B^2}}$ where(x_1, y_1) are the coordinates of point and A and B are coefficient of x,y in given line so d = $7\over{2}$ which is greatest.

questions.question: Which of the following lines is a normal to the circle (x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 10
questions.question_raw: Which of the following lines is a normal to the circle (x - 1)2 + (y - 2)2 = 10
questions.explanation:
(x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 10
Comparing the above equation with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 we get center as (1, 2) putting (1, 2) in L.H.S of above line, we get 1 + 2 = 3 = R.H.S
$\Rightarrow$ It satisfies the equation of line or the line passes through center and the line passing through the centre is normal.

questions.question: A circle with its centre on the line y = x + 1 is drawn to pass through the origin and touch the line y = x + 2. The centre of the circle is
questions.question_raw: A circle with its centre on the line y = x + 1 is drawn to pass through the origin and touch the line y = x + 2. The centre of the circle is
questions.explanation:
Let (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 be the equation of circle with (h, k) as the center and r be the radius.
As the center lies on the line y = x + 1
$\Rightarrow$ k = h + 1 or k = 1 + h ...(i)
Circle passes through origin so (0, 0) will satisfy the equation of line so putting (0, 0) in the equation of circle we get (h)^2 + (k)^2 = r^2 .....(ii)
putting k from (i) into (ii), we get
h^2 + (h + 1)^2 = r^2 
2h^2 + 2h + 1 = r^2 ....(iii)
and as the circle touches the line y = x + 2 so radius should be equal to the distance from the center to this line.
$\Rightarrow$ r =$\frac {h-k+2}{\sqrt 2}$ 
putting value ok k from (i) in above equation, we get
r = $\frac {h-(h+1)+2}{\sqrt{2}}$ 
r = $\frac {1}{\sqrt{2}}$ 
putting the value of r in (iii)
2h^2 + 2h + 1 = $\frac{1}{2}$ 
2h^2 + 4h + 1 = 0
2h^2 + 2h + 2h + 1 = 0
(2h + 1)(2h = 1) = 0 which gives h = $\frac{-1}{2}$ putting h in (i) we get k = $\frac{1}{2}$ 
Hence center is $(\frac{-1}{2},\frac{1}{2})$

questions.question: Four distinct points $(2\lambda ,3\lambda )$, (1, 0), (0, 1) and (0, 0) lie on a circle for
questions.question_raw: Four distinct points {tex}(2\lambda ,3\lambda ){/tex}, (1, 0), (0, 1) and (0, 0) lie on a circle for
questions.explanation:
Because the equation of circle will be x^2 + y^2 - x - y = 0 which is a quadratic equation and by putting the given fourth point we will get two different values of $\lambda$. 
Proof: let equation of cirle be (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 
As (1, 0) (0, 1) and (0, 0) lie on circle, we get
(1 - h)^2 + k^2 = r^2 ....(i)
h^2 + (1 - k)^2 = r^2 ....(ii)
h^2 + k^2 = r^2 ....(iii)
Comparing (i) and (iii) , we get h =$\frac{1}{2}$ 
Comparing (ii) and (iii) we get k =$\frac{1}{2}$ 
putting the value of h and k in (iii) we get $r^2=\frac{1}{4}$ 
Hence we get the equation of circle as (2x - 1 )^2 + (2y - 1)^2 = 1 
Now putting $(2\lambda ,3\lambda )$in above circle, we get
$(4\lambda-1)^2+(6\lambda-1)^2=1$ 
Which is quadratic and will give 2 values of $\lambda$

questions.question: The locus of the point of intersection of the lines x cos $\alpha $ + y sin $\alpha = a$ and x sin $\alpha $ - y cos $\alpha $ = b is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the point of intersection of the lines x cos {tex}\alpha {/tex} + y sin {tex}\alpha = a{/tex} and x sin {tex}\alpha {/tex} - y cos {tex}\alpha {/tex} = b is
questions.explanation:
Solving the above two equations we get,
x = $a\cos \alpha - b\sin \alpha$ .....(i) 
y = $ a\sin \alpha - b\cos \alpha$ .....(ii)
Squaring both sides of (i) and (ii) and adding both the equation we get x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2.
Which represents the locus of circle as
 it is a quadratic equation
 coeff of x^2 = coeff of y^2
 there is no term involving xy.

questions.question: The line y = mx + c is a normal to the circle x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, if
questions.question_raw: The line y = mx + c is a normal to the circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, if
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 + 2gx = 2fy + c = 0 has center (-g, -f)
As normal passes through the center so center will satisfy the equation of normal put y = -f and x = -g in equation of the line y = mx + c
-f = m(-g) + c
Which gives mg = f + c.

questions.question: The line 3x – 4y = 0
questions.question_raw: The line 3x – 4y = 0

options.label: A
options.text: is a normal to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25

options.label: B
options.text: is a tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25

options.label: C
options.text: does not pass through the origin

options.label: D
options.text: does not meet the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25

correct_option_texts: is a normal to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25
questions.explanation:
x^2 + y^2 = 25
After completing the square, we get
(x - 0)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = 5^2 
So the center is (0, 0) and the radius is 5 units.
As the normal should pass through the centre of the circle, the center should satisfy the equation of normal.
So 3x – 4y = 0 is the equation of normal as (0, 0) satisfy this equation.

questions.question: The equations x = a cos $\theta $ + b sin $\theta $ , and $y = a\sin \theta - b\cos \theta $ , 0 $ \le $ $\theta $ $ \le $ 2 $\pi $ represent
questions.question_raw: The equations x = a cos {tex}\theta {/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta {/tex} , and {tex}y = a\sin \theta - b\cos \theta {/tex} , 0 {tex} \le {/tex} {tex}\theta {/tex} {tex} \le {/tex} 2 {tex}\pi {/tex} represent
questions.explanation:
x = a cos $\theta $ + b sin $\theta $ , and $y = a\sin \theta - b\cos \theta $ 
Putting the value of x and y in x^2 + y^2 
$ (a\cos \theta + b\sin \theta)^2+( a\sin \theta - b\cos \theta)^2 $ we get a^2+b^2
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2
Which is quadratic in nature, coefficient of x^2 = coefficient of y^2 and no term involving xy
Hence its locus represents a circle.

questions.question: If the lines 3x - 4y + 4 = 0 and 6x - 8y - 7 = 0 are tangents to the circle then the radius of the circle is
questions.question_raw: If the lines 3x - 4y + 4 = 0 and 6x - 8y - 7 = 0 are tangents to the circle then the radius of the circle is
questions.explanation:
The given lines are parallel. Hence the tangents are parallel tangents. Distance between these lines is equal to the diameter
D = $|8 - (-7)|\over \sqrt{36+64}$ = $\frac{3}{2}$ 
Therefore, the radius is $\frac{3}{4}$

questions.question: A circle passes through (0, 0) ( a, 0), (0, b). The coordinates of its centre are
questions.question_raw: A circle passes through (0, 0) ( a, 0), (0, b). The coordinates of its centre are
questions.explanation:
Because line joining the points (a, 0) and (0, b) will be diameter and it's mid-point will be centre of the circle.
Or
let the equation of circle be $(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=r^2$ 
as circle passes through (0, 0), (a, 0) and (0, b), they will satisfy the above equation
putting the points in above equation we get
$h^2+k^2=r^2$ ...(i)
$(a-h)^2+k^2=r^2$ ...(ii)
$h^2+(b-k)^2=r^2$ ...(iii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get h = $\frac a2$
whereas solving (i) and (iii) we get k = $\frac b2$
hence its center is $\left( \frac a2, \frac b2 \right)$

questions.question: The focus of the parabola x^2 - 8x + 2y + 7 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The focus of the parabola x2 - 8x + 2y + 7 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
x^2 - 8x + 2y + 7 = 0
x^2 - 8x = -2y - 7
After completing the square we get,
(x - 4)^2 = -2y + 9
(x - 4)^2 = $-4 \frac{1}{2}\left(y-\frac{9}{2}\right)$ 
Comparing the above equation with x^2 = -4ay focus is (0, -a)
Putting x co-ordinate = 0 we get x - 4 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = 4
and y co-ordinate = -a we get $(y -\frac{ 9}{2})=-\frac{1}{2} $ 
$\Rightarrow$ y = 4
Hence (4, 4) is the focus.

questions.question: The radius of the circle passing through the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{9}$ = 1 and having its centre at (0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The radius of the circle passing through the foci of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{9}{/tex} = 1 and having its centre at (0, 3) is

Comparing with the standard equation we get a = 4 and b = 3 we get c =$\sqrt{16-9}$ = $\sqrt{7}$ 
So focus is $(\sqrt{7},0)$ and the given centre is (0, 3).
Using distance formulae. we get radius = 4 units

questions.question: The line y = c is a tangent to the parabola x^2 = y - 1 if c is equal to

x^2 = c - 1
or x^2 - (c - 1) = 0
Here discriminat =$\sqrt{4c-4}$ 
Line y = c is tangent when the discriminant is equal to 0.
Putting discriminant = 0 we get c = 1.
(0, c) will be a point on the parabola.

questions.question: The line y = mx + c, touches the parabola y^2 = 4ax if
questions.question_raw: The line y = mx + c, touches the parabola y2 = 4ax if
questions.explanation:
y = mx + c ......(i)
y^2 = 4ax ....(ii)
Putting the value of y from (i) in (ii), we get
(mx + c)^2 = 4ax
$\Rightarrow$ (mx)^2 + x(2mc - 4a) + c^2 = 0
Here discriminant =$\sqrt{(2mc-4a)^2-4m^2c^2}$ = $\sqrt{16a^2-16amc}$ 
When discriminant > 0 line touches parabola at two points, when discriminant < 0 lines do not touch parabola and when discriminant = 0 line touches parabola at one point and we know that tangent is a line that touches the curve at exactly one point.
So putting discriminate = 0 and solving
we get $c = \frac{a}{m},m \ne 0$ 
On putting the value of y from line in the parabola and solving for equal roots.

questions.question: The equations x = at^2, y = 4at; t $\in$ R represent
questions.question_raw: The equations x = at2, y = 4at; t {tex}\in{/tex} R represent

y = 4at
Squaring both sides, we get
y^2 = 16a^2t^2 
Putting the value of at^2 i.e. at^2 = $\frac{y^{2}}{16 a}$ in x = at^2 we get,
16ax = y^2 
or y^2 = 4(4a)x
Which is nothing but equation of parabola.

questions.question: $x=\frac{e^t+e^{-t}}{2}, y=\frac{e^t-e^{-t}}{2} ; t \in R$ represents

questions.explanation:
$x=\frac{e^t+e^{-t}}{2}, y=\frac{e^t-e^{-t}}{2} ; t \in R$ 
Squaring both sides of both the equation, we get
$$x^2 = $\frac {(e^t + e^-t)^2}{ 4}$ and y^2 = $\frac {(e^t - e^-t)^2}{4}$ 
Subtracting one equation from another we get
x^2 - y^2 = 1 which is nothing but equation of hyperbola.

i.e. put x - a = 0 and y = 0

questions.question: The two parabolas x^2 = 4y and y^2 = 4x meet in two distinct points. One of these is the origin and the other is
questions.question_raw: The two parabolas x2 = 4y and y2 = 4x meet in two distinct points. One of these is the origin and the other is
questions.explanation:
Given, equations of the parabola are x^2 = 4y and y^2 = 4x ... (i)
$\therefore$ $\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right)^{2}$ = 4x
$\Rightarrow$ x^4 - 64x = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = 0, x = 4
On putting the values of x in Eq. (i), we get y = 0 and y = 4, −4
($\because$ y = −4 does not satisfy the equation x^2 = 4y)
Thus, the points of intersection are (0, 0) and (4, 4).
Hence, the point other than origin is (4, 4).

questions.question: The angle between the tangents drawn from the origin to the circle = (x - 7)^2 + (y + 1)^2 = 25 is
questions.question_raw: The angle between the tangents drawn from the origin to the circle = (x - 7)2 + (y + 1)2 = 25 is
questions.explanation:
Let tangent from origin be y = mx
Using the condition of tangency, we get
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{7 m+1}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}$ = 5
(7m + 1)^2 = 25(m^2 + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ 24m^2 + 14m - 24 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 12m^2 + 7m - 12 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 12m^2 + 16m - 9m - 12 = 0
(4m - 3)(3m - 4)
$\therefore$ m = $\frac{3}{4}$ and m = -$\frac{4}{3}$ 
The angle between tangents = $\frac{\pi}{2}$

questions.question: Three normals to the parabola y^2 = x are drawn through a point (c, 0) then
questions.question_raw: Three normals to the parabola y2 = x are drawn through a point (c, 0) then
questions.explanation:
The equation of the normal to a parabola y^2 = 4ax is
y = mx - 2am - am^3
Hence the equation of the normal to the given parabola y^2 = x is 
mx - $\frac{m}{2}$ - $\frac{m^3}{4}$ 
Since it passes through (c, 0)
mc - $\frac{m}{2}$ - $\frac{m^3}{4}$ = 0
on solving we get 
m = 0 or m^2 = 4(c - $\frac{1}{2}$)
If m = 0 then the equation of the normal is y = 0
If m^2 $\ge$0, then 4(c - $\frac{1}{2}$) $\ge$0
Hence c - $\frac{1}{2}$ $\ge$0 or c > $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: S and T are the foci of an ellipse and B is an end of the minor axis. If STB is an equilateral triangle, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: S and T are the foci of an ellipse and B is an end of the minor axis. If STB is an equilateral triangle, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
s = (ae, 0) and T = (-ae, 0) and B = (0, b)
Since it is an equilateral triangle, ST^2 = TB^2
This implies 4a^2e^2 = a^2e^2 + b^2
3a^2e^2 = b^2 
3a^2e^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)
3e^2 = 1 - e^2
Therefore e = $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: The centre of a circle passing through the points (0, 0), (1, 0) and touching the circle ${x^2} + {y^2} = 9$ is
questions.question_raw: The centre of a circle passing through the points (0, 0), (1, 0) and touching the circle {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = 9{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Since the circle passes through (0, 0) the equation reduces to
c = 0 ....(1)
Since it passes through (1, 0),
1 + 2g + c = 0
This implies g = -1/2
Since the circle touches the circle x^2 + y^2 = 9, their radii should be equal
2$\sqrt{g^2 + f^2 + c}$ = 3
Substituting the values and simplifying we get f = $\pm\sqrt 2$
Hence the centre is $\left(\frac{1}{2},-\sqrt{2}\right)$

questions.question: The number of tangents to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 4y = 0 through the point (-1, 2) is
questions.question_raw: The number of tangents to the circle x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y = 0 through the point (-1, 2) is
questions.explanation:
The given equation of the circle can be written as (x - 1)^2 - 1 + (y + 2)^2 - 4 = 0
(x - 1)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 5
This implies the radius is $\sqrt 5$ and the centre is (1, -2)
The given point is (-1, 2)
The distance between the centre of the circle and the given point is
$\sqrt{(-1-1)^2 + (2+2)^2}$ = $\sqrt 20$ 
Since this is greater than the radius, the point lies outside the circle. Hence two tangents can be drawn.

questions.question: The equation of the tangent to the conic x^2 - y^2 - 8x + 2y + 11 = 0 at (2, 1) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the tangent to the conic x2 - y2 - 8x + 2y + 11 = 0 at (2, 1) is
questions.explanation:
Differentiating the given equation w.r.t x, we get,
2x - 2y$dy\over dx$ - 8 + 2 $dy\over dx$ = 0
$dy\over dx$(1 - y) = x - 4
Therefore, $dy\over dx$ = $x-4\over 1-y $ 
Therefore, $dy\over dx$(2,1)_ is not defined
 The equation of the tangent at (x_1, y_1) is y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)
Therefore, the equation of the tangent is x - 2 = 0

questions.question: If the major axis of an ellipse is three times the minor axis, then its eccentricity is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the major axis of an ellipse is three times the minor axis, then its eccentricity is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\frac{2\sqrt 2 }{3}$
Length of the major axis = 2b
Length of the minor axis = 2a
According to question, the major axis of the ellipse is three times the minor axis.
i.e. 2b = 3(2a)
$\Rightarrow$ 2b = 6a
$\Rightarrow$ a = $\frac {2b} {6}$
$\Rightarrow$ a = $\frac {b} {3}$, b = 3a
Here, a > b, so the major and the minor axes of the ellipse are along the x - axis and they y - axis, respectively
Now, e $=\sqrt{1-\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{1-\frac{\frac{b^{2}}{9}}{b^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{9}}$
$\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}$
$\Rightarrow e=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}$

questions.question: At what point on the parabola y^2 = 4x the normal makes equal angles with the axes?
questions.question_raw: At what point on the parabola y2 = 4x the normal makes equal angles with the axes?
questions.explanation:
If the normal has slope - 1 then the tangent, that is perpendicular to the normal, will have slope = + 1 
Hence its equation is y = x + k
Hence the point of intersection between the parabola and the normal is 
(x + k)^2 = 4x
i.e; x^2 + 2kx - 4x + k^2 = 0
For discriminant value to be zero (b^2 - 4ac) = 0
(2k - 1)^2 - 4k^2 = 0
on solving this we get k = 1
Therefore the equaiton is x^2 - 2x + 1 = 0
This implies x = 1 and y = $\pm$2

questions.question: The number of common tangents to the circles x^2 + y^2 - x = 0, x^2 + y^2 + x = 0 is

questions.explanation:
The equation of the circles can be written as (x - $1\over 2$)^2 + y^2 = $1\over 4$ and (x + $1\over 2$)^2 + y^2 = $1\over 4$ 
Hence their radii is $1\over 2$ and the centres is ($1\over 2$, 0) and ($-1\over 2$,0)
Distance between their centres is 1 and sum of the radii is also 1, hence the circles touch externally.
Therefore number of tangents that can be drawn is 3.

questions.question: The equation x^2 + 3y^2 - 9x + 2y + 1 = 0 represents

a = 200, b = 10,

questions.question: The radius of the circle 3x (x – 2) + 3y(y + 1) = 4 is
questions.question_raw: The radius of the circle 3x (x – 2) + 3y(y + 1) = 4 is
questions.explanation:
The general form of the given circle is x^2 + y^2 - 2x + y - $\frac{4}{3}$ = 0
Hence g = 1, f = $\frac{-1}{2}$ and c = $\frac{-4}{3}$
Radius = $\sqrt{g^2 + f^2 -c}$ 
Substituting the values we get,
Radius = $\sqrt{1 +\frac{1}{4}+\frac{4}{3}}$ = $\sqrt\frac{31}{12}$

questions.question: If the angle between the lines joining the foci of an ellipse to an extremity of a minor axis is 90^o, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: If the angle between the lines joining the foci of an ellipse to an extremity of a minor axis is 90o, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
The triangle formed is a right-angled triangle.
Let us consider STS' as the triangle right angled at T.
The coordinates of S is (ae, 0), S' (-ae, 0) and T(0, b)
By applying Pythagoras theorem
ST^2 + S'T^2 = SS'^2
i.e; (a^2e^2 + b^2) + (a^2e^2 + b^2) = 2 a^2e^2
on simplifying we get
e^2 = $\frac{1}{2}$ or e = $\frac{1}{\sqrt2}$

questions.question: The vertex of the parabola y^2 = 4a(x + a) is
questions.question_raw: The vertex of the parabola y2 = 4a(x + a) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation y = Y and (x + a) = X
Then the equation of the parabola can be written as
Y^2 = 4aX, whose vertex is (0, 0)
Therefore, x + a = 0 and y = 0., implies x = -a and y = 0
Hence, the vertex of the given parabola is (-a, 0).

questions.question: If 3x + y = 0 is a tangent to the circle which has its center at the point (2, - 1), then the equation of the other tangent to the circle from the origin is
questions.question_raw: If 3x + y = 0 is a tangent to the circle which has its center at the point (2, - 1), then the equation of the other tangent to the circle from the origin is
questions.explanation:
The other tangent will be perpendicular to the given tangent 3x + y = 0
Therefore its equation is x - 3y + k = 0
Since it passes through the origin, k = 0
Hence the equation of the other tangent is x - 3y = 0

questions.question: The radius of the sphere through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The radius of the sphere through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
We know that general equation of sphere is x^2 + y^2 + z^2 + 2gx + 2fy + 2hz + c = 0
Since sphere passing through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) so putting these values one by one in given equation, we get
16 + 9 + 8g + 6f + c = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 8g + 6f + c = -25 ......(i)
16 + 9 + 8f + 6h + c = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 8f + 6h + c = -25 ......(ii)
25 + 10f + c = 0 ......(iii)
16 + 9 + 8g + 6h + c = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 8g + 6h + c = -25 ......(iv)
By (ii) - (iv) we have f = g
Using this in (i) we have 16f +c = -25 .....(v)
Now solving (iii) and (v) we get f = 0 
Using value of f we have g = 0, h = 0, c = -25
Now radius =$\sqrt {{{( - g)}^2} + ( - f) + {{( - h)}^2} - c} $ = $\sqrt {25} $ = 5

questions.question: The points (4, 7, 8), (2, 3, 4), (-1, -2, 1) and (1, 2, 5) are the vertices of a
questions.question_raw: The points (4, 7, 8), (2, 3, 4), (-1, -2, 1) and (1, 2, 5) are the vertices of a
questions.explanation:
The vertices of a quadrilateral are A( 4, 7, 8), B(2, 3, 4) ,C (- 1, -2, 1) and D(1, 2, 5) 
AB =$\sqrt {{{(4 - 2)}^2} + {{(7 - 3)}^2} + {{(8 - 4)}^2}} $ = $\sqrt{4+16+16}$ = $\sqrt{36}$ = 6 
BC =$\sqrt {{{(2 + 1)}^2} + {{(3 + 2)}^2} + {{(4 - 1)}^2}}$ = $\sqrt{9+25+9}$ = $\sqrt{43}$ 
CD =$\sqrt {{{(1 + 1)}^2} + {{(2 + 2)}^2} + {{(5 - 1)}^2}} $ = $\sqrt{4+16+16}$ = $\sqrt{36}$ = 6
DA = $\sqrt {{{(4 - 1)}^2} + {{(7 - 2)}^2} + {{(8 - 5)}^2}} $ = $\sqrt{9+25+9}$ = $\sqrt{43}$ 
Here opposite sides of quadrilateral ABCD are equal, so it may be parallelogram or rectangle
Now , diagonal AC =$\sqrt {{{(4 + 1)}^2} + {{(7 +2)}^2} + {{(8 -1)}^2}} $ = $\sqrt{25+81+49}$ = $\sqrt{155}$ 
BD = $\sqrt {{{(2 - 1)}^2} + {{(3 - 2)}^2} + {{(4 - 5)}^2}}$ = $\sqrt{1+1+1}$ = $\sqrt{3}$ 
Here the length of diagonals AC and BD are different so ABCD is a parallelogram.

questions.question: The distance of the point (x, y, z) from the XY–plane is

questions.question_raw: The equation (x – 1)(x – 2) = 0 represents, in three dimensional space, a
questions.explanation:
The equation (x - 1) (x - 2) = 0 represents, in three dimensional space, a pair of parallel planes.
(x - 1) (x - 2) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ (x - 1) = 0, (x - 2) = 0
x = 1 and x = 2 
According to the equation ax + by + cz = d and by the formula $\frac{{a_1}}{{a_2}} = \frac{{b_1}}{{b_2}} = \frac{{c_1}}{{c_2}}$ we have both the planes represent pair of parallel planes.

questions.explanation:
Given:
Place: x = 0 and two points (−2, 3, 4) and (1, −2, 3)
Let say a point (x, y, z) in x = 0 place
So, x = $\frac{m+n(-2)}{m+n}=\frac{m-2 n}{m+n}$ 
0 = $\frac{m-2 n}{m+n}$ $\Rightarrow$ m = 2n
So, $\frac{m}{n}=\frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow$ 2 : 1

questions.question: The point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Let the point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is (x, y, z)
Then according to the given condition and distance formula between two points we have 
$\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-3)^{2}}$ 
$\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2} + {z^2}} $ = $\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{x^{2}+(y-2)^{2}+z^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-3)^{2}}$ 
Taking ist two expressions and solving them we get x^2 = (x - 1)^2 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 = x^2 - 2x + 1 $\Rightarrow$ -2x + 1 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{1}{2}$ 
Similarly by taking i^st and 3^rd we get y = 1 and by taking ist and 4th we get z = $\frac{3}{2}$ 
So the required point is ($\frac{1}{2}$, 1, $\frac{3}{2}$)

Now L.H.S of (i) = x + y
For (0, 0, z), L.H.S of (i) = 0 + 0 = 0 = R.H.S of (i)
Hence the result.

questions.question: The centre of the sphere, which passes through (a, 0, 0), (0, b, 0) (0, 0, c ) and (0, 0, 0) is where abc $ \ne $ 0
questions.question_raw: The centre of the sphere, which passes through (a, 0, 0), (0, b, 0) (0, 0, c ) and (0, 0, 0) is where abc {tex} \ne {/tex} 0
questions.explanation:
General equation of the sphere is x^2 + y^2 + z^2 + 2gx + 2fy + 2hz + d = 0......(i)
Since (i) passes through the point (0, 0, 0) using this in (i) we get d = 0
Similarly (i) passes through (a, 0, 0), (0, b, 0) (0, 0, c) using these values in (i)
a^2 + 2ag = 0 $\Rightarrow$ a(a + 2g) = 0
b^2 + 2bf = 0 $\Rightarrow$ b(b + 2f) = 0
c^2 + 2ch = 0 $\Rightarrow$ c(c + 2h) = 0
But as abc $\ne $ 0 So, a $\ne $ 0, b $\ne $ 0, c $\ne $ 0
So from above equations we have a = - 2g, b = - 2f, c = -2h centre is (-f, -g, -h) = ($\frac{a}{2}$, $\frac{b}{2}$, $\frac{c}{2}$)

questions.question: The centre of the sphere through the points ( 0 , 3 , 4 ) , ( 0 , 5 , 0 ) , ( 4 , 0 , 3 ) and ( - 3 , 4 , 0 ) is
questions.question_raw: The centre of the sphere through the points ( 0 , 3 , 4 ) , ( 0 , 5 , 0 ) , ( 4 , 0 , 3 ) and ( - 3 , 4 , 0 ) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the required sphere be 
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}+2 \mathrm{ux}+2 \mathrm{vy}+2 \mathrm{wz}+\mathrm{d}=0$ ...........(1)
since it passes through (0,3,4),(0,5,0),(4,0,3) and (-3,4,0) therefore
$0^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}+2 u(0)+2 v(3)+2 w(4)+d=0$
$4^{2}+0^{2}+3^{2}+2 u(4)+2 v(0)+2 w(3)+d=0$
$(-3)^{2}+4^{2}+0^{2}+2 u(-3)+2 v(4)+2 w(0)+d=0$
$\Rightarrow 6 v+8 w+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots .(2)$
$\Rightarrow 8 u+6 w+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots(3)$
$\Rightarrow-6 u+8 v+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots .(4)$
$\Rightarrow 10 \mathrm{v}+25+\mathrm{d}=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots \text { (5) }$
Subtracting (5) from (2),(3),(4) respectively, we get 
$\Rightarrow-4 \mathrm{v}+8 \mathrm{w}=0 \ldots \ldots .(6)$
$\Rightarrow 8 u+6 w-10 v=0............(7)$
$\Rightarrow-6 u-2 v=0 \ldots \ldots .(8)$
Solving these, we get, u=v=w=0
$\therefore$ Centre of sphere (-u,-v,-w)=(0,0,0)

questions.question: The points A ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) , B ( 1 , $\sqrt 3 $ , 0 ) , C ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) and D ( 1 , 0 , $\sqrt 3 $) are the vertices of
questions.question_raw: The points A ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) , B ( 1 , {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex} , 0 ) , C ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) and D ( 1 , 0 , {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex}) are the vertices of
questions.explanation:
Direction ratios of AB are $(1-0, \sqrt{3}-0,0-0) \text { i.e }(1, \sqrt{3}, 0)$
Direction ratios of BC are$(1-0, \sqrt{3}-0,0-0) \text { i.e }(1,-\sqrt{3}, 0)$
Direction ratios of CD are $(-1-0,0-0, \sqrt{3}-0) \text { i.e }(-1,0, \sqrt{3})$
Direction ratios of CD are $(-1-0,0-0,-\sqrt{3}-0) \text { i.e }(-1,0, \sqrt{3})$
If ABCD is a parallelogram, then
$\mathrm{AB} \| \mathrm{CD} \text { and } \mathrm{AD} \| \mathrm{BC}$
Now,$\frac{1}{-1} \neq \frac{\sqrt{3}}{0} \neq \frac{0}{\sqrt{3}}$ $\left[\because\frac{{\mathbf a}_1}{{\mathbf a}_2}\neq\frac{\mathbf b1}{{\mathbf b}_2}\neq\frac{{\mathbf c}_1}{{\mathbf c}_2}\right]$
$\therefore$ AB is not parallel to CD
Similarly $\frac{-1}{1} \neq \frac{0}{\sqrt{-3}} \neq \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{0}$
$\Rightarrow$ AD and BC are not parallel 
$\therefore$ ABCD is not a parallelogram and hence it is not a square or rhombus

questions.question: The points A (0, 2, 0), B ($\sqrt 3 $, 1, 0), and $\mathrm{C}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, 1, \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ are the vertices of

questions.explanation:
To check which type of triangle these points form we need to check the distance between the two points
AB=$\sqrt{(\sqrt 3)^2+1+0}$ = 2
BC = $\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}-\sqrt{3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2}$ = 2,
Similarly AC = 2 by distance formula between two points.
Since all sides are same so the triangle is equilateral.

questions.question_raw: Area of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} whose vertices A (-1, 2, 3), B(1, 1, 1) and C (2, - 1, 3) is

Formula is $\frac{1}{2}|\vec AB \times\vec AC| $ where $\vec AB$ has coordinates = P.V. of B - P.V of A = 2, -1, -2 and $\vec AC$ has coordinates = 3, -3, 0 
Area of triangle ABC = $\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} i & j & k \\ 2 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & 0 \end{array}\right|$ = $\frac{1}{2}|6 i+6 j+3 k|$
= $\frac{1}{2} \times 9$ 
= 4.5

questions.question_raw: G is the centroid of triangle ABC . If P.V. of the points G , A , B , C are respectively , {tex}3\hat i + 7\hat j + \hat k,2\hat i + \hat j + \hat k,x\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k,and4\hat i + y\hat j + \hat k,then\left( {x,y} \right) = {/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\vec{g}=\frac{\vec{a}+ \vec{b}+ \vec{c}}{3}$ centroid formula
$3(3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$=$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}+x \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+4 \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$9 \hat{i}+21 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$=$(6+x) \hat{i}+(4+y) \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$

questions.question: If the four points (3, -2, -1), (2, 3, -4), (-1, 1, 2) and (4, 5, $\lambda $) are coplanar then $\lambda $ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the four points (3, -2, -1), (2, 3, -4), (-1, 1, 2) and (4, 5, {tex}\lambda {/tex}) are coplanar then {tex}\lambda {/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Since A(3, -2, -1), B(2, 3, -4), C(-1, 1, 2) and D(4, 5, $\lambda $) are coplanar
$\overrightarrow {AB} = - 1\hat i + 5\hat j - 3\hat k$ 
$\overrightarrow {AC} = - 4\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k$ 
$\overrightarrow {AD} = 1\hat i + 7\hat j + (\lambda + 1)\hat k$ 
Hence $[\overrightarrow {AB} \;\overrightarrow {AC} \;\overrightarrow {AD} ] = 0\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&5&{ - 3} \\ { - 4}&3&3 \\ 1&7&{\lambda + 1} \end{array}} \right| $ = 0
-1(3$\lambda$ + 3 - 21) - 5(-4$\lambda$ - 4 - 4) - 3(-28 - 3) = 0
$\lambda$ = $\frac{-146}{17}$

questions.question: A line AB in three-dimensional space makes angles 45° and 120° with the positive X-axis and the positive Y-axis, respectively. lf AB makes an acute angle $\theta$ with the positive X-axis, then $\theta$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: A line AB in three-dimensional space makes angles 45° and 120° with the positive X-axis and the positive Y-axis, respectively. lf AB makes an acute angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the positive X-axis, then {tex}\theta{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We know that,
cos^2 45^o + cos^2 120^o + cos^2 $\theta$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \theta$ = 1
$\Rightarrow$ cos^2 $\theta$ = $\frac{1}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ cos $\theta$ = $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\theta$ = 60^o or 120^o 
Since $\theta$ is acute, therefore $\theta$ = 60^o

Since AM Perpendicular to BC
Direction of AM = (4$\lambda$ + 1 - 1, 0, -2$\lambda$ + 6 - 1)4(4$\lambda$) + 0 - 2(-2$\lambda$ + 5) = 0
$20\lambda $ = 10

Hence M (x, y, z) = ($\frac{4 \times 1}{2+1}$ ,4, $\frac{-2 \times 1}{2+6}$)
M = (3,4,5)

questions.question: The points (1, 2, 3), (-1, -1, -1) and (3, 5, 7) are the vertices of
questions.question_raw: The points (1, 2, 3), (-1, -1, -1) and (3, 5, 7) are the vertices of

Let A = (1, 2, 3), B = (-1, -1, -1) and C = (3, 5, 7)
By distance formula between two points (x_1, y_1, z_1) and (x_2, y_2, z_2) = $\sqrt {{{(x_1 - x_2)}^2} + {{(y_1 - y_2)}^2} + {{(z_1 - z_2)}^2}} $ 
We get AB = $\sqrt {29} $ , BC = $\sqrt {116} $ = 2$\sqrt {29} $ 
AC =$\sqrt {29} $ 
As we know the triangle is possible only if the sum of any two sides is greater than 3rd but in our case, this condition is not holding so the triangle is not possible, these are collinear points.

questions.question: The angle between a line with direction ratios 2 : 2 : 1 and a line joining (3, 1, 4) to (7, 2, 12)
questions.question_raw: The angle between a line with direction ratios 2 : 2 : 1 and a line joining (3, 1, 4)  to (7, 2, 12)
questions.explanation:
The angle between a line with direction ratios 2 : 2 : 1 and a line joining (3, 1, 4) to (7, 2, 12)
Direction ratios of the line joining the points A(3, 1, 4), B(7, 2, 12) is <x2-x1 , y2-y1, z2-z1> = < 7-3 , 2-1 , 12-4> = <4 ,1 ,8>
Now as the angle between two lines having direction ratios <a1,b1,c1> and <a2,b2,c2> is given by
$\operatorname{Cos}^{-1} \frac{a 1 a 2+b 1 b 2+c 1 c 2}{\sqrt{a 1^2+b 1^2+c 1^2} \sqrt{a 2^2+b 2^2+c 2^2}}$
Using the vuales we have
$\cos ^{-1} \frac{2 \times 4+2 \times 1+1 \times 8}{\sqrt{2^2+2^2+1^2} \sqrt{4^2+1^2+8^2}}$
$=\cos ^{-1} \frac{18}{27}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{2}{3}$

questions.question: Lines OA, OB are drawn from 0 with direction cosines proportional to <1, - 2, - 1> and <3, - 2, 3> respectively. The direction ratios of the normal to the plane AOB are
questions.question_raw: Lines OA, OB are drawn from 0 with direction cosines proportional to <1, - 2, - 1> and <3, - 2, 3> respectively. The direction ratios of the normal to the plane AOB are
questions.explanation:
Let the direction ratios of normal to plane be a, b, c.
The line in the plane with drs 1, -2, -1 and 3, -2, 3 must be perpendicular to normal 
Then a_1a_2 + b_1b_2 + c_1c_2 = 0
Thus a - 2b - c = 0
3a - 2b + 3c = 0
Solving by cross multiplication a = -8k, b = -6k, c = 4k
Direction of normal will be 4, 3, -2.

questions.question: The projections of a vector on the three coordinate axes are 6, -3 and 2, respectively. The direction cosines of the vector are
questions.question_raw: The projections of a vector on the three coordinate axes are 6, -3 and 2, respectively. The direction cosines of the vector are
questions.explanation:
Projection of vector on coordinate axes are 
x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1, z_2 - z_1
$\Rightarrow$ x_2 - x_1 = 6, y_2 - y_1 = -3, z_2 - z_1 = 2
Now, $\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right)^{2}}$ = $\sqrt{36+9+4}$ = 7
So, the direction cosines of the vector are $\frac{6}{7},-\frac{3}{7}, \frac{2}{7}$

questions.question: If the projections of $\overrightarrow {PQ} $ on OX, OY, OZ are respectively 1, 2, 3 and 4, then the magnitude of $\overrightarrow {PQ} $ is
questions.question_raw: If the projections of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} on OX, OY, OZ are respectively 1, 2, 3 and 4, then the magnitude of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let $\overrightarrow {PQ} = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k$ 
Since $OX = \hat iOY = \hat jOZ = \hat k$ 
Projection of PQ on OX = $\frac{{\overrightarrow {PQ} .\overrightarrow {OX} }}{{|OX|}}$ 
12 = $\frac{x}{1}$ 
x = 12
Similarly
Projection of PQ on OY = $ \frac{{\overrightarrow {PQ} .\overrightarrow {OY} }}{{|OY|}}$ 
3 = $\frac{y}{1}$ 
y = 3
Similarly
Projection of PQ on OZ = $\frac{{\overrightarrow {PQ} .\overrightarrow {OZ} }}{{|OZ|}}$ 
4 = $\frac{z}{1}$ 
z = 4
Hence $\overrightarrow {PQ} = 12\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k$ 
$\left| {PQ} \right| = \sqrt {{{12}^2} + {3^2} + {4^2}} = 13$

questions.question: The centre of sphere passing through four points (0, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0), (1, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 4) is
questions.question_raw: The centre of sphere passing through four points (0, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0), (1, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 4) is

Equation of sphere is given by x^2 + y^2 + z^2 + 2ux + 2vy + 2wz + d = 0
Since sphere passing through four points (0, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0), (1, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 4)
Substituting in the given equation we get d =0
4 + 4v = 0
v = -1
1 + 2u = 0
u = $\frac{-1}{2}$
16 + 8w = 0
w = -2
Centre of sphere (-u, -v, -w) = ($\frac{1}{2}$, 1, 2)

questions.question: The ratio in which the line joining (2, 4, 5) (3, 5, -4) is divided by the YZ – plane is
questions.question_raw: The ratio in which the line joining (2, 4, 5) (3, 5, -4) is divided by the YZ – plane is
questions.explanation:
The ratio in which the line joining (2, 4, 5) (3, 5, - 4) is divided by the YZ – plane is the point in YZ plane will be P(0, y, z) let it divides the line joining points A(2, 4, 5) and B(3, 5, - 4) in the ratio k : 1 
Then X coordinate of point P will be given by $3k+2 \over k+1$ which is equal to zero. 
i.e. $3k+2 \over k+1$= 0 $\Rightarrow$ 3k + 2 = 0 $\Rightarrow$ k = $\frac{-2}{3}$ 
So, YZ plane divides in -2 : 3

questions.question: The area of the triangle whose vertices are (1, 2, 3), (2, 5, -1), (-1, 1, 2) is ________ sq. units.
questions.question_raw: The area of the triangle whose vertices are (1, 2, 3), (2, 5, -1), (-1, 1, 2) is ________ sq. units.
questions.explanation:
Let $\vec a = (1\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k)$, $\vec{b}=(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-1 \hat{k})$, $\vec{c}=(-1 \hat{i}+1 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ 
Area of triangle = $\frac{1}{2}[\vec a*\vec b + \vec b*\vec c + \vec c*\vec a]$ 
= $ \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 2&5&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|} \right] + \left[ {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&5&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&2 \end{array}} \right|} \right] + \left[ {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&2 \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right|} \right]$ 
= $ \frac{1}{2}[ - 17\hat i + 7\hat j + 1\hat k + 11\hat i - 3\hat j + 7\hat k - 1\hat i + 5\hat j - 3\hat k]$ 
= $ \frac{1}{2}[ - 7\hat i + 9\hat j + 5\hat k]$ 
Area = $ \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {49 + 81 + 25} $ 
Area = $\frac{\sqrt{155}}{2}$

questions.question: The direction ratios of a normal to the plane through ( 1 , 0 , 0 ) , ( 0 , 1 , 0 ) , which makes an angle of $\frac \pi 4$ with the plane x + y = 3
questions.question_raw: The direction ratios of a normal to the plane through ( 1 , 0 , 0 ) , ( 0 , 1 , 0 ) , which makes an angle of {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} with the plane x + y = 3
questions.explanation:
Equation of plane through (1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0) be ax + by + cx + d = 0
Let $\overrightarrow n = (a,b,c)$ unit vector normal to the plane
$\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} = 1$
Equation of plane gives a + d = 0 and b + d = 0
solving we get a - b = 0 ....(1).
plane makes angles $\frac \pi 4$ with x + y = 3
$\cos {45^o} = \frac{{a(1) + b(1) + c(1)}}{{1*\sqrt 2 }}$
a + b = 1 ...(2)
solving (1) and (2) we get
a = b = $\frac 12$
Hence $\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} = 1$
$c = \frac {1}{\sqrt 2 }$
drs of normal $(\frac 12, \frac 12, \frac {1}{\sqrt 2 })$
drs of normal $(1,1,\sqrt 2 )$

cos^-1 = $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$

questions.question: The points P (0, 0, 0), Q (2, 0, 0), R (1, $\sqrt 3 $, 0) and S$\left(1, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ lie
questions.question_raw: The points P (0, 0, 0), Q (2, 0, 0), R (1, {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex}, 0) and S{tex}\left(1, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} lie

options.label: A
options.text: in a plane at right angles to Z – axis

questions.explanation:
The equation of plane passing through (0, 0, 0) is a(x - 0) + b(y - 0) + c(z - 0) = 0
ax + by + cz = 0. If it passes through Q (2, 0, 0), R (1, $\sqrt 3 $, 0) then we get
2a = 0; a +$\sqrt 3$ b = 0 i.e. we get a = 0 and b = 0
Now if we put co-ordinates of S it does not satisfy the equation.
Hence points are not coplanar.

questions.question: $\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1+b x)}{x}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1+b x)}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\log (1+a x)}{x}-\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{log (1 + bx)}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0}\frac{\log (1+a x)}{x} \cdot \frac{a}{a}-\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\log (1+b x)}{x} \cdot \frac{b}{b}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ a - b

questions.question: If $f(x)=\left|4 x-x^2-3\right|$ when $x €[0,4]$, then, which of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw: If {tex}f(x)=\left|4 x-x^2-3\right|{/tex} when {tex}x €[0,4]{/tex}, then, which of the following is correct?
questions.explanation:
Clearly, $x=1,3$ are the points of global minimum (as the values being equal).
And, $x=0,4$ are the points of global maximum (as the values being equal).
And, $x=2$, is the point of local maximum (as the values being equal).
Thus, $x=3$ is the global maximum.

questions.question: Let f(x) = x sin$\frac 1 x$, x $ \ne $ 0, then the value of the function at x = 0, so that f is continuous at x = 0, is
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = x sin{tex}\frac 1 x{/tex}, x {tex} \ne {/tex} 0, then the value of the function at x = 0, so that f is continuous at x = 0, is

options.label: A
options.text: $% MathType!Translator!2!1!LaTeX.tdl!LaTeX 2.09 and later! % MathType!MTEF!2!1!+- % feaagGart1ev2aaatCvAUfeBSjuyZL2yd9gzLbvyNv2CaerbuLwBLn % hiov2DGi1BTfMBaeXatLxBI9gBaerbd9wDYLwzYbItLDharqqtubsr % 4rNCHbGeaGqiVu0Je9sqqrpepC0xbbL8F4rqqrFfpeea0xe9Lq-Jc9 % vqaqpepm0xbba9pwe9Q8fs0-yqaqpepae9pg0FirpepeKkFr0xfr-x % fr-xb9adbaqaaeGaciGaaiaabeqaamaabaabaaqcfayaaiabgkziUk % abg6HiLcaa!39D7! \[ \to \infty \]% MathType!End!2!1! $$\sqrt{2}$

questions.explanation:
Here, if we directly put x = 0, f(0) = 0 $\times$ sin ($\frac{1}{0}$) = 0.
At L.H.L, put x = 0 - h$% MathType!Translator!2!1!LaTeX.tdl!LaTeX 2.09 and later! % MathType!MTEF!2!1!+- % feaagGart1ev2aaatCvAUfeBSjuyZL2yd9gzLbvyNv2CaerbuLwBLn % hiov2DGi1BTfMBaeXatLxBI9gBaerbd9wDYLwzYbItLDharqqtubsr % 4rNCHbGeaGqiVu0Je9sqqrpepC0xbbL8F4rqqrFfpeea0xe9Lq-Jc9 % vqaqpepm0xbba9pwe9Q8fs0-yqaqpepae9pg0FirpepeKkFr0xfr-x % fr-xb9adbaqaaeGaciGaaiaabeqaamaabaabaaqcfayaamaaxababa % GaciiBaiaacMgacaGGTbaabaGaamiAaiabgkziUkaaicdaaeqaaaaa % !3D0B! \[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \]% MathType!End!2!1! $, $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} x.\sin \frac1x$ f(0 - h) = 0.
At R.H.L, put x = 0 + h ,$\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} x.\sin \frac1x$$% MathType!Translator!2!1!LaTeX.tdl!LaTeX 2.09 and later! % MathType!MTEF!2!1!+- % feaagGart1ev2aaatCvAUfeBSjuyZL2yd9gzLbvyNv2CaerbuLwBLn % hiov2DGi1BTfMBaeXatLxBI9gBaerbd9wDYLwzYbItLDharqqtubsr % 4rNCHbGeaGqiVu0Je9sqqrpepC0xbbL8F4rqqrFfpeea0xe9Lq-Jc9 % vqaqpepm0xbba9pwe9Q8fs0-yqaqpepae9pg0FirpepeKkFr0xfr-x % fr-xb9adbaqaaeGaciGaaiaabeqaamaabaabaaqcfayaamaaxababa % GaciiBaiaacMgacaGGTbaabaGaamiAaiabgkziUkaaicdaaeqaaaaa % !3D0B! \[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \]% MathType!End!2!1! $, f(0 + h) = 0.
Hence, L.H.L = f(0) = R.H.L.
f(x) is continuous at x = 0.

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \to 0}\frac{\tan x-\sin x}{x^3}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \to 0}\frac{\tan x-\sin x}{x^3}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
The function can be written as 
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin x(1-\cos x)}{\left(x^{3}\right)(\cos x)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim _\limits {x \rightarrow 0} \left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{x^2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\cos x}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \to 0} \frac{x \log (1+x)}{1-\cos x}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \to 0} \frac{x \log (1+x)}{1-\cos x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \log (1+x)}{1-\cos x} \cdot \frac{x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\log (1+x)}{x}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1-\cos x}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ $1 \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow$ 2

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{p x}-e^{q x}}{x}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{p x}-e^{q x}}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{p x}+1-1-e^{q x}}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac {{e}^{px}-1}{x}-\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\frac {{e}^{qx}-1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \left(\frac{e^{p x}-1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{p}{p}\right)-\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{q x}-1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{q}{q}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ p - q

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-x)^n-1}{x}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-x)^n-1}{x}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The given limit is in the form f $\frac 00$ using L'hospital Rude
Putting x = 0 in $\frac{n(1-x)^{n-1}}{1}\cdot (-1)$

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x-x}{x^2 \tan x}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x-x}{x^2 \tan x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
The following limit is in the form of $\frac{0}{0}$ 
Using L'Hospital rule;
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan ^{2} x}{\left(x^{2}\right)+\left(x^{2} \tan ^{2} x\right)+(\tan x)(2 x)}$ = $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \left(\frac{(\tan x)^{2}}{x^{2}}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1+\tan ^{2} x+\left(\frac{2 \tan x}{x}\right)}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $1 \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1+0+2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{3}$

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\left[ {\cos x} \right]$
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\left[ {\cos x} \right]{/tex}
questions.explanation:
L.H.L. = $\underset{x \rightarrow 0}{lim}[\cos (x-h)]$ = 0
R.H.L = $\underset{x \rightarrow 0}{lim}[\cos (x+h)]$ = 0

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\frac{\sin x-x}{x^3}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\frac{\sin x-x}{x^3}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Since the limit is in the form of $\frac{0}{0}$. By applying L'hospital first time we get, $\frac{\cos x-1}{3 x^2}$ 
Again using L'Hospital; $\frac{-\sin x}{6 x}$ 
Again using L'Hospital we get $\frac {-1}{6}$

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x}\right) \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x}\right) \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \;[(\frac{\sin x}{x})-(\frac{x}{x})](\cos \frac1x)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (0) finite number = 0

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x^4}\right) \sin x$ equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x^4}\right) \sin x{/tex} equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x^3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin x-x}{x^3}\right)$ 
Now using L'Hospital we have, $\frac{\cos x-1}{3 x^2}$
Again using L'Hospital, $\frac {-\sin x}{6x}$
Again using L'hospital; $\frac {-1}{6}$

questions.question: Let f(x) = $\frac{2-(256-7 x)^{1 / 8}}{(5 x+32)^{1 / 5}-2}, x \neq 0$, then for f to be continuous at x = 0, f(0) must be equal to
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = {tex}\frac{2-(256-7 x)^{1 / 8}}{(5 x+32)^{1 / 5}-2}, x \neq 0{/tex}, then for f to be continuous at x = 0, f(0) must be equal to

questions.question: If $\begin{array}{c} \frac{\sin [x]}{[x]},[x] \neq 0 \\ 0 \quad,[x]=0 \end{array}$, where [.] denotes the greatest integer function, then $\lim \limits _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\begin{array}{c} \frac{\sin [x]}{[x]},[x] \neq 0 \\ 0 \quad,[x]=0 \end{array}{/tex}, where [.] denotes the greatest integer function, then {tex}\lim \limits _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0^+}\frac{sin\;[x]}{[x]}$ = 1
$\Rightarrow \mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0^-}\frac{sin\;[x]}{[x]}$ = sin 1
$\Rightarrow$ L.H.L.$\neq$ R.H.L.
hence limit does not exist.

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty } x\left( {{a^{1/x}} - 1} \right)$, where a > 0, is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty } x\left( {{a^{1/x}} - 1} \right){/tex}, where a > 0, is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let x = $\frac{l}{t}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{t \rightarrow 0}{lim} \frac{a^{t}-1}{t}$ = log_e a

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Mathematics > Limits and Derivatives

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to \infty } \frac{\sum \limits_{r=1}^n r}{n^2}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to \infty } \frac{\sum \limits_{r=1}^n r}{n^2}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{n^{2}}$ = $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\frac{(n+1)}{2 n}$ 
Substitute n = $\frac{l}{t}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{t \rightarrow 0}\frac{(1+t)}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{\sum \limits_{r=1}^n r^2}{n^3}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{\sum \limits_{r=1}^n r^2}{n^3}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}}{n^{3}}$ = $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\frac{\left(2 n^{2}+3 n+1\right)}{6 n^{2}}$
Substitute n = $\frac{l}{t}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{t \rightarrow 0}\frac{\left(2+3 t+t^{2}\right)}{6}=\frac{1}{3}$

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac 1 x$
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac 1 x{/tex}
questions.explanation:
When x $\rightarrow$ 0^-; f(x) $\rightarrow$ $-\infty$
When x $\rightarrow$ 0^+; f(x) $\rightarrow$ $\infty$
L.H.L $\ne$ R.H.L.
Hence limit does not exist.

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;x\left[ x \right]$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;x\left[ x \right]{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\underset{x \rightarrow 0^{-}}{lim}(x-h)[x-h]$ = $\underset{x \rightarrow 0}{lim}(x-h)(x-h-1)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (-h)(-h - 1) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}{lim}(x+h)[x+h]$ = $\underset{x \rightarrow 0}{lim}(x+h)(x)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (h)(0) = 0

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{(1+x)^n-1}{x}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{(1+x)^n-1}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: $\lim \limits_{x \to 0} \frac{(1+x)^n-1^n}{(1+x)-(1)}=\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0}n(1+x)^{n-1}=n$

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{\frac{1}{1-n^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-n^{2}}+\ldots+\frac{n}{1-n^{2}}\right\}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{\frac{1}{1-n^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-n^{2}}+\ldots+\frac{n}{1-n^{2}}\right\}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1}{1-n^{2}}+\frac{2}{1-n^{2}}+ \ldots+\frac{n}{1-n^{2}}\right]$
= $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{1}{1-n^{2}}\right)$ [1 + 2 + 3 ... n]
= $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{1}{1-n^{2}}\right)\left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right]$
= $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{n(n+1)}{(1-n)(1+n)}\right]$
= $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{2} \frac{n}{1-n}$
= $\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{\frac{1}{n}-1}$
= $\frac{1}{2}$(-1)
= $\frac{-1}{2}$

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^2(x+h)-\sin ^2 x}{h}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^2(x+h)-\sin ^2 x}{h}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[\sin (x+h)-\sin x][\sin (x+h)+\sin x]}{h}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{2 x+h}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{h}{2}\right) 2 \cos \left(\frac{2 x+h}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)}{h}$ 
= sin 2x

questions.question: If a is a real number, then $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a} \frac{|x-a|}{x-a}$
questions.question_raw: If a is a real number, then {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a} \frac{|x-a|}{x-a}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
R.H.L = $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a^+}\frac{x-a}{x-a}$ = 1
L.H.L = $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a^-}\frac{a-x}{x-a}$ = -1
L.H.L $\ne$ R.H.L. 
Hence limit does not exists.

questions.question: If k be an integer, then $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to {k^ + }}\;(x - \left[ x \right])$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If k be an integer, then {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to {k^ + }}\;(x - \left[ x \right]){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\underset{x \rightarrow k^{+}}{lim}(x-[x])$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{h \rightarrow o}{lim}(k+h-[k+h])$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{h \rightarrow o}{lim}(k+h-k)$ 
= 0

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to {a^ - } }\frac{|x-a|}{x-a}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to {a^ - } }\frac{|x-a|}{x-a}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim\limits_{x \to {a^ - } }\frac{|x-a|}{x-a}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\frac{|(a-h)-a|}{(a-h)-a}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{-h}$ = -1

questions.question: If k be an integer, then $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to {k^ - }^{}} \;\;(x - \left[ x \right])$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If k be an integer, then {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to {k^ - }^{}} \;\;(x - \left[ x \right]){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\underset{x \rightarrow k^{-}}{lim}(x-[x])$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{h \rightarrow 0}{lim}(k-h-[k-h])$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\underset{h \rightarrow 0}{\operatorname{lim}}(k-h-k+1)$ 
= 1

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\left(\frac{\tan x-x}{x}\right) \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\left(\frac{\tan x-x}{x}\right) \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} is equal to

options.label: B
options.text: a real number other than 0 and 1

questions.explanation:
$\lim\limits_{x \to 0}\left(\frac{\tan x-x}{x}\right) \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (0) Finite number = 0

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 3}\frac{\sqrt{x^2+10}-\sqrt{19}}{x-3}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 3}\frac{\sqrt{x^2+10}-\sqrt{19}}{x-3}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Using L'Hospital,
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{\frac{2 x}{\sqrt[2]{x^{2}+10}}}{1}$ 
Substituting x = 3 in $\frac{\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+10}}}{1}$ 
We get $\frac{3}{\sqrt{19}}$

questions.question: Maximum value of x^3 - 3x + 2 in [0, 2] is
questions.question_raw: Maximum value of x3 - 3x + 2 in [0, 2] is
questions.explanation:
Polynomial functions are continuous and derivable into their entire domain f'(x) > 0. for |x| > 0.
So it will be an increasing function in [0, 2]
$\Rightarrow$ f(0) = 2 and f(2) = 4
So, f(2) will be maximum.

questions.question: $\lim\limits_{x \to 0 + } \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{16+\sqrt{x}}-4}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim\limits_{x \to 0 + } \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{16+\sqrt{x}}-4}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}\frac{1}{\frac{\sqrt{16+\sqrt{z}}-\sqrt{16}}{(16+\sqrt{z})-(16)}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}{lim} 2(\sqrt{16+\sqrt{x}})$ 
= 8

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Mathematics > Limits and Derivatives

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec x-\sqrt{2}}{x-\frac{\pi}{4}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec x-\sqrt{2}}{x-\frac{\pi}{4}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Using L' Hospital rule
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec x-\sqrt{2}}{x-\frac{\pi}{4}}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{2}$

questions.question: What is the value of $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \tan x}{\cot x}$ ?
questions.question_raw: What is the value of {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \tan x}{\cot x}{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \tan x}{\cot x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}}$
$ =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} {x} $
$ =0 $

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty }\;\left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + x + 1} - x} \right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty }\;\left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + x + 1} - x} \right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Substitute x = $\frac{1}{t}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\lim _\limits{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{t^{2}+t+1}-1}{t}$ 
Using L' Hospital
$\lim _\limits{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{2 t+1}{2 \sqrt{t^{2}+t+1}}}{1}$ 
= $\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;\;\left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;\;\left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Put x = $\frac1t$ 
Then, $\mathop {lim}\limits_{t \to 0 }\;\frac{\left( {\sqrt {1+{t^2}} - 1} \right)}{t}$ 
Applying L'Hospital 
$\mathop {lim}\limits_{t \to 0 }\frac{\frac{2t}{2\sqrt{1+t^2}}}{1}$ = 0

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan x-1}{x-\frac{\pi}{4}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan x-1}{x-\frac{\pi}{4}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let $x-\frac{\pi}{4}$ = t
$\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+t\right)-1}{t}$ 
$\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \tan t}{(1-\tan t)(t)}$ 
= 2

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin \sqrt{x+h-\sin \sqrt{x}}}{h}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin \sqrt{x+h-\sin \sqrt{x}}}{h}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos \left(\frac{\sqrt{x+h}+\sqrt{x}}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\sqrt{x+h}-\sqrt{x}}{2}\right)}{h}$
using L'Hospital
$\Rightarrow \frac{\cos \sqrt{x}}{2 \sqrt{x}}$

questions.question: $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 1} \;\left( {\cos \left[ x \right]} \right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 1} \;\left( {\cos \left[ x \right]} \right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
R.H.L. = $\underset{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}{lim}(\cos [x])$ = cos 1 = 1
L.H.L = $\underset{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}{lim}(\cos [x])$ = cos 0 = 0
Since L.H.L $\ne$ R.H.L therefore limit does not exist.

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan x}{\log (1+x)}$ is equal to

$\Rightarrow$ 1$\cdot$1 = 1

Using L'Hospital
$\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 9} \frac{\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{\sqrt{f(x)}}}{\frac{1}{2^{\sqrt{x}}}}$
$\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 9} \frac{\sqrt{x} \cdot f^{\prime}(x)}{\sqrt{f(x)}}$
Substituting the given values; we get 3

questions.question: If x = f(t) and y = g (t), then $\frac {d^2y} {dx^2}$ is qual to

1 = $f^{\prime}(t) \frac{d t}{d x}$ and $\frac{d y}{d x}=g^{\prime}(t) \frac{d t}{d x}$

questions.question: The two lines of regression are 2x - 7y + 6 = 0 and 7x – 2y +1 = 0. What is correlation coefficient between x and y?

questions.question: The mean of 5 observations is 4.4. If three observations are 1, 2 and 6 and the difference of the other two is 5, then the other two observations are:
questions.question_raw: The mean of 5 observations is 4.4. If three observations are 1, 2 and 6 and the difference of the other two is 5, then the other two observations are:
questions.explanation:
Let other two numbers be x and y.
Mean (x) = 4.4 = $\frac{(1+2+6+x+y)}{5}$ 
This gives (x + y) = 13 ....(i) 
Also, (x - y) = 5 .....(ii) 
Therefore from (i) and (ii)
x = 9 and y = 4

questions.question: If x and y are related as y – 4x = 3, then the nature of correlation between x and y is

Comparing the given equation with ax + by + c = 0
a = -4, b = 1 and c = -3
So ab < 0
Hence, $\rho = \frac{{\frac{{ - b}}{a}}}{{\left| {\frac{b}{a}} \right|}} = \frac{{\frac{{ - b}}{a}}}{{\frac{{ - b}}{a}}} = 1$ 
So a perfect positive correlation.

questions.question: The mean weight of a group of 10 items is 28 and that of another group of n items is 35. The mean of a combined group of 10 + n items is found to be 30. The value of n is
questions.question_raw: The mean weight of a group of 10 items is 28 and that of another group of n items is 35. The mean of a combined group of 10 + n items is found to be 30. The value of n is
questions.explanation:
Sum of weights of 10 items = 280
Sum of weights of n items = 35n
So, sum of weights of (10 + n) items = 280 + 35n
So, mean = $\frac{280 + 35n}{10 + n}$
30(10 + n) = 280 + 35n 
On solving we get, n = 4

Here, 
r = 0.28, Cov(x, y) = 7.6, $\sigma_x$ = 3
So, r = $\frac{\operatorname{Cov}(x, y)}{\sigma_{x} \sigma_{y}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 0.28 = $\frac{7.6}{3 \sigma_{y}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sigma_y$ = 9.05

questions.question: If the mean of the squares of first n natural numbers be 11, then n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the mean of the squares of first n natural numbers be 11, then n is equal to
questions.explanation:
Mean = $\frac{{\frac{{n(n + 1)(2n + 1)}}{6}}}{n} = \frac{{(n + 1)(2n + 1)}}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 11 = $\frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 66 = (n + 1)(2n + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ 2n^2 + 3n - 65 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 2n^2 + 13n - 10n - 65 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (2n + 13)(n - 5) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ n = 5, $\frac{-13}{2}$ 
So, n = 5

$\Rightarrow$ n^2 = X_1 + X_2 + .... + X_n 
Here, n can be any natural number.

questions.question: Mean deviation from the mean for the observations – 1, 0, 4 is
questions.question_raw: Mean deviation from the mean for the observations – 1, 0, 4 is
questions.explanation:
Given observations are -1,0,4
Mean $\bar x$ = $\frac{-1+0+4}{3}$ = 1
Now, deviations of x_i from mean are |-1-1|, |0-1|, |4-1| i.e. 2, 1, 3
Sum of deviations $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\left|\mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{1}}-\overline{\mathbf{x}}\right|$ = 2 + 1 + 3 = 6
Mean deviation $\frac{1}{n} \Sigma\left|x_{1}-\bar{x}\right|$ = $\frac{1}{3} \times 6$ = 2

b_xy, = $\frac{-1}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\rho = \pm \sqrt {({b_{yx}} {b_{xy}})}$

Hence, $\rho = - \frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: The mean of 50 observations is 36, if two observations are 30 and 42 are deleted, then the mean of the remaining observations is
questions.question_raw: The mean of 50 observations is 36, if two observations are 30 and 42 are deleted, then the mean of the remaining observations is
questions.explanation:
Mean = $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{i}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 36 = $\frac{1}{50} \sum_{i=1}^{50} X_{i}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\sum_{i=1}^{50} X_{i}$ = 36 $\times$ 50 = 1800
After deleting the observations 30 and 42.
New sum = 1800 - 30 - 42 = 1728
New mean $\frac{1728}{48} $ = 36

questions.question: The S.D. of the first n natural numbers is
questions.question_raw: The S.D. of the first n natural numbers is
questions.explanation:
First n natural numbers are 1, 2, 3.....n
We know that, $\sum x = \frac{{n(n + 1)}}{2}$ 
Sum of square is $\sum {{x^2}} = \sum {({1^2} + {2^2} + {3^2} + ......... + {n^2})} $ 
$\sum x^{2}=\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}$ 
SD = $\sqrt{\frac{\sum x^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum x}{n}\right)^{2}}$ 
= $\sqrt{\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6 n}-\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2 n}\right)^{2}}$ 
= $\sqrt{\frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}-\left(\frac{(n+1)}{2}\right)^{2}}$ 
= $\sqrt{(n+1)\left[\frac{2 n+1}{6}-\frac{(n+1)}{4}\right]}$ 
= $\sqrt{\frac{(n+1)(n-1)}{12}}$ 
= $\sqrt{\frac{n^{2}-1}{12}}$

questions.question: In a group of students, mean weight of boys is 80 kg and mean weight of girls is 50kg. If the mean weight of all the students taken together is 60kg, then the ratio of the number of boys to that of the girls is
questions.question_raw: In a group of students, mean weight of boys is 80 kg and mean weight of girls is 50kg. If the mean weight of all the students taken together is 60kg, then the ratio of the number of boys to that of the girls is
questions.explanation:
Let the no. of boys be x and no. of girls be y
Sum of weights of boys = 80x
Sum of weights of girls = 50y
Sum of weights of boys and girls together = 60(x + y)
Hence, 80x + 50 y = 60x + 60y
Which gives, 20x = 10 y
So, x : y = 1 : 2

questions.question: If the mean of 3, 4, x, 7, 10 is 6, then the value of x is
questions.question_raw: If the mean of 3, 4, x, 7, 10 is 6, then the value of x is
questions.explanation:
Mean = $\frac{3+4+x+7+10}{5}$ = $\frac{24+x}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 6 = $\frac{24+x}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 30 = 24 + x
$\Rightarrow$ 30 - 24 = x
$\Rightarrow$ x = 6

questions.question: If r is the correlation coefficient, then
questions.question_raw: If r is the correlation coefficient, then

By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
${\langle x,y\rangle ^2} \le \langle x,x\rangle \cdot\langle y,y\rangle $ 
Which gives, $\left(\sum x_{i} y_{i}\right)^{2} \leq\left(\sum\left(x_{i}^{2}\right)\right) \cdot\left(\sum\left(y_{i}^{2}\right)\right)$ .........(1) 
$\left(\sum x_{i} y_{i}\right) \leq \sqrt{\left(\sum\left(x_{i}^{2}\right)\right)} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\sum\left(y_{i}^{2}\right)\right)}$ 
We, know that, r = $ \frac{{\sum {\Delta X\Delta Y} }}{{\sqrt {\sum {\Delta {X^2}} } \sqrt {\sum {\Delta {Y^2}} } }}$ 
Using (1) it is clear that, $ - 1 \le r \le 1$
